| *Homotopy Type Theory* book (free) | HoTT canon | Current canon, depth, heterodox |
| Nielsen, Chuang, *Quantum Computation and Quantum Information* (10th anniv.) | Quantum computing canon | Permanent canon, depth, spine |
| Kitaev, Shen, Vyalyi, *Classical and Quantum Computation* | Quantum alternative | Current canon, depth |
| Pure-mathematician-only algebra texts | Often broader than CS needs | Skip (in favor of selective engagement) |
| Cookbook category theory without mathematics | Limited deep understanding | Skip (in favor of mathematically serious treatments) |
| Elementary undergraduate algebra texts | Too elementary for serious work | Skip (in favor of graduate texts) |
| Quantum computing without linear algebra | Limited preparation | Skip (in favor of mathematically grounded treatments) |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

The honest framing of this section's content as specialized rather than foundational matters because the casual presumption among some CS practitioners is that any serious mathematical preparation must include abstract algebra and category theory. This presumption produces investment that is often poorly calibrated. A machine learning practitioner spending time on category theory rather than on additional probability or optimization is typically misallocating effort. A web development practitioner engaging seriously with abstract algebra without specific application to cryptography is typically misallocating effort. Calibration matters.

For CS practitioners whose work does depend on this material, the investment is demanding and pays dividends. Cryptography practitioners working without algebraic preparation produce work that is either limited in scope or unreliable in security. Programming language theorists working without category-theoretic preparation are excluded from parts of contemporary literature. Functional programmers working without categorical thinking produce code that is idiomatic in surface but lacks the deeper organization that categorical thinking enables. The investment for these practitioners is foundational rather than supplementary.

Category theory in particular has acquired increased CS visibility through the functional programming community and through programming language research. Various recent developments — homotopy type theory, applied category theory (the work by Fong, Spivak, and others on category theory applications to systems and processes), various categorical approaches to deep learning emerging in research — represent ongoing areas where category-theoretic content is producing genuinely new computer science work. Practitioners engaging with these areas should expect category-theoretic preparation to be valuable.

For practitioners pursuing cryptography specifically, the rise of post-quantum cryptography has shifted the algebraic content cryptographers need. Classical cryptography (RSA, elliptic curves) used number theory and elementary group theory; post-quantum cryptography uses lattice theory, module theory over polynomial rings, and various algebraic structures that lattice-based and code-based cryptosystems use. Cryptography practitioners pursuing post-quantum work benefit from algebraic preparation extending beyond what classical cryptography required.

For practitioners pursuing quantum computing, recent advances in quantum hardware and quantum algorithms have elevated practical relevance of quantum mathematical preparation. The mathematical foundations remain the same, but the practitioner population for whom this preparation is relevant has expanded as quantum computing has matured.

A final practical observation: among the mathematical subjects in this chapter, abstract algebra and category theory have the most variable utility across CS subjects. Practitioners should take seriously the question of whether their CS work actually depends on this material before investing in it. The investment is large; the payoff varies dramatically by what work the practitioner pursues. Honest assessment of need produces better allocation of mathematical preparation time than uniform presumption that all mathematical subjects deserve equal investment.

### 7.8 — Mathematical Methods for AI

#### What it is, properly

Mathematical methods for AI is not a mathematical subject in its own right — it is a collection of mathematical content drawn from various mathematical subjects that contemporary AI work depends on. The reason for treating these methods as coherent topic is pragmatic: CS practitioners pursuing machine learning, deep learning, foundation models, reinforcement learning, AI safety, mechanistic interpretability, various other contemporary AI subjects require mathematical preparation drawn from multiple mathematical subjects, and understanding of which mathematical content is most required is valuable preparation in itself. The section addresses this pragmatic concern by integrating mathematical content most relevant to contemporary AI work.

The relationship between AI and mathematics has a particular character. Contemporary AI is an empirical discipline — some of what is known comes from empirical investigation rather than mathematical derivation. But empirical AI work depends on mathematical content for several purposes: it provides conceptual framework for understanding what AI systems are doing, computational machinery that AI systems use, and theoretical foundations that AI work draws on. The mathematical preparation for AI is practical without being merely instrumental: AI practitioners benefit from mathematical fluency that supports their work without necessarily pursuing mathematical research depth.

The conceptual core of mathematical preparation for contemporary AI distinguishes several aspects, drawing across mathematical subjects covered earlier in this chapter and adding integration that AI-specific work requires.

*Linear algebra at depth* (Section 7.1) is foundational and load-bearing. AI work involves tensor operations, matrix decompositions, eigenanalysis, various linear algebraic operations applied throughout. AI practitioners benefit from linear algebra preparation extending beyond introductory treatment — fluency with matrix calculus (gradients, Jacobians, Hessians of vector and matrix-valued functions), fluency with various decompositions and their uses, computational fluency with linear algebraic operations at scale.

*Multivariable calculus and gradient-based reasoning* (Section 7.2) is foundational. deep learning is gradient-based optimization, with chain rule applied throughout backpropagation. AI practitioners benefit from multivariable calculus preparation with particular emphasis on gradients, chain rule, automatic differentiation, Taylor expansions, various computational aspects of multivariable calculus that AI work uses.

*Probability theory at depth* (Section 7.3) is foundational. machine learning is probabilistic in foundation, Bayesian methods are probabilistic, information-theoretic content is probabilistic. AI practitioners benefit from probability preparation with particular emphasis on conditional probability, standard distributions and their properties, expectation and variance, limit theorems, Bayesian reasoning, Monte Carlo methods, information theory.

*Statistics at appropriate depth* (Section 7.3) is valuable. empirical AI work depends on statistical methodology — appropriate experimental design, appropriate baselines, appropriate statistical analysis, appropriate handling of variance, appropriate avoidance of various statistical pitfalls. AI practitioners benefit from statistical preparation with particular emphasis on empirical methodology, bootstrap and various resampling methods, confidence intervals and appropriate uncertainty representation.

*Optimization at depth* (Section 7.6) is foundational. deep learning is optimization, with gradient descent and various optimizers central. AI practitioners benefit from optimization preparation extending beyond casual treatment — appreciation for convexity (when problems are convex versus non-convex), fluency with gradient methods and their variants, appreciation for various failure modes of optimization, appreciation for trade-offs in various optimization choices.

*Information theory* (covered within probability material in Section 7.3) is valuable. entropy, mutual information, KL divergence, cross-entropy, various information-theoretic quantities are central to machine learning work. AI practitioners benefit from information theory preparation with particular emphasis on connections to machine learning loss functions, uses in various ML methods.

*Statistical learning theory* provides theoretical foundation for machine learning. PAC learning, VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, generalization bounds, various theoretical content provide conceptual framework for understanding why and when machine learning works. Shalev-Shwartz and Ben-David's *Understanding Machine Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.1, free) provides accessible introduction to statistical learning theory.

*High-dimensional probability* is increasingly important for contemporary AI work. Concentration inequalities, various high-dimensional phenomena, and random matrix theory provide mathematical machinery relevant to high-dimensional learning. Vershynin's *High-Dimensional Probability* (mentioned in Section 7.3, free) is a useful reference.

*Stochastic processes and Markov chains* are important for reinforcement learning, Bayesian computation through MCMC, and various other applications. Introductory treatment in probability courses is adequate for many AI practitioners; deeper engagement is valuable for RL specialists and others.

*Differential equations* are relevant for neural ODEs, diffusion models, and other contemporary AI methods. Introductory ODE treatment is adequate for many AI practitioners; deeper engagement is valuable for specialists.

*Manifold theory and differential geometry* are relevant for various advanced topics — information geometry for understanding optimization geometry, various geometric perspectives on neural networks, and other advanced applications. Preparation is specialized rather than required for many AI practitioners.

*Category theory* is moderately relevant for functional programming approaches to AI and various theoretical perspectives. Preparation is specialized rather than required for many AI practitioners.

#### What CS subjects depend on this preparation

The mathematical preparation described here is required across parts of contemporary AI work:

*Machine learning foundations* (Section 5.1) depends on linear algebra, multivariable calculus, probability and statistics, optimization, information theory, and statistical learning theory.

*Deep learning* (Section 5.2) depends on the same foundations with additional emphasis on tensor operations, automatic differentiation, and computational aspects.

*Foundation models* (Section 5.3) depends on these foundations with additional emphasis on high-dimensional phenomena, scaling considerations, and computational aspects at scale.

*Reinforcement learning* (Section 5.4) depends on these foundations with additional emphasis on stochastic processes, Markov chains, and decision theory.

*Mechanistic interpretability* (Section 5.6) depends on linear algebra at depth with additional emphasis on geometric and spectral analysis.

*AI safety* (Section 5.7) depends on these foundations with additional emphasis on probability theory, decision theory, various theoretical content.

#### What preparation provides AI practitioners

Mathematical preparation for AI provides AI practitioners with several capacities.

1. **Conceptual framework for understanding what AI systems do.** Mathematical preparation makes neural network forward passes, training procedures, and learned representations intelligible as mathematical objects rather than opaque implementation details.

2. **Computational fluency with AI mathematics.** Matrix operations, gradients, probability distributions, and related objects have to be computable, not merely recognizable. Serious AI work depends on that fluency.

3. **Empirical methodology.** Statistical methodology shapes experimental design, baseline selection, and analysis. It is what separates reliable empirical AI work from performance anecdotes.

4. **Fluency with optimization.** Gradient methods, optimizers, and optimization failure modes are central to deep learning work. AI practitioners need to reason about training as optimization, not only as code execution.

5. **Uncertainty.** Probabilistic and Bayesian thinking help practitioners reason about uncertainty in models, data, evaluations, and deployment settings. This produces more reliable work than overconfident point estimates.

6. **Ability to read and contribute to mathematical AI literature.** Frontier AI papers often present methods through mathematical formulations, prove theoretical results, or analyze empirical phenomena mathematically. Reading that literature requires mathematical fluency.

7. **Transfer across AI subjects.** Linear algebra supports deep learning, mechanistic interpretability, and other areas; probability supports machine learning, RL, and Bayesian methods; optimization supports training for major AI methods. Mathematical preparation is among the most transferable preparation for AI work.

#### How study should proceed

Mathematical study for AI should be calibrated to AI subjects of interest and level of engagement intended. A broad AI practitioner working primarily with standard methods through libraries benefits from moderate mathematical preparation. An AI researcher working at the frontier benefits from mathematical preparation extending beyond the standard ML curriculum.

A reasonable progression for AI-focused mathematical preparation starts with foundational coverage of linear algebra (using Strang or Axler from Section 7.1), multivariable calculus (using standard texts from Section 7.2), probability and statistics (using Blitzstein-Hwang and Wasserman from Section 7.3), optimization (using Boyd-Vandenberghe from Section 7.6).

Intermediate progression adds information theory (using MacKay or Cover-Thomas from Section 7.3), statistical learning theory (using Shalev-Shwartz-Ben-David from Section 5.1, free), and Bayesian methods (using Gelman et al. or McElreath from Section 7.3).

Advanced progression for frontier work adds high-dimensional probability (Vershynin), information geometry, manifold theory, category theory if functional programming approaches are pursued, and other specialized content as specific AI subjects require.

Integrated treatments specifically for AI include Deisenroth, Faisal, and Ong's *Mathematics for Machine Learning* (2020, freely available online), which covers linear algebra, calculus, probability, and related mathematical content with machine learning focus. The book is unusually well-tuned to AI practitioner needs and is recommended as a primary entry.

Bishop's *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.1) and Murphy's *Probabilistic Machine Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.1) are machine learning textbooks with mathematical depth. Engagement with these texts develops mathematical fluency through machine learning context.

Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville's *Deep Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.2) Chapters 2-4 cover linear algebra, probability, and numerical computation specifically for deep learning preparation.

Computational practice with mathematical content in AI contexts is essential. Implementing neural network components from scratch (without high-level libraries), implementing various ML algorithms, and working through derivations all develop mathematical intuition that library use alone does not produce.

#### Reference material

##### Canonical entry points

The canonical entry point integrating mathematical preparation for AI is Deisenroth, Faisal, and Ong's *Mathematics for Machine Learning* (2020, freely available online). The book covers linear algebra, calculus, probability, and optimization with machine learning focus and integration. It is recommended as the primary reference.

Bishop's *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.1) develops mathematics through machine learning content. The book functions both as an ML textbook and as a mathematical reference.

Murphy's *Probabilistic Machine Learning: An Introduction* and *Probabilistic Machine Learning: Advanced Topics* (mentioned in Section 5.1) provide more contemporary alternative with similar approach.

For deep learning specifically, Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville's *Deep Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.2) Chapters 2-4 cover mathematical foundations specifically for deep learning. Bishop and Bishop's *Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts* (2024, mentioned in Section 5.2) provides contemporary updated alternative.

For statistical learning theory specifically, Shalev-Shwartz and Ben-David's *Understanding Machine Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.1, free) provides accessible introduction. Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman's *The Elements of Statistical Learning* (second edition, mentioned in Section 5.1, free) provides broader statistical perspective on machine learning.

##### The progression

After foundational treatment, progression branches according to AI subjects of interest.

For machine learning theory at depth: Vapnik's *The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory* (mentioned in Section 7.3), Mohri, Rostamizadeh, and Talwalkar's *Foundations of Machine Learning* (second edition, 2018), various papers in machine learning theory.

For optimization in machine learning at depth: Bottou, Curtis, and Nocedal "Optimization Methods for Large-Scale Machine Learning" survey (free), Wright and Recht's *Optimization for Data Analysis* (2022), Beck's *First-Order Methods in Optimization* (2017).

For high-dimensional probability and concentration of measure at depth: Vershynin's *High-Dimensional Probability* (mentioned in Section 7.3, free), Boucheron, Lugosi, and Massart's *Concentration Inequalities* (2013).

For information geometry at depth: Amari's *Information Geometry and Its Applications* (2016) covers information geometry. Ay, Jost, Lê, and Schwachhöfer's *Information Geometry* (2017) provides alternative.

For Bayesian methods at depth: Gelman et al.'s *Bayesian Data Analysis* (mentioned in Section 7.3), Murphy's *Probabilistic Machine Learning* with Bayesian focus, various papers on variational inference and various Bayesian computational methods.

For deep learning theory at depth: Roberts, Yaida, and Hanin's *The Principles of Deep Learning Theory* (2022, free, mentioned in Section 5.2), various papers on neural tangent kernels, implicit bias of gradient descent, various theoretical work.

For information theory and machine learning at depth: MacKay's *Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms* (2003, free, mentioned in Section 7.3) provides unified treatment. Cover and Thomas's *Elements of Information Theory* (mentioned in Section 7.3) provides pure information theory reference.

For reinforcement learning mathematics at depth: Sutton and Barto's *Reinforcement Learning* (mentioned in Section 5.4) covers RL mathematical foundations. Agarwal, Jiang, Kakade, and Sun's *Reinforcement Learning: Theory and Algorithms* (mentioned in Section 5.4, free) provides theoretical depth.

For mechanistic interpretability mathematics: linear algebra at depth (Section 7.1) is foundational, with additional content from Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread (mentioned in Section 5.6) covering mathematical framework specific to interpretability.

For AI safety mathematics: probability and decision theory at depth, with additional content from AI safety literature (Section 5.7).

##### Reference works

Deisenroth-Faisal-Ong serves as reference for integrated AI mathematical preparation.

Bishop and Murphy serve as reference combining ML and mathematics.

Goodfellow-Bengio-Courville Chapters 2-4 serve as reference for deep learning mathematical foundations.

Shalev-Shwartz-Ben-David and Hastie-Tibshirani-Friedman serve as reference for statistical learning.

Vershynin serves as reference for high-dimensional probability.

Various canonical references mentioned in earlier sections (Sections 7.1, 7.2, 7.3, 7.6) serve as references for specific mathematical subjects.

##### What to skip and why

Books on AI mathematics that abstract over mathematical content in favor of cookbook approaches produce practitioners who cannot reason about what AI methods are doing.

Most general mathematics-for-engineers books may be appropriate for broader engineering audiences but are less directly relevant to AI practitioners than AI-oriented mathematical references.

Books promising AI mastery without mathematical preparation should be approached with skepticism. AI work requires mathematical preparation, and treatments that abstract over the mathematics produce limited preparation.

Books on AI mathematics published before recent AI developments may cover foundational content adequately but may miss mathematical content relevant to contemporary methods.

|---|---|---|
| Deisenroth, Faisal, Ong, *Mathematics for Machine Learning* (free) | Integrated AI math entry | Current canon, entry, spine |
| Bishop, *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* (mentioned 5.1) | ML with mathematical depth | Permanent canon, depth, spine |
| Murphy, *Probabilistic Machine Learning* (mentioned 5.1) | Contemporary probabilistic ML | Current canon, depth, spine |
| Bishop, Bishop, *Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts* (mentioned 5.2) | Contemporary DL mathematical | Current canon, depth |
| Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville, *Deep Learning* Chapters 2-4 (mentioned 5.2) | DL math foundations | Permanent canon, entry |
| Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman, *The Elements of Statistical Learning* (free, mentioned 5.1) | Statistical ML reference | Permanent canon, depth, spine |
| Shalev-Shwartz, Ben-David, *Understanding Machine Learning* (free, mentioned 5.1) | Learning theory accessible | Current canon, depth |
| Vapnik, *The Nature of Statistical Learning Theory* (mentioned 7.3) | Foundational learning theory | Permanent canon, depth |
| Mohri, Rostamizadeh, Talwalkar, *Foundations of Machine Learning* (2nd ed.) | Learning theory depth | Current canon, depth |
| Bottou, Curtis, Nocedal optimization for ML survey (free) | ML optimization | Current canon, primary source |
| Wright, Recht, *Optimization for Data Analysis* | Contemporary ML optimization | Current canon, depth |
| Roberts, Yaida, Hanin, *Principles of Deep Learning Theory* (free, mentioned 5.2) | DL theory | Current canon, depth |
| Vershynin, *High-Dimensional Probability* (free, mentioned 7.3) | High-dim probability | Current canon, depth |
| Boucheron, Lugosi, Massart, *Concentration Inequalities* | Concentration depth | Current canon, depth |
| Amari, *Information Geometry and Its Applications* | Information geometry | Current canon, depth |
| MacKay, *Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms* (free, mentioned 7.3) | Unified information theory and ML | Permanent canon, depth |
| Sutton, Barto, *Reinforcement Learning* (mentioned 5.4) | RL mathematical foundations | Permanent canon, depth, spine |
| Agarwal, Jiang, Kakade, Sun, *RL: Theory and Algorithms* (free, mentioned 5.4) | RL theory | Current canon, depth |
| Gelman et al., *Bayesian Data Analysis* (mentioned 7.3) | Bayesian methods | Permanent canon, depth |
| Anthropic Transformer Circuits Thread (free, mentioned 5.6) | Interpretability math | Current canon, primary source |
| AI safety mathematical literature (mentioned 5.7) | AI safety math | Current, sampling |
| Cookbook AI math approaches | Limited deep understanding | Skip (in favor of serious treatments) |
| General math-for-engineers books | Less AI-specific | Skip (in favor of AI-oriented references) |
| AI books promising mastery without math | Limited preparation | Skip (in favor of mathematically grounded treatments) |
| Pre-deep-learning AI math books | Miss recent content | Skip alone (supplement with current material) |

Mathematical preparation for contemporary AI is transferable. CS practitioners pursuing AI work seriously benefit from preparation extending beyond the casual treatment that introductory ML resources typically provide. The investment pays dividends across AI work and distinguishes deep understanding from surface familiarity.

Mathematical preparation for AI should be calibrated to intended level of engagement. Practitioners working primarily with standard methods through high-level libraries benefit from moderate preparation. Researchers and practitioners working at the frontier benefit from deeper preparation that moderate preparation does not provide.

Mathematical preparation for AI benefits from integration of mathematical study with AI practice rather than pure mathematical study followed by AI work. Implementing neural network components from scratch, deriving optimization procedures from foundations, and working through derivations as part of AI practice develops mathematical intuition more efficiently than detached study alone.

Mathematical preparation for AI has increasing rather than decreasing relevance as AI capabilities advance. A casual perspective sometimes assumes that AI advances will reduce mathematical demands on practitioners because libraries and pre-trained models abstract over mathematical content. Empirical experience suggests the opposite: AI advances tend to elevate mathematical demands on practitioners working at the frontier, with mathematical fluency increasingly distinguishing deep practitioners from users of pre-built tools.

Mathematical preparation for AI overlaps with preparation for scientific computing, graphics, robotics, and other CS subjects, but with different emphasis. Practitioners pursuing AI alongside these subjects benefit from mathematical preparation that supports multiple directions while still respecting their different mathematical demands.

The Computer Science Map —— *An intellectual architecture for long-term learning, not an actionable curriculum.*

## Chapter 1 — The Shape of the Field

*This map has three purposes: orientation — where a subject sits in CS, what it is for, and what it depends on; preparation — what background knowledge makes it learnable rather than opaque; and resource judgment — which books, courses, papers, and other sources are worth using, and in what role. It is not a curriculum or a completion list. Resource tags used throughout: entry for first contact; spine for a main text or course; depth for serious extension; reference for later consultation; heterodox for useful disagreement; bridge for material connecting areas; current for sources that track a changing field.*

### 1.1 — What Computer Science Is

Computer science studies computation and the practice of making computation useful. It asks a small set of recurring questions:

- How can a process be described precisely?
- What can and cannot be computed?
- How should information be represented, stored, moved, and queried?
- How can large systems remain correct, fast, secure, and maintainable under real constraints?
- How can computational systems learn, reason, interact, or act in the world?

Programming is the working medium of the field, but CS is broader than programming. It includes mathematical reasoning, system design, data representation, human interaction, and learned behavior.

### 1.2 — What Computer Science Does

CS repeatedly turns five things into objects of study:

1. **Procedures.** "Sort these records," "route this packet," or "train a classifier" becomes an algorithm, a program, a protocol, a model, or a proof obligation. Relevant areas: programming, algorithms, theory of computation, programming languages, type systems, formal methods. Guiding question: what is being computed, and what can be proved or measured about it?

2. **Machines.** Computation runs on processors, memory hierarchies, operating systems, runtimes, networks, storage engines, browsers, clusters, and accelerators. Relevant areas: architecture, operating systems, networks, compilers, databases, distributed systems, cloud infrastructure, embedded systems, HPC. Guiding question: what happens when an ideal process meets time, memory, hardware, concurrency, failure, and scale?

3. **Information.** Data must be represented, indexed, compressed, queried, transformed, visualized, embedded, secured, and interpreted. Relevant areas: data structures, databases, information retrieval, data engineering, graphics, scientific computing, much of ML. Guiding question: what representation makes the right operations possible?

4. **Behavior.** Programs respond to input; services coordinate; models predict or generate; robots act; interfaces shape human action; security systems resist adversaries. Relevant areas: application development, HCI, robotics, security, machine learning, reinforcement learning, AI systems. Guiding question: what should the artifact do in a changing environment?

5. **Trust, failure, and change.** Real software is modified, attacked, misunderstood, scaled, reused, and operated by teams. Relevant areas: testing, debugging, verification, observability, security engineering, reliability engineering, documentation, version control, software design. Guiding question: how can computational work remain understandable and dependable over time?

### 1.3 — Seven Regions of the Field

Read the field as seven connected regions:

1. **Foundational trunk:** programming; discrete mathematics; theory of computation; computer architecture; operating systems; networks; algorithms and data structures; databases; distributed systems. These provide the reusable vocabulary: processes, proof, abstraction, resources, representation, concurrency, persistence, and failure.

2. **Theoretical region:** programming language theory, type theory, formal semantics, logic, category theory, formal methods, cryptography, advanced complexity. It supplies the language of meaning, correctness, specification, proof, security, and impossibility.

3. **Systems region:** OS internals, database internals, stream processing, observability, cloud infrastructure, compilers, embedded systems, HPC. It studies performance, resource limits, faults, scheduling, concurrency, deployment, and operation.

4. **Intelligence region:** machine learning, deep learning, foundation models, reinforcement learning, classical AI, mechanistic interpretability, AI safety. It studies models, data, representation, optimization, generalization, agency, evaluation, and risk.

5. **Applied region:** web and application development; graphics and games; security; HCI; robotics; scientific computing. These areas combine multiple CS bodies of knowledge around domain constraints.

6. **Mathematical substrate:** linear algebra, probability, statistics, logic, optimization, calculus, algebra, category theory, discrete mathematics. The practical question is not "how much mathematics does CS require?" but "which mathematics does this direction require, and at what depth?"

7. **Tools and craft:** languages, Unix, version control, editors, build systems, debugging, testing, code reading, paper reading, writing, AI-assisted development. These determine whether knowledge becomes usable in real work.

Learn enough of the trunk to support later work; enter a specialization when a real interest appears; return to mathematics and tools when the specialization exposes a gap.

### 1.4 — How Subjects Combine

CS is not a single line from basics to applications. Most subjects draw from several regions at once, and the combination explains what preparation they actually require.

**Cryptography** is theory-heavy with systems consequences. It depends on algorithms, complexity, probability, and number theory — but real cryptographic work also meets protocols, side channels, key management, and deployment constraints. Learning only the mathematics misses why systems break; learning only the tooling misses what security claims mean.

**Databases** are data-centered but deeply systems-shaped. The relational model, query languages, and query optimization are about information structure; storage engines, concurrency control, replication, and recovery are systems problems. Modern databases further touch ML through learned indexes, vector search, and training data infrastructure.

**Machine learning** depends on linear algebra, probability, statistics, optimization, data engineering, numerical computing, distributed training, and domain judgment. This is why ML can be easy to run and hard to understand: the tooling hides several layers of mathematics, data work, and systems behavior.

**Web and application development** uses programming, networks, databases, distributed systems, security, UI design, observability, and deployment. Its difficulty is rarely about one deep theorem; it is about keeping many moving parts coherent while users, requirements, and infrastructure change.

**Compilers** are theoretical and systems-oriented at once. Parsing, semantics, type systems, and optimization connect to theory; code generation, runtime behavior, and memory layout connect to systems. A compiler is one of the clearest examples of abstract program meaning becoming executable machinery.

The practical lesson: when a subject feels unexpectedly hard, the missing background is often in a different region from the subject's name. A learner stuck in ML may need probability or systems; stuck in databases, concurrency; stuck in security, both formal definitions and operating-system detail.

### 1.5 — Three Kinds of Difficulty

Different subjects are hard in different ways, and confusing them wastes effort.

**Conceptual difficulty** means the subject introduces a genuinely new object of thought — recursion, virtual memory, proof by induction, the relational model, gradient descent, distributed consensus. No amount of practice resolves confusion that is actually conceptual; the remedy is slow reading, restatement, worked examples, and repeated exposure until the concept functions naturally.

**Technical difficulty** means the subject requires fluency with mechanisms: a language, framework, debugger, proof assistant, graphics API, or ML library. No amount of re-reading resolves confusion that is actually a practice deficit; the remedy is use, error, and correction.

**Compositional difficulty** means many individually understood parts must work together — an operating system, compiler, distributed database, robotics stack, or ML deployment pipeline. The remedy is building things, debugging failures, and reading implementations of systems that work.

Reading more does not always solve a practice problem. Building more does not always solve a conceptual one. Recognizing which difficulty is actually present is the first step.

### 1.6 — How to Read This Map

This map is not a curriculum. It does not prescribe an order, and finishing it is not a goal.

The resource sections answer a specific question: which resource should serve as first encounter, main spine, deeper extension, reference, corrective, bridge, or current source for this subject? They are not reading lists to be completed. A reader who uses them to choose one good spine and one corrective for a chosen subject is using them correctly.

For a reader without a specific direction, Chapter 2 is the right starting point: it covers the trunk subjects that every specialization reuses. For a reader with a specific target — systems, intelligence, theory, application — the relevant specialization chapter is the right starting point, with Chapter 2 as the diagnostic for what trunk knowledge is actually missing. Chapter 7 maps which mathematics a given direction requires and at what depth. Chapter 8 covers the cross-cutting practice — languages, tools, debugging, reading code and papers, writing — that makes subject knowledge usable.

A first pass through this map should produce orientation, not obligation. The goal is to know where things are.


## Chapter 2 — The Foundational Trunk

A subject belongs to the foundational trunk if and only if competent work in any major specialization of contemporary computer science depends on having internalized its conceptual content — not merely encountered it, not memorized it for an exam, but absorbed it deeply enough that its concepts function as cognitive equipment.

Six subjects that commonly appear on lists of CS foundations — the theory of computation, computer architecture, operating systems, networks, databases, and distributed systems — are treated in Chapters 3 and 4, where foundational grounding and specialization-level depth can be addressed together without repetition. What remains here are three subjects that belong to no single axis yet underlie all of them: programming as a way of thinking, the discrete mathematics that supports formal reasoning, and algorithms and data structures.

**On mathematics.** Discrete mathematics is the only mathematical subject that satisfies the trunk criterion across every major CS direction; it is treated fully in §2.2. Linear algebra, probability, and calculus are genuinely foundational for the intelligence axis but have limited necessity elsewhere; they are addressed in §5.0, where they are first required at real depth.

**On how CS knows what it knows.** CS produces knowledge through proofs, experiments, benchmarks, case studies, and system-building — and these modes are not interchangeable. A complexity result holds for all possible algorithms; an empirical ML result holds under specific training conditions; a benchmark result holds for specific hardware and workloads. Recognizing which epistemic mode a source is operating in is a foundational skill that no subject chapter teaches directly, so it is named here.


### 2.1 — Programming as Conceptual Practice

#### Code Is a Way of Thinking Precisely

Programming is often introduced as the skill of writing code that produces the right output. That description is not false, but it hides the real subject. Programming is the practice of constructing, manipulating, and reasoning about precise descriptions of processes.

This distinction matters because beginners and mature programmers do not merely differ in speed — they perceive different objects. A beginner sees statements, syntax, variables, and error messages. A mature programmer sees control flow, data flow, invariants, representations, interfaces, hidden assumptions, failure cases, and the shape of the process being described.

The conceptual content of programming is mostly language-independent. Programs involve sequence, branching, repetition, recursion, abstraction, composition, indirection, state, lifetime, scope, and representation — structures one can reason about. A program may terminate or fail to terminate, preserve or violate an invariant, expose or hide a representation, compose cleanly or leak assumptions across boundaries.

Programming is foundational for a specific reason: every later CS subject becomes concrete through programs. Operating systems are studied through kernel code and system calls. Machine learning becomes real through numerical programs and training loops. Databases expose their ideas through query execution, storage engines, and transaction logic. Programming is not one specialization among others; it is the medium in which the rest of the field becomes executable.

#### Meaning, Abstraction, Correctness

Three questions organize programming as a foundational subject.

**What does a program mean?** A program is finite text, but its execution may be enormous or unbounded. Its meaning can be understood operationally, as the states it passes through; denotationally, as the mathematical function it denotes; axiomatically, as relations between preconditions and postconditions; or behaviorally, as what it does in interaction with an environment. A capable programmer does not need formal semantics before writing useful programs, but they do need the habit behind semantics: code has meaning beyond the surface sequence of tokens.

**Where should abstraction happen?** Every abstraction hides something to make something else easier. Good abstraction hides accidental detail while preserving the right operations. Bad abstraction hides the very facts needed for reasoning. The difficult part is rarely knowing that abstraction is useful; it is knowing what boundary deserves to exist.

**What does correctness require?** Correct with respect to which specification, input assumptions, resource limits, failure modes, and user expectations? Testing examples is necessary but insufficient: the possible input space is too large, the specification may be incomplete, and real software sits inside changing environments. Type systems, tests, contracts, proofs, code review, and fault tolerance are different answers to the same pressure — programs are required to be right in situations no one can inspect exhaustively.

These three questions explain why programming is a trunk subject. Later chapters specialize each of them: programming language theory makes meaning formal; systems programming asks how abstractions behave under resources and failure; algorithms asks about the cost of processes; formal methods asks how correctness claims can be mechanically checked.

#### Why One Language Is Never Enough

Different regions of CS require different modes of programming, and fluency in only one mode is a ceiling.

Systems work requires programming close to the machine. C remains unavoidable for reading existing systems software; Rust is increasingly important for new systems work. Both force attention to memory layout, lifetime, and resource ownership in ways that higher-level languages conceal. Machine learning requires fluency in numerical programming — usually Python with NumPy, PyTorch, or JAX — where the ability to read array programs, understand automatic differentiation, and reason about training dynamics matters more than Python syntax. Programming language theory and formal methods reward functional fluency: Scheme, OCaml, Haskell, Lean, or Coq make recursion, types, reduction, and proof visible as first-class concerns. Application work requires pragmatic fluency with libraries, APIs, deployment, formats, and tests — real programming, but not a complete picture of the field.

The trunk-level goal is not mastery of every ecosystem. It is exposure to enough distinct styles that "programming" no longer means one language or one habitual way of decomposing a problem.

#### What Competence Looks Like

1. **Reading unfamiliar code.** Mature programmers can enter a codebase they did not write and infer its architecture, conventions, local abstractions, and failure points. This is acquired by reading real implementations, not by completing isolated exercises.

2. **Decomposing before typing.** Faced with a problem of moderate complexity, a mature programmer identifies subproblems, representations, interfaces, invariants, and build order before producing code. The typing is visible; the decomposition is the real work.

3. **Choosing the right paradigm.** State machines, recursive definitions, pipelines, relational transformations, event handlers, and constraint systems are different shapes of thought. A programmer with only one paradigm tends to force every problem into it.

4. **Using primary sources.** Language references, RFCs, manuals, source code, and library implementations are often more reliable than tutorials. Mature programmers learn to use unfriendly documentation because it is where the truth usually is.

5. **Debugging as model repair.** A hard bug is evidence that the programmer's mental model and the system's actual behavior diverge. The point is not only to make the symptom disappear; it is to discover which assumption was wrong.

6. **Knowing when not to build.** Programming rewards construction, but maturity includes recognizing when a problem needs analysis, proof, measurement, or design discussion rather than another layer of code.

#### The Stance Programming Teaches

Programming gives the learner a distinctive epistemic stance. Precision is enforced: ambiguous intentions do not execute, and code forces hidden assumptions into contact with machinery in a way that argument alone does not. Understanding becomes constructive: to understand recursion, parsing, gradient descent, or a scheduler in the programmer's sense is to be able to build a working version and explain its behavior. Artifacts become arguments: code is not merely output but a claim about how a process should be organized, and reading code means reading that claim. Construction is legitimate knowledge-making: some problems become clear only through building, and programming trains the ability to explore by making — while also exposing the cost of making too soon.

This stance is powerful but not complete. Mathematics proves; systems engineering measures; HCI studies users; security thinks adversarially. Programming is foundational because it provides one of the central stances, not because it replaces the others.

#### Traps That Produce Shallow Programmers

| Trap | Why it misleads | Better response |
|---|---|---|
| Language obsession | Treats programming as a choice between Python, Java, and Rust rather than as the study of process, abstraction, and correctness. | Choose a good first environment, then focus on the concepts the language makes visible. |
| Syntax comfort | Syntax is easy to see and easy to mistake for progress. | Work on problems that require recursion, decomposition, data modeling, and testing. |
| Premature framework dependence | Frameworks produce impressive artifacts while hiding the mechanisms a learner needs to understand. | Build a few things below the framework level: a parser, a web server, a toy database, a simple training loop. |
| Avoiding hard debugging | Escaping a bug avoids the moment where the mental model would have improved. | After a difficult bug, write down the wrong assumption, the evidence, and the mechanism that actually occurred. |
| Early AI overuse | Coding assistants can solve exercises before the learner has built the ability the exercise was meant to train. | Use little or no AI assistance during early exercises; introduce it later as an amplifier for work one can already explain. |
| Intermediate plateau | Working programs of moderate size can feel like mastery while design, performance, and correctness remain shallow. | Seek harder contexts: unfamiliar paradigms, existing codebases, systems-level code, formal verification, performance work. |

#### Resources

*Note: McConnell, Fowler, and Brooks, which concern programming-in-the-large — construction across teams, refactoring, and software project dynamics — are addressed in Chapter 7 (Software Engineering as a Discipline), where their concerns fit more naturally.*

| Resource | Use it for | Why it matters | Caveat / placement | Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Abelson & Sussman, *Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs* | Conceptual entry to programming | Frontloads recursion, higher-order functions, abstraction, and program structure before language trivia. | Demanding; best when the learner can tolerate mathematical style and slow progress. | Permanent canon, entry |
| Berkeley CS 61A / *Composing Programs* | More scaffolded route to SICP-style ideas | Keeps SICP's conceptual ambition while using Python and a modern course structure. | Use first if SICP is too abrupt; SICP remains a valuable depth pass. | Current canon, entry |
| Felleisen et al., *How to Design Programs* | Systematic program design | Teaches a disciplined route from problem statement to data definition, examples, templates, tests, and implementation. | Narrower than SICP; often the better first encounter for learners who need method over insight. | Permanent canon, entry |
| Kernighan & Ritchie, *The C Programming Language* | Imperative programming near the machine | Makes pointers, memory, arrays, compilation, and low-level representation unavoidable. | Not a complete systems education; pair with systems programming practice. | Permanent canon, depth |
| *The Rust Programming Language* | Modern systems programming | Makes ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, and memory safety explicit in ways useful for contemporary systems work. | Does not replace C for reading older systems code. | Current canon, bridge |
| Pierce, *Types and Programming Languages* | Bridge from programming to type theory | Shows how programming languages can be studied with mathematical precision; the transition from implementation to semantics. | More Chapter 3 than trunk; use after basic programming and discrete mathematics. | Permanent canon, depth |
| Bird, *Thinking Functionally with Haskell* / Harper, *Programming in Standard ML* | Functional programming as a different mode of thought | Trains equational, recursive, and type-aware programming habits that imperative experience does not produce. | The goal is paradigm fluency, not Haskell or ML specialization. | Depth |
| Van Roy & Haridi, *Concepts, Techniques, and Models of Computer Programming* | Unified view of paradigms | Treats paradigms as configurations of concepts rather than brand identities — the closest thing to a unified theory of programming. | Large and unconventional; better as a conceptual reference than a first text. | Heterodox, conceptual |
| Open-source codebases: Redis, SQLite, CPython, Lean, small Unix tools | Reading real programs | Builds the ability to infer architecture and design from code not written for the learner. | Choose codebases deliberately; the practice of large-scale reading is treated more fully in Chapter 8. | Project, bridge |
| Hunt & Thomas, *The Pragmatic Programmer* | Practitioner judgment | Covers habits of professional programming: feedback loops, automation, naming, and responsibility for outcomes. | Useful orientation; not a substitute for conceptual foundations. | Reference |
| Seibel, *Coders at Work* | How mature programmers think and differ | Long interviews reveal disagreement, taste, habit, and growth paths among strong practitioners. | Not instructional; use as perspective on what development looks like across careers. | Heterodox |
| Graham, *Hackers and Painters* | Programming as creative practice | A sharp, Lisp-centered, opinionated defense of programming as making and taste. | Read critically; useful because it provokes judgment, not because it is neutral. | Heterodox |
| Hillel Wayne, Russ Cox, Julia Evans, Bret Victor | Contemporary practitioner essays and talks | Strong writing on formal methods in practice, language design, debugging, and visual programming. | Sample selectively; following everything is not the point. | Current, ongoing |
| Language-specific books: *Effective Java*, *Effective Python*, *Programming Rust* | Professional idiom once foundations are in place | Useful when a language is being used seriously in real work and its conventions matter. | Poor first route into programming as a conceptual practice. | Defer |
| Framework-specific books and tutorials | Operational reference | Helpful when using React, Django, PyTorch, or Kubernetes professionally. | Do not confuse framework fluency with programming competence. | Defer |
| "Learn X in N days" books | Quick syntax exposure | May help with surface orientation. | Train speed and syntax, not depth. | Skip as primary |
| Martin, *Clean Code* | Naming and clarity advice | Contains useful advice and influenced a generation of programmers. | Prescriptive and often overgeneralized; balance with context-sensitive sources. | Use with caution |


### 2.2 — Discrete Mathematics and the Foundations of Proof

#### Proof Is the Entry Fee

Discrete mathematics is often described as the collection of mathematical topics useful in CS: logic, sets, functions, relations, combinatorics, graphs, trees, recurrence relations, number theory, and elementary probability. The list is useful, but it hides the point. Discrete mathematics is where CS first learns to make claims precise enough to prove.

This matters because many CS claims are universal. An algorithm works for all inputs of a certain form. A data structure preserves an invariant after every operation. A protocol cannot guarantee two properties at once. A grammar generates exactly one language. A recurrence has a particular asymptotic solution. None of these claims can be established by trying examples. They require definitions, assumptions, and arguments.

The trunk-level purpose of discrete mathematics is therefore not to collect clever tricks. It is to train the reader to ask:

- What exactly is being claimed?
- What objects does the claim range over?
- Which assumptions are being used?
- What would count as a proof?
- What would count as a counterexample?

Without this training, later theoretical subjects become vocabulary without force. With it, statements about algorithms, types, automata, databases, protocols, and programs become things one can evaluate rather than merely repeat.

#### The CS Slice of Mathematics

Discrete mathematics is not a single natural branch of mathematics. It is a CS-shaped selection from several areas, organized around finite, countable, symbolic, and recursively defined structures.

| Topic | What it gives CS |
|---|---|
| Logic | Precise claims, inference, quantifiers, implication, satisfiability, and the grammar of formal reasoning. |
| Sets, relations, and functions | The basic language for objects, mappings, equivalence, order, composition, and structure. |
| Proof techniques | Induction, contradiction, construction, contrapositive, case analysis, pigeonhole arguments, and counting arguments. |
| Combinatorics | Counting, enumeration, recurrence, symmetry, and the analysis of large families of objects. |
| Graphs and trees | The language of networks, dependencies, paths, connectivity, recursion, syntax, and many data structures. |
| Recurrences and asymptotics | The mathematical basis for algorithm analysis and resource growth. |
| Number theory | Modular arithmetic, divisibility, primes, and the substrate for cryptography and several algorithmic areas. |
| Probability in discrete settings | Randomized algorithms, hashing, average-case reasoning, and the probabilistic method. |

The unity is not the topic list. The unity is the practice of defining discrete objects and proving claims about them.

#### Three Problems This Subject Solves

Discrete mathematics answers three foundational needs in CS.

1. **Formal precision.** Natural language leaves slack. Mathematics reduces slack by forcing definitions: what is a graph, a path, a function, a relation, a language, an invariant? CS needs this discipline because tiny ambiguities often become large bugs or false theorems.

2. **Finite arguments about unbounded cases.** CS constantly makes claims about unbounded families: all input sizes, all states reachable by a program, all strings in a language, all graphs of a given class. Proof techniques make finite reasoning cover infinitely many cases. Induction and structural induction matter because many computational objects are recursive.

3. **Structural recognition.** Lists, trees, graphs, grammars, automata, programs, and proofs have shape. Once the shape is identified, a problem often becomes an instance of a known pattern. This is why graph theory, induction, recurrence solving, and combinatorics appear everywhere in CS.

#### Why It Comes Before Most of the Theoretical Trunk

Discrete mathematics is load-bearing for most of the theoretical trunk and much of the rest. The theory of computation is built from logic, formal languages, and induction. Algorithms and data structures depend on recurrences, asymptotics, graph structure, and invariant reasoning. Databases build on relational logic and query constraints. Distributed systems depend on impossibility arguments and probabilistic reasoning about concurrent behavior. Cryptography is largely applied discrete mathematics: number theory, probability, and proof under computational assumptions. Programming language theory, type theory, and formal methods all rest on inductive definitions, structural induction, and the proof techniques this subject develops.

The relationship to programming is more subtle. A programmer can write useful software without discrete mathematics. But reasoning about programs beyond informal testing requires it: termination, invariants, correctness, complexity, and safety properties all depend on the habits this subject trains.

Discrete mathematics is more central to the foundational trunk than calculus. Calculus is essential for the intelligence axis, graphics, scientific computing, and control; it is not the default prerequisite for most of the foundational trunk. Discrete mathematics is.

#### Competence Means Choosing the Right Argument

Discrete-mathematical competence is visible in practical habits:

1. **Reading claims carefully.** The reader can parse quantifiers, assumptions, definitions, and the difference between a statement that seems plausible and one that has actually been established.

2. **Writing proofs.** The reader can choose among induction, contradiction, construction, contrapositive, case analysis, counting, and probabilistic arguments, then assemble a proof whose conclusion really follows.

3. **Strengthening definitions.** Definitions are not labels; they are machinery. A good definition makes proof and implementation easier. A weak definition leaves ambiguity that later gets patched in prose, code, or theorem statements.

4. **Thinking combinatorially.** The reader can count cases, recognize symmetry, identify recursive structure, estimate growth, and avoid brute enumeration when structure is available.

5. **Using rigor without worshipping it.** Proof is superior to testing when the claim is mathematical and the proof is available. But not every engineering question should be forced into full formalism. Maturity is knowing which standard of evidence fits the claim.

#### Traps That Keep It Superficial

| Trap | Why it misleads | Better response |
|---|---|---|
| Treating it as formula manipulation | Some topics involve computation, but the central activity is argument. | Prefer exercises that require proof, explanation, or counterexample, not only numerical answers. |
| Memorizing proofs | Reproducing a proof is not the same as learning the technique it demonstrates. | After reading a proof, identify the reusable move it used, then try a nearby problem without looking. |
| Underestimating induction | The mechanics are simple; choosing the right inductive hypothesis is hard. | Practice strong induction, structural induction, and the move of proving a stronger statement than the original target. |
| Resisting formality as pedantry | In this subject, precision is the content, not decoration. | Treat definitions as tools for preventing ambiguity, then later decide how much formality a practical situation needs. |
| Treating proof as performance | A proof can be technically correct and still obscure the idea. | Value proofs that reveal why the result is true, not only proofs that close the argument. |

#### Resources

| Resource | Use it for | Why it matters | Caveat / placement | Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lehman, Leighton, Meyer, *Mathematics for Computer Science* (MIT, free) | Main CS-oriented entry | Treats proof as central, covers the right CS-facing material, and integrates probability better than most alternatives. Freely available. | Dense enough to require real work; best paired with exercises and lectures. | Current canon, entry, spine |
| MIT 6.042J course videos | Companion to *Mathematics for Computer Science* | Provides pacing, worked examples, and explanations for self-learners using the text. | Videos should support problem work, not replace it. | Current canon, entry |
| Velleman, *How to Prove It* | Proof bottleneck | Systematic treatment of proof technique — quantifiers, induction, proof strategies — for the reader who can follow definitions but cannot yet construct arguments. | Narrow; follow with a broader discrete mathematics text. | Permanent canon, entry |
| Hammack, *Book of Proof* (free) | Proof bottleneck, free alternative | Similar scope to Velleman, freely available, and often clearer on specific proof strategies. A natural first choice when cost or access matters. | Does not cover the full CS-facing discrete mathematics curriculum; pair with MCS. | Current canon, entry |
| Rosen, *Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications* | Broad traditional reference | Comprehensive, example-rich, and useful for checking standard topics when another explanation is needed. | More conventional and less conceptually oriented than MCS; not ideal as the primary self-study spine. | Reference |
| Graham, Knuth, Patashnik, *Concrete Mathematics* | Sums, recurrences, asymptotics, and combinatorics at depth | Written by and for computer scientists; unusually strong for the mathematics behind algorithm analysis. | Demanding; best after an introductory pass through MCS or equivalent. | Permanent canon, depth |
| Mitzenmacher & Upfal, *Probability and Computing* | Probability for algorithms and randomized methods | Connects probability directly to hashing, randomized algorithms, and approximation — closer to §2.3 than to trunk-level discrete math. | More specialized than trunk-level discrete math; use when algorithms become the bottleneck. | Depth, bridge |
| Diestel, *Graph Theory* / Bondy & Murty, *Graph Theory with Applications* | Graph theory beyond the introductory level | Useful for algorithms, networks, distributed systems, and any graph-heavy specialization. | Not needed before basic graph concepts are comfortable. | Depth |
| Hardy & Wright, *An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers* / Niven, Zuckerman & Montgomery | Number theory, especially for cryptography | Gives modular arithmetic, primes, congruences, and related tools that cryptography and some algorithmic areas require. | Only specific paths need depth here; introductory number theory in MCS is enough for most. | Depth |
| Enderton, *A Mathematical Introduction to Logic* / Huth & Ryan, *Logic in Computer Science* | Logic beyond the operational level | Supports formal methods, type theory, computability, and semantics — the bridge between discrete mathematics and the computation axis. | More §3.6 than trunk; defer unless logic specifically becomes the bottleneck. | Depth, bridge to §3.6 |
| Pólya, *How to Solve It* | Problem-solving heuristics | Teaches moves such as specializing, generalizing, analogy, decomposition, and working backward — useful corrective for learners who have technique without method. | General rather than CS-specific. | Heterodox |
| Pólya, *Mathematics and Plausible Reasoning* | Mathematical discovery before proof | Explains how conjectures form before proof — a perspective that polished textbooks systematically hide. | Best after some proof experience; its lessons are harder to absorb cold. | Heterodox |
| Aigner & Ziegler, *Proofs from THE BOOK* | Mathematical taste | Shows what elegant proofs look like across many subjects. | Not a training manual; use for taste and motivation, not as a curriculum. | Heterodox |
| Lean, Mathlib, and the Xena Project | Formalized mathematics as a companion practice | Shows how proof becomes machine-checkable; useful for verifying a completed argument and for developing facility with tools that are reshaping mathematical practice. | Do not let tooling replace pencil-and-paper proof skill; proof assistants are treated fully in §3.5. | Current, bridge to §3.5 |
| "Discrete math for engineers" books | Quick operational exposure | Occasionally useful for formula-level review. | Usually too light on proof for serious CS foundations. | Skip as primary |
| "Math for programmers" books | Motivation and informal orientation | Can reduce fear for beginners. | Rarely enough for the trunk; use only as a bridge to serious material. | Skip as primary |

1. "Three Organizing X" 成了新模板
我们用这个结构替换了原来的 "The deep problems the field tries to answer"，但现在它本身成了固定格式。Chapter 4 应该更灵活：有些节用两个张力、有些节用一个核心洞见展开、有些节根本不需要这种框架。
2. Curry-Howard 重复出现

3. 资源重复噪音
TAPL 在 §3.4 和 §3.5 均出现，Software Foundations 在 §3.5 和 §3.6 均出现，Chlipala CPDT 同上。目前用"see §X.Y"标注，但读者仍需在两个表格中看到同一条目。改进方案：后出现的节点将这类资源从表格中删去，正文中一句引用即可。
4. §3.3 Complexity 的 Class Landscape 段落过细
列出了 PH、BPP、IP、MIP、#P、BQP、FPT、Fine-grained 等全部分类，在一个 landscape map 中可能过于详尽。对于非专业读者来说，这是参考性而非学习性内容，可以压缩为表格或更简短的概览。
5. "What the Subject Produces" 跨节形式过于一致
每节都有 3-5 条编号列表，格式高度相似。在 Chapter 4 中可以用散文段落替代部分编号列表，或直接省略，视节的性质而定。
6. Contemporary Relevance 有撞车现象
多个节都提到了"工具成熟度提升"和"LLM 的影响"，措辞不同但论点相近。每个节的 Contemporary Relevance 应该聚焦该领域真正独特的近期变化。 -->
## Chapter 3 — The Computation Axis
