| Pure-theory real-time books without practice | Incomplete coverage | Skip |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Embedded and real-time systems is among the more contemporarily active subjects on the systems axis. Six developments are reshaping the discipline now.

1. **Growth in embedded systems complexity, driven by automotive, medical, and industrial-IoT contexts.** Modern automotive electronic control units, medical device computers, and industrial controllers run software more sophisticated than the embedded software of a decade ago, with significant connectivity, autonomy, and functional safety requirements. The engineering practices required for this software are more demanding than the engineering practices that earlier embedded systems required. The trajectory points toward continued increase in complexity, with corresponding increase in the engineering disciplines required.

2. **Rust adoption in embedded contexts, particularly safety-critical contexts.** Rust's safety properties — eliminating entire classes of memory-safety bugs at compile time, while supporting the resource-aware programming embedded contexts require — have produced enthusiasm in embedded communities. The contemporary Rust embedded ecosystem is growing rapidly, with adoption visible in automotive contexts, in firmware for various devices, and in safety-critical systems being developed in Rust from the ground up. The trajectory points toward continued Rust adoption, with implications for the dominant role C has played in embedded contexts for several decades.

3. **The maturation of edge AI and TinyML.** Deploying machine learning inference on constrained embedded devices has produced new tooling, new techniques (quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation), and new application contexts. The trajectory points toward continued development of edge AI as a subdiscipline at the intersection of embedded systems and machine learning, with implications for both fields.

4. **The increasing connectivity of embedded systems.** Earlier embedded systems were largely isolated; contemporary embedded systems are connected, often as part of broader IoT or industrial systems, with significant implications for security, software updates, and operational management. Embedded security has emerged as a concern, with literature on secure embedded systems development. The trajectory points toward continued increase in connectivity with continued development of the security disciplines required.

5. **The maturation of formal methods in embedded contexts.** The high-reliability requirements of safety-critical embedded systems have driven adoption of formal verification techniques — SPARK Ada in aerospace and defense, various model checking tools in automotive and medical contexts, the formal verification of specific kernel components in operating systems used in safety-critical contexts. The trajectory points toward continued formal methods adoption, with implications for how the most demanding embedded engineering is done.

6. **The emergence of RISC-V as an alternative to ARM in embedded contexts.** RISC-V, as an open instruction set with industrial backing, is increasingly an alternative to ARM for embedded processors. The contemporary RISC-V ecosystem is significant, with silicon vendors producing RISC-V microcontrollers, growing software support, and growing industrial adoption. The trajectory in this area is genuinely uncertain — ARM remains dominant — but RISC-V's growth is real, and the embedded landscape may look different in a decade than it does today.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of embedded engineering is robust. The core concerns — computation under resource constraint, timing as correctness, integration with physical systems, reliability under environmental adversity — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Embedded and real-time systems is the systems-axis subject most distant from general-purpose software engineering. The investment in this specialization is deep and specific: embedded engineering competence does not transfer to general-purpose software engineering as cleanly as competence in some other systems-axis subjects does, and the converse is similarly limited. Learners pursuing this specialization should expect to develop discipline-specific engineering practices through extended practice, and should recognize that the gap between embedded and general-purpose engineering reflects a genuine difference in what the disciplines require rather than a transient difference that will erode over time.

### 4.11 — High-Performance Computing

#### What it is, properly

High-performance computing is the discipline of computation at the largest scales of resource consumption — using thousands or millions of processor cores, terabytes or petabytes of memory, hundreds or thousands of accelerators, all coordinated to solve problems that smaller systems cannot solve at all or cannot solve in usable time. The subject treats computation as a physical engineering activity, with attention to the specific hardware architectures of large systems, the software techniques required to use those architectures effectively, and the scientific and engineering applications that motivate the field. The discipline has historical depth — supercomputing predates much of contemporary software engineering — and unusual contemporary breadth, with HPC techniques now influencing machine learning infrastructure, financial computing, simulation across the sciences, and the accelerated computation that increasingly characterizes contemporary infrastructure.

The conventional account treats HPC as *scientific computing on supercomputers* — large simulations of physical systems running on the largest machines available, primarily of interest to physicists, chemists, climate scientists, and computational biologists. The account captures part of the field but understates its contemporary breadth. Three recognitions reshape how the discipline is approached. Exploiting parallelism at large scale requires different engineering disciplines than sequential or modestly parallel computation. Performance at scale is determined by data movement at least as much as by computation itself. The engineering of high-performance systems requires the integration of hardware specifics, algorithmic choices, and software techniques in ways that smaller-scale engineering can typically separate. The HPC engineer reasons about computation in terms of memory hierarchies, communication patterns, parallel algorithm design, and the specific characteristics of the hardware being targeted, with disciplines and tools that general-purpose engineering does not provide.

The subject divides into several distinct concerns. *Parallel computing* is the foundational subject of computing using multiple processing units simultaneously — the algorithms, the programming models, the synchronization techniques, forms of parallelism. *Distributed-memory computing* is the subdiscipline of computing across multiple machines connected by networks, where the parallel processing units do not share memory and must communicate through explicit messaging. *Shared-memory parallelism* is the contrasting subdiscipline of computing within single machines where multiple processors share memory, with its own characteristic concerns: cache coherence, false sharing, various synchronization primitives. *Accelerated computing* is the contemporary subdiscipline of computing on specialized hardware (GPUs initially, with extension to TPUs, FPGAs, and various AI accelerators) — the most active area of contemporary HPC. *Performance engineering* is the discipline of analyzing and optimizing the performance of computational programs, with overlap with HPC but extending beyond it. *Scientific computing* is the application-oriented subject concerned with the numerical and computational techniques required for scientific simulation — the original motivation for HPC, though the techniques have spread far beyond their origin.

The intellectual content of HPC is distinctive in several ways. Integration with hardware is deeper than in most CS subjects: HPC engineers regularly engage with the specifics of memory hierarchies, with the characteristics of specific interconnects, with the architectural details of specific accelerators, in ways that general-purpose software engineering largely abstracts away. The mathematical content is load-bearing: numerical analysis, linear algebra, optimization theory, and forms of applied mathematics matter directly, with HPC sitting unusually close to applied mathematics among CS subjects. The performance discipline is more rigorous: HPC engineers regularly engage with formal performance modeling, with detailed empirical performance analysis, with the tools for understanding precisely where computational time and resources are spent. Scaling concerns are more demanding: programs that work correctly on small inputs may perform catastrophically poorly at scale, and the engineering of programs that maintain efficiency as resources grow is its own discipline.

This account matters because the gap between HPC engineering and general-purpose software engineering is large, and the cost of this gap has been increasing as more software begins to face HPC-like concerns. Machine learning infrastructure now routinely faces HPC-scale concerns — training large models requires HPC-style engineering, and inference at scale faces similar concerns. Contemporary data engineering increasingly faces HPC-style scaling concerns. Scientific computing continues to require HPC engineering. Engineers competent in this discipline have access to techniques that general-purpose engineers lack, and the gap is increasingly visible in the performance differences between systems built by HPC-trained engineers and systems built by engineers approaching scale problems with general-purpose backgrounds.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Four problems organize high-performance computing as a discipline. They cut across the spectrum from a small cluster to the largest exascale system, and they recur whenever a high-performance system is being designed.

##### Parallelism extraction and management

Programs run faster on parallel systems only to the extent that they actually parallelize — that the work decomposes into pieces that execute simultaneously on different processing units. The discipline of HPC is the discipline of finding parallelism in computational problems and managing it effectively across the available resources.

The intellectual content includes the body of parallel algorithms (parallel reduction, scan, sorting, various parallel graph algorithms, the work on parallel numerical methods), the parallel programming models (shared memory, message passing, partitioned global address space, various task-based models, various GPU programming models), and the analytical frameworks for understanding parallel performance — Amdahl's law, Gustafson's law, various scaling analyses, the work-span model, various complexity models for parallel computation. The mathematical content is real: the analysis of parallel algorithms is genuinely a subdiscipline of theoretical computer science, with connections to the broader complexity theory of Sections 2.3 and 3.6.

##### Data movement at scale

At HPC scale, the cost of moving data dominates the cost of computing on it. A floating-point operation on contemporary hardware takes nanoseconds; reading from memory takes hundreds of nanoseconds; reading from a disk takes milliseconds; communicating across a network takes microseconds to milliseconds depending on distance. Programs that manage data movement well perform orders of magnitude better than programs that do not, regardless of how computationally efficient their algorithms are in isolation.

The discipline of HPC is the discipline of engineering data movement: choosing memory layouts that support efficient access, designing algorithms that exhibit locality, structuring communication to minimize latency and maximize bandwidth, exploiting forms of memory hierarchy. The intellectual content includes cache-aware and cache-oblivious algorithms, forms of data partitioning, the work on communication-avoiding algorithms (which trade additional computation for reduced communication), the techniques for overlapping computation with communication, and forms of collective communication. The achievement is the recognition that performance at scale is determined as much by data architecture as by algorithm choice.

##### Heterogeneous and specialized hardware

Contemporary HPC systems are heterogeneous — combining general-purpose CPUs with specialized accelerators (GPUs, TPUs, FPGAs, various AI accelerators), with each component having different performance characteristics, different programming models, and different best-fit workloads. Programming such systems requires reasoning about which work runs on which hardware, how data flows between heterogeneous components, and how to coordinate various processing units to make effective use of the system as a whole.

The intellectual content includes the body of techniques for heterogeneous programming: the GPU programming models (CUDA, ROCm, SYCL, OpenCL), the techniques for offloading work to accelerators, the work on automatic parallelization and accelerator targeting, the unified-programming models across heterogeneous systems. Contemporary developments are: the rise of AI accelerators, the work on heterogeneous programming abstractions (oneAPI, and others), and the broader integration of accelerated computing into mainstream HPC. Engineers approaching contemporary HPC must engage with this heterogeneity directly, since homogeneous systems are essentially extinct at the highest performance levels.

##### Correctness and reproducibility at scale

Parallel and distributed programs have more opportunities for error than sequential programs, and the errors are harder to detect and reproduce. Race conditions, deadlocks, forms of data corruption from incorrect synchronization, and forms of numerical instability that parallel computation can introduce all become engineering concerns at HPC scale. The numerical reproducibility concerns are particularly subtle: parallel reductions can produce different floating-point results depending on the order of operations, and programs that produce different results on different runs are genuinely problematic for scientific computing.

The intellectual content includes the techniques for parallel correctness (the synchronization disciplines, the tools for detecting races and deadlocks), the work on numerical reproducibility (deterministic parallel reduction, the careful analysis of floating-point operations at scale), and the forms of fault tolerance that become essential at the largest scales, where hardware failures during computation become likely rather than negligibly rare. The engineering of correct and reproducible parallel programs at scale is genuinely difficult, and the discipline is correspondingly demanding.

#### Where it sits in the map

High-performance computing depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the systems-axis trunk: computer organization (Section 2.4) is foundational — HPC engineering depends on understanding how memory hierarchies, processor pipelines, and various hardware features actually behave, and the architectural fluency that Section 2.4 develops is load-bearing. Operating systems (Section 2.5) provides the conceptual foundation for understanding how operating systems support or hinder HPC workloads. Computer networks (Section 2.6) provides the foundation for understanding the interconnect technologies that distributed-memory HPC depends on. Algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides the foundation for the algorithmic content of HPC, with the parallel algorithm material extending Section 2.7. Distributed systems (Section 2.9) connects to HPC but with different emphasis — distributed systems concerns reliability and consistency at scale, while HPC concerns performance at scale, with the engineering disciplines diverging accordingly.

From the computation axis, engagement with theoretical computer science is direct. The complexity theory material in Sections 2.3 and 3.6 is foundational, with parallel complexity theory extending the basic complexity material into the parallel domain. Various forms of theoretical analysis of parallel algorithms constitute a subdiscipline.

From the data axis, engagement is increasing. Contemporary HPC handles data volumes, with the data engineering of large-scale scientific datasets, the data engineering of machine learning training datasets, and forms of data management at HPC scale all constituting active areas. The intersection with database systems (Section 4.2) and with stream processing (Section 4.4) is active.

From the intelligence axis, engagement has grown over the past decade and is now arguably the dominant motivation for contemporary HPC. The training of large machine learning models is one of the largest contemporary uses of HPC resources, and the engineering of training infrastructure is HPC engineering. The intersection with machine learning has reshaped both fields: HPC has become more focused on the workloads that ML imposes, and ML infrastructure has become more sophisticated as it has absorbed HPC engineering disciplines.

The relationship to compilers (Section 4.7) is direct. HPC depends on compiler optimization for performance, and contemporary HPC includes work on domain-specific compilers — particularly for ML workloads, as discussed in Section 4.7. The intersection of HPC with compilers (auto-vectorization, various polyhedral optimization techniques, forms of automatic parallelization) is an active research area.

The relationship to embedded systems (Section 4.8) is one of contrast. HPC and embedded systems represent opposite ends of the resource spectrum, with different engineering disciplines. But the disciplines share concerns: detailed performance characterization, the integration of computation with specific hardware, the careful engineering of resource use. Engineers competent in one of these disciplines often find the other accessible despite the differences.

Within this map, the subject sits squarely on the System axis, with engagement across the other axes. Engagement with the Computation axis runs through parallel complexity theory and forms of theoretical analysis. Engagement with the Data axis runs through the data engineering at scale that contemporary HPC requires. Engagement with the Intelligence axis is increasing through the ML workload pressure on contemporary HPC infrastructure. The discipline is unusual in its engagement with applied mathematics and with specific scientific application domains — engagement that more general-purpose CS subjects do not require.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized high-performance computing acquires several capacities that distinguish them from general-purpose software engineers approaching scale problems.

The most fundamental is **fluency with parallel programming**. HPC requires reasoning about parallel algorithms, expressing parallel computations in major programming models (MPI, OpenMP, GPU programming models, task-based models), and debugging parallel programs effectively. Parallelism is considered from the start of algorithm design rather than added afterward.

Closely related is **the discipline of performance analysis**. Performance work needs hypothesis-driven investigation, profiling tools, performance models, and careful distinction between correlation and causation. The goal is analysis that converges on actual problems rather than apparent symptoms.

A third capacity is **fluency with the memory hierarchy**. HPC work depends on reasoning about cache levels, main memory, distributed memory, and storage hierarchies, then explaining program behavior in terms of how computation interacts with those hierarchies.

A fourth, more habit-than-skill capacity is **the disposition for measuring rather than assuming**. HPC treats performance assumptions with skepticism until measurement validates them. Optimization is driven by evidence rather than intuition.

A fifth capacity is **facility with mathematical techniques relevant to scale**. HPC depends on numerical linear algebra, numerical analysis, basic optimization theory, and error analysis. This mathematical preparation distinguishes HPC from purely software-focused performance work.

The final capacity is **the disposition for hardware-specific reasoning**. Programs are understood in relation to the hardware they run on, and hardware characteristics become part of algorithm design. This runs against much general-purpose abstraction, but it is essential for HPC and other high-performance contexts.

#### How the field sees the world

High-performance computing embeds a worldview that is unusually distinctive among CS subjects.

##### Performance as primary engineering concern

Performance is a first-class engineering concern, addressed deliberately throughout development rather than as a residual concern handled at the end if time allows. The commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer correctness and clarity over performance and treat performance work as suspect optimization. The HPC engineer takes a different view: at the scales HPC operates at, performance is essential to the engineering goal, and treating it as secondary produces systems that fail to accomplish what they were built for. The disposition produces engineering that integrates performance thinking from the start rather than retrofitting it onto pre-existing engineering, and produces engineers capable of performance work rather than only the more limited optimization that secondary-concern treatment produces.

##### Hardware as engineering substance

The specific hardware programs run on is integral to engineering rather than incidental detail, and competent HPC engineering requires engagement with hardware specifics. This commitment runs against the abstraction layers that much of contemporary software engineering relies on. The HPC engineer engages directly with cache behavior, vector instruction sets, interconnect characteristics, accelerator architectures, and other hardware specifics that affect program performance. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat hardware fluency as essential engineering preparation rather than as an optional specialization.

##### Measurement as primary epistemology

Performance properties of systems are properly known through measurement, not through reasoning from first principles, and the measurement discipline is integral to engineering practice. This commitment shapes how HPC engineers approach problems: they instrument programs, collect detailed performance data, construct measurement-based models, and treat untested performance assumptions with skepticism. The disposition is informed by extensive experience with the gap between intuition and measurement — between what engineers expect performance to be and what measurement reveals — and it produces engineering practice more empirically grounded than the more intuitive performance work practiced elsewhere.

##### The integration of mathematics with engineering

Competent work in the discipline requires mathematical fluency — in numerical analysis, in linear algebra, in optimization theory, in forms of applied mathematics that HPC depends on. This commitment runs against the disciplinary separation that much of contemporary CS practices, where mathematical work is often delegated to specific roles. HPC engineering integrates the mathematical and the engineering work as continuous activities, with the mathematical preparation shaping what engineering can accomplish. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment do not separate mathematics from engineering but treat them as integrated dimensions of the work.

##### Scale as real concern

Scaling — the behavior of systems as resources grow — is a primary engineering concern with its own disciplines. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat scale as something that can be added later, that programs which work at small scales will straightforwardly extend to large ones. The HPC engineer takes a different view: programs that work at small scale often fail at large scale, and the engineering of programs that maintain efficiency as resources grow is a discipline. The disposition produces engineering that considers scaling characteristics from the start, that constructs programs whose efficiency at small scale predicts efficiency at large scale, and that recognizes the engineering required to achieve this property.

##### Application context as integral to engineering

HPC engineering operates in close contact with specific scientific and engineering applications — climate simulation, computational chemistry, structural analysis, forms of molecular dynamics, the contemporary machine learning workloads. The commitment is to engaging with these application contexts as integral to engineering rather than as external specifications. The HPC engineer engages with the scientists and engineers whose computations they support, learns the application domain at the level required to make sound engineering decisions, and treats the application context as integral rather than as user requirements to be implemented mechanically. This commitment produces engineering that integrates with scientific practice rather than serving it from a distance, and engineers capable of collaboration across disciplinary boundaries.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning high-performance computing are characteristic and distinct from the pitfalls of other systems-axis subjects. The discipline's distance from general-purpose software engineering and its mathematical content create many opportunities for misdirected learning.

##### Parallelism as keyword

Engineers approaching parallel programming sometimes treat parallelism as something added through specific syntactic constructs — annotating loops with parallel pragmas, calling parallel library functions, other surface-level mechanisms — without engaging with the underlying engineering of parallel algorithms. The result is parallel code that fails to scale: the parallelism is nominal but the actual computation is not effectively parallelized, the data dependencies prevent useful parallel execution, the synchronization overhead dominates the parallel speedup. The remedy is to engage with parallelism as algorithm design rather than as syntactic transformation: to study parallel algorithms seriously, to understand which computations parallelize well and which do not, to design programs from the start with effective parallelism rather than retrofitting parallelism onto sequential designs.

##### Speedup without baseline

Engineers measuring parallel performance sometimes report speedups that are misleading because the sequential baseline is not actually well-optimized — the parallel code shows speedup over a poor sequential baseline, when an optimized sequential implementation might actually be faster than the parallel one. This pattern is particularly common when comparing GPU implementations against CPU implementations, where naive CPU implementations can be slower than optimized CPU implementations, and the apparent GPU speedup may evaporate against an optimized CPU baseline. The remedy is to use well-optimized sequential baselines for comparison, to report speedups against the best available sequential implementation rather than against unoptimized baselines, and to recognize that meaningful parallel speedup requires both effective parallelization and optimization at the sequential level.

##### Data-locality blindness

Engineers approaching HPC sometimes focus on the computational aspects of programs while neglecting the data movement aspects — designing algorithms with good asymptotic computational complexity but poor locality properties, with the result that performance is dominated by memory and communication overhead rather than by computation. At HPC scale, this pattern produces programs that use enormous resources without achieving useful work — most of the resources are spent moving data rather than computing on it. The remedy is to treat data movement as a primary concern from the start of algorithm design: to consider memory access patterns, to design for locality, to engage with forms of cache-aware and communication-avoiding algorithms, and to recognize that at scale, where data is and how it moves often matters more than what is computed on it.

##### GPU as magic

GPU acceleration has become marketable, and engineers can adopt GPUs without engaging with the engineering required to use them effectively. The result is GPU code that fails to achieve speedup over CPU baselines, that uses GPU resources poorly, that runs into various subtle GPU programming pitfalls (warp divergence, memory access patterns that defeat coalescing, various synchronization overheads). The remedy is to engage with GPU programming as an engineering discipline with its own characteristic concerns: to study the GPU programming literature seriously, to understand the GPU architecture at the level required for effective programming, to use GPU profiling tools to understand actual GPU behavior. The discipline transfers across GPU vendors but requires investment to develop.

##### Numerical-stability neglect

Engineers approaching numerical computation sometimes treat floating-point arithmetic as approximately equivalent to real-number arithmetic, neglecting various ways floating-point computation can produce results different from mathematical expectations. At HPC scale, the accumulated effect of small floating-point errors can produce results that are incorrect, and the discipline of numerical stability — designing computations that produce accurate results despite floating-point error, analyzing how errors propagate through computations — is essential. The remedy is to engage with the numerical analysis literature seriously: to study how floating-point error propagates, to understand the techniques for stable computation, to use the analytical tools for understanding numerical behavior. The investment is demanding but produces engineers capable of computation that produces meaningful results at scale.

##### Benchmark-driven optimization

HPC has benchmark culture, with the standard benchmarks (HPL, HPCG, and others) providing performance measures for system comparison. Engineers can come to optimize for benchmarks rather than for actual workloads, with the result that their work produces good benchmark numbers but limited improvement on the problems users actually care about. The remedy is to recognize that benchmarks are useful tools but not the engineering goal: to understand what specific workloads need to perform well, to optimize for those workloads rather than for benchmark proxies, and to use benchmarks as one of several tools rather than as the primary engineering target.

##### Reproducibility neglect

Parallel computation can produce results that vary across runs — particularly with floating-point reductions where order of operations affects results — and engineers sometimes fail to engage with this reproducibility concern adequately. For scientific computing applications, results that vary across runs are genuinely problematic: they undermine the scientific use of the computation. The discipline of reproducible parallel computation has techniques for deterministic parallel reduction, careful management of random number generators in parallel contexts, and various forms of bit-reproducible computation. The remedy is to engage with reproducibility as an engineering concern from the start: to understand which computations require reproducible results, to use the appropriate techniques for those computations, and to verify reproducibility where it matters.

##### The LLM shortcut, applied to HPC work

An eighth pitfall, increasingly relevant, is the LLM shortcut as it applies to HPC work. Modern AI tools can produce parallel code, GPU code, and other forms of HPC code, but the cost of LLM shortcuts is unusually high in HPC. The tools tend to produce code that follows common patterns, but HPC engineering often requires departures from common patterns to achieve actual performance, and code that appears reasonable can perform worse than carefully engineered alternatives. Furthermore, HPC depends on internalized understanding of hardware behavior, of parallel algorithm design, and of performance analysis that the cognitive struggle of working through HPC problems develops, and the LLM shortcut bypasses this development. The discipline required is to do early HPC work with deliberate attention to the underlying concerns, even when AI assistance could produce working code, and to use AI tools as supplements to internalized understanding rather than as replacements for it.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on high-performance computing has a distinctive shape. The discipline has historical depth, with foundational texts dating from the 1980s and 1990s that remain authoritative on conceptual fundamentals. The contemporary literature is unusually diverse, with academic textbooks, vendor documentation, conference proceedings, and practitioner literature all constituting load-bearing material. The intersection with applied mathematics produces overlap with the numerical analysis and scientific computing literatures.

##### Canonical entry points

The standard introduction to parallel programming is Pacheco and Malensek's *An Introduction to Parallel Programming* (second edition, 2021). The book covers the major parallel programming models — MPI, OpenMP, CUDA at an introductory level — at the depth required for foundational competence. It is appropriate as a first text for serious engagement with parallel programming.

For more depth on the conceptual foundations, Lin and Snyder's *Principles of Parallel Programming* covers the underlying concepts more thoroughly than the model-focused introductions. The book is appropriate for learners interested in the deeper foundations rather than only in specific programming models.

For introduction to high-performance computing as a broader discipline, Hager and Wellein's *Introduction to High Performance Computing for Scientists and Engineers* covers the integration of parallel programming with the architectural and performance-engineering concerns that characterize HPC. The book is unusually well-suited to learners approaching HPC from scientific and engineering backgrounds.

Hennessy and Patterson's *Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach* (mentioned in Section 2.4) is the foundational architecture reference and warrants direct engagement for HPC engineers. The book's coverage of memory hierarchies, parallelism, and accelerators is foundational, and HPC engineers consult it throughout their careers.

For numerical computing specifically, Heath's *Scientific Computing: An Introductory Survey* provides accessible introduction to the numerical methods that scientific computing depends on. Trefethen and Bau's *Numerical Linear Algebra* provides depth on the numerical linear algebra that underlies parts of HPC. Both are appropriate for learners pursuing scientific computing as part of their HPC engagement.

The reading-order recommendation: Pacheco and Malensek as the parallel programming introduction, Hager and Wellein as the HPC engineering introduction, Hennessy and Patterson as the architectural reference, and the appropriate numerical computing texts as application support.

The major project documentation — the MPI standard, the OpenMP specification, the CUDA programming guide — constitutes reference material that practitioners consult continuously.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression branches according to specialization.

*Parallel algorithm design.* For learners pursuing parallel algorithms specifically, JaJa's *An Introduction to Parallel Algorithms* provides foundational coverage. Papers on parallel complexity theory, on the work-span model, on cache-oblivious algorithms (Frigo, Leiserson, Prokop, and Ramachandran's foundational work), and on communication-avoiding algorithms (Ballard, Demmel, and collaborators) constitute the contemporary research literature. The Cormen et al. *Introduction to Algorithms* (CLRS, mentioned in Section 2.7) has parallel algorithm material in its later editions.

*Distributed-memory programming.* For MPI-based programming, Pacheco's older *Parallel Programming with MPI* (still useful) and Gropp, Lusk, and Skjellum's *Using MPI* (third edition, 2014) cover the standard. The MPI standard itself (currently version 4.0) is the authoritative reference. Papers on MPI implementation and optimization extend the picture.

*Shared-memory and OpenMP.* Chapman, Jost, and Van Der Pas's *Using OpenMP* covers the foundational material. The OpenMP specification is the authoritative reference, and the OpenMP Application Programming Interface documentation provides the contemporary reference.

*GPU programming and accelerated computing.* For CUDA-based GPU programming, NVIDIA's CUDA Programming Guide (free) is the foundational reference. Sanders and Kandrot's *CUDA by Example* provides accessible introduction, though it is now somewhat dated. Cheng, Grossman, and McKercher's *Professional CUDA C Programming* provides depth. Across GPU vendors, the ROCm documentation (AMD), the SYCL specifications, and the OpenCL documentation cover the alternatives. Kirk and Hwu's *Programming Massively Parallel Processors* (fourth edition, 2022) is the contemporary academic standard for GPU programming.

*High-performance numerical computing.* Demmel's *Applied Numerical Linear Algebra* covers the linear algebra foundations. Quarteroni, Sacco, and Saleri's *Numerical Mathematics* covers the broader numerical methods. The BLAS and LAPACK documentation, the FFTW documentation, and other foundational library documentation constitute reference material. The work on communication-avoiding algorithms (various papers from Demmel's group and others) extends the picture into the contemporary research direction.

*Performance engineering.* Bryant and O'Hallaron's *Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective* (mentioned in Section 2.4) covers the systems-level performance engineering. Brendan Gregg's *Systems Performance* (mentioned in Section 4.5) covers operational performance engineering. Papers on performance modeling — the Roofline model from Williams, Waterman, and Patterson and its various extensions — constitute contemporary literature. McKenney's *Is Parallel Programming Hard, And, If So, What Can You Do About It?* (free) provides unusual depth on parallel programming at the systems level.

*Specific application domains.* HPC has application-specific literature. Scientific computing has its various subdomains — computational fluid dynamics, computational chemistry, climate modeling — each with its own literature. Machine learning training infrastructure has its own contemporary literature: papers on distributed training, model parallelism, and training infrastructure. Financial computing has its own subdomain literature.

*Heterogeneous and exascale computing.* Contemporary work on heterogeneous and exascale computing is. Papers on the contemporary exascale systems (Frontier, Aurora, El Capitan), papers on heterogeneous programming abstractions (Kokkos, RAJA), and papers on the contemporary directions in HPC architecture constitute the contemporary research literature. The Top500 documentation and various analyses of system trends provide the broader context.

*The HPC software stack.* The foundational libraries (BLAS, LAPACK, ScaLAPACK, MAGMA, various tensor libraries, various solver libraries) constitute software infrastructure that HPC engineers work with. The documentation for these libraries, and the papers on their design and implementation, constitute literature. The build systems and package managers for HPC (Spack, EasyBuild) have their own documentation that practitioners consult routinely.

##### Reference works

The MPI standard is the authoritative reference for MPI. The OpenMP specification is the authoritative reference for OpenMP. The CUDA Programming Guide and the CUDA C++ Programming Guide are the authoritative references for CUDA. The other parallel programming standards have their own authoritative documentation.

The BLAS, LAPACK, and ScaLAPACK reference manuals constitute reference for the foundational numerical libraries.

Hennessy and Patterson's *Computer Architecture* is the foundational architecture reference and is consulted throughout HPC careers.

The Top500 list (top500.org) and its various analyses constitute reference for the state of the largest HPC systems.

The contemporary architecture documentation — for the major CPU architectures (Intel, AMD, ARM Neoverse), for the major GPU architectures (NVIDIA Hopper, AMD MI series, and others), for various AI accelerators — constitutes reference for specific hardware.

The Roofline model and the performance modeling tools (the Berkeley Performance Roofline Toolkit, and others) constitute reference for performance analysis methodology.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the HPC mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*Cluster computing versus HPC.* The contemporary cloud computing and big data communities have developed different approaches to large-scale computation than traditional HPC, with overlap but distinct emphasis. Writings comparing HPC and cluster-computing approaches are heterodox to traditional HPC framings in their argument that the traditional HPC approach is not always the appropriate one. Engaging with these critical perspectives provides perspective on HPC engineering choices.

*Functional and high-level approaches to parallelism.* Various approaches to parallel programming have argued for higher-level abstractions than the traditional MPI and OpenMP approaches: the functional parallel programming approaches (Manticore, various Haskell parallel approaches), the data-parallel approaches (NESL and its successors), the task-based approaches (Cilk, OpenMP tasks in their later forms). These represent alternative directions that have produced research but limited mainstream HPC adoption. Engagement with them provides perspective on what alternatives to the mainstream might look like.

*Performance-portability initiatives.* The contemporary performance-portability work (Kokkos, RAJA, and others) is heterodox to the traditional vendor-specific approaches in its argument that programs should be written once and run efficiently on multiple architectures. The argument is contested but has serious proponents and industrial adoption in specific contexts. Engaging with the performance-portability literature provides perspective on the alternatives to vendor-specific programming.

*The ML compilation perspective.* The contemporary ML compilation work (TVM, XLA, IREE, mentioned in Section 4.7) represents an alternative direction for high-performance computing — using compiler infrastructure to produce high-performance code rather than relying on hand-tuned libraries. The argument is that as workloads diversify, hand-tuned approaches scale poorly and compiler-based approaches are necessary. The position has support in specific domains but more limited general adoption. Engagement with the ML compilation work provides perspective on this alternative direction.

*Simplicity advocacy.* Various practitioners have argued that HPC has become over-engineered, with various sophisticated programming models, performance optimizations, and architectural complexities producing systems that are difficult to use and maintain without proportionate benefit. Engagement with this minimalist tradition provides perspective on engineering tradeoffs in HPC.

##### What to skip and why

Older books on supercomputing for systems that are no longer in use should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The HPC landscape has changed over the past decade, and the appropriate primary references address contemporary architectures.

General parallel computing books that focus on theoretical models without engaging with contemporary engineering practice should be supplemented with practitioner-oriented material rather than relied on as sole references. The discipline requires both theoretical foundation and engineering practice.

Vendor-marketing books on specific HPC products should be skipped except as practical references. The genre is uneven; the canonical academic references and the vendors' authoritative documentation are more reliable for foundational learning.

Books on parallel programming that focus exclusively on a single programming model — only MPI, only OpenMP, only CUDA — should be supplemented with broader treatments. Contemporary HPC requires fluency with multiple models, and single-model treatments produce incomplete understanding.

Books on scientific computing that focus exclusively on the mathematics without engaging with the computational implementation should be supplemented with HPC engineering treatments. Effective scientific computing requires integration of the mathematical and computational dimensions, and treatments emphasizing one without the other produce incomplete preparation.

Most online tutorials on HPC topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the project documentation, the well-maintained vendor tutorials, the talks from HPC conferences — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without the depth that HPC actually requires.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary HPC content are conferences. Supercomputing (SC) is the major event; the ISC conferences are the European equivalent; the more specialized conferences (PPoPP, PACT, ICPP, IPDPS) constitute the academic conference landscape, with proceedings available. The vendor conferences (NVIDIA's GTC, various AMD events) include HPC content.

Several blogs and online resources are useful for ongoing engagement. The HPCwire publication covers the HPC industry continuously. The Top500 analyses provide ongoing context on system trends. National laboratory publications (Oak Ridge, Argonne, Lawrence Livermore, Lawrence Berkeley) include HPC content. Practitioner blogs from prominent HPC engineers and researchers — Jack Dongarra's various writings, the blogs from leaders in specific HPC subareas — provide ongoing perspective.

National laboratory technical reports constitute contemporary literature on specific HPC topics. The reports often go into depth that journal publications do not, and they constitute reference material for practitioners working in specific areas.

Practitioner-focused podcasts (The HPC Podcast, and others) provide ongoing audio content.

Contemporary papers on machine learning training infrastructure increasingly constitute HPC literature in their own right. Papers from major industrial AI labs on training systems — the GPT training papers, the LLaMA papers, the Anthropic publications on training infrastructure, the Google publications on TPU systems — constitute contemporary HPC literature even when not framed as such.

##### Reference table

| Communication-avoiding algorithms papers (Ballard, Demmel et al.) | Contemporary research | Current canon, Sampling |
