| MacKay, *Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms* (free) | Integrated information theory and ML | Permanent canon, Reference |
| Cover, Thomas, *Elements of Information Theory* (2nd ed.) | Information theory standard | Permanent canon, Reference |
| Sutton, Barto, *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* (2nd ed.) | RL foundation (treated in 5.4) | Permanent canon, Spine |
| Bottou, Curtis, Nocedal, "Optimization Methods for Large-Scale Machine Learning" | Contemporary optimization survey | Current canon, Entry |
| scikit-learn documentation (free) | Authoritative classical ML reference | Current canon, Reference, Ongoing |
| PyTorch / TensorFlow / JAX documentation | Contemporary infrastructure reference | Current canon, Reference, Ongoing |
| Wasserman, *All of Statistics* | Frequentist alternative perspective | Permanent canon, Heterodox |
| Duda, Hart, Stork, *Pattern Classification* | Classical pattern recognition | Permanent canon, Heterodox |
| Marr, *Vision* | Cognitive science perspective | Permanent canon, Heterodox, Conceptual |
| Marcus, Davis, *Rebooting AI* | Skeptical perspective | Contemporary supplement, Heterodox |
| Hernán, Robins, *Causal Inference: What If* (free) | Causal inference standard | Current canon, Reference |
| NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR proceedings (free) | Primary research venues | Contemporary supplement, Sampling, Currency |
| arXiv (cs.LG, stat.ML) | Contemporary research repository | Contemporary supplement, Sampling, Ongoing |
| The Gradient and machine learning newsletters | Ongoing developments | Contemporary supplement, Ongoing |
| Distill (archived, free) | Historically valuable expository | Permanent canon, Conceptual |
| Major industrial AI lab publications | contemporary research | Contemporary supplement, Sampling |
| Lipton, Steinhardt, "Troubling Trends in ML Scholarship" | Methodological correction | Current canon, Entry |
| Pre-deep-learning neural network books | Mostly dated | Skip (with exceptions) |
| Industry-specific ML books | Mixed quality | Skip |
| Library-only ML books without foundations | Incomplete coverage | Skip |
| "ML without math" books | Incomplete coverage | Skip |
| Application-only ML books without foundations | Limited transferability | Skip |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Machine learning foundations is among the more contemporarily active subjects on the intelligence axis. Six developments are reshaping the discipline now.

1. **The reorientation of the field around large-scale deep learning.** The classical machine learning techniques that dominated the field through the 2000s have been displaced for the most contemporarily prominent problems by deep learning approaches, particularly for problems involving images, text, and other unstructured data. Treated in detail in Section 5.2 and Section 5.3. The classical techniques retain importance for specific contexts (tabular data, small-data regimes, problems requiring interpretability), but the contemporary attention has shifted to deep learning. The trajectory points toward continued deep learning dominance, with the foundational material in this section providing the conceptual context for engaging with the contemporary work.

2. **The increasing engagement with foundational theory.** The empirical successes of contemporary machine learning have outrun the available foundational theory, and the contemporary research includes work on developing theory adequate to the empirical phenomena — work on neural tangent kernels, work on the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent, work on generalization beyond the classical bias-variance framework, work on the mathematics of attention and transformer architectures. The trajectory points toward continued theoretical development, though whether the theory will catch up to the empirical successes remains genuinely uncertain.

3. **The maturation of methodological discipline.** The reproducibility problems that machine learning has experienced over the past decade have produced response: better methodological standards, better dataset documentation, better attention to evaluation pitfalls, other forms of methodological maturation. The trajectory points toward continued methodological development, with potential for improvement in the reliability of machine learning research and practice.

4. **The increasing engagement with safety and interpretability concerns.** The deployment of machine learning systems in consequential contexts has produced attention to whether and how the systems behave correctly, what they have learned, and how their behavior can be controlled. Treated in Sections 5.6 and 5.7. The trajectory points toward continued growth in safety and interpretability work.

5. **The increasing engagement with causal reasoning.** The limitations of purely correlational machine learning — particularly when models are deployed in contexts that differ from the contexts in which they were trained — have produced interest in causal approaches to machine learning. The trajectory points toward continued causal-inference development, with potential for influence on how machine learning is approached in consequential applications.

6. **The industrial concentration of machine learning research.** parts of the most influential contemporary machine learning research come from a small number of large industrial AI labs, with implications for the field's character. The trajectory in this area is genuinely uncertain — the role of academic research relative to industrial research, the implications of compute-driven research for the kinds of work that can be done, the ethical questions about industrial AI development — but the contemporary distribution of research activity is unusual in CS history.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of machine learning is robust. The core concerns — generalization, optimization under stochasticity, representation and inductive bias, the finite-data regime — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Machine learning foundations is the intelligence-axis subject most heavily dependent on mathematical preparation. The depth of competence is bounded by probability and statistics, linear algebra, optimization, and enough engineering practice to connect the mathematics to real systems. Practitioners who attempt machine learning without that preparation produce work limited by the gap, and the cost of the gap has grown as the field has matured.

### 5.2 — Deep Learning

#### What it is, properly

Deep learning is the discipline of neural networks of depth — multilayer networks of differentiable functions whose parameters are trained by gradient-based optimization on quantities of data. The subject treats deep neural networks as a computational paradigm with characteristic architectural patterns, characteristic training procedures, characteristic theoretical concerns, and characteristic application domains. The discipline has historical depth — the foundational ideas date from the 1940s and 1950s, with development in the 1980s and 1990s — but its contemporary prominence dates from approximately 2012, when deep learning approaches began producing dramatic empirical improvements over previous methods on benchmark problems. Subsequent development has reshaped the entire intelligence-axis region of computer science.

The conventional account treats deep learning as the practice of training neural networks — choosing an architecture, defining a loss function, running gradient descent on training data, evaluating on test data, deploying trained models. This account captures the surface activity but understates what deep learning has become as an intellectual subject. Three recognitions reshape how the discipline is approached. Computational behavior can be obtained by composing many layers of simple differentiable transformations and training the composition end-to-end on data. Architectures matter because different choices encode different inductive biases that determine what can be learned. The empirical phenomena of deep learning frequently outrun available theoretical understanding, requiring methodological discipline and humility about what is known versus what is assumed. These recognitions combine to produce a discipline with empirical successes, conceptual challenges, and practical implications across virtually every domain of computing.

The subject divides into several distinct concerns. *Architecture design* is the layer concerned with how the computational graphs of deep learning models are structured — convolutional networks for spatial data, recurrent networks for sequential data, transformers for attention-based processing, the more contemporary architectures (state-space models, mixture-of-experts, and others). The architectural choices matter: different architectures perform very differently on different problems, and the development of effective architectures is itself a subdiscipline. *Training procedures* is the layer concerned with how networks are actually trained — initialization schemes, optimizers (SGD with momentum, Adam, AdamW, and others), learning rate schedules, regularization techniques, various practical concerns. The training procedures matter in practice: networks that fail to train under one procedure may train successfully under another, and practical training knowledge is part of the field rather than mere craft. *Loss functions and objectives* is the layer concerned with what networks are actually trained to do — the variety of loss functions for classification, regression, ranking, generation, the self-supervised objectives, the contrastive and reinforcement-style objectives. *Theoretical foundations* is the subdiscipline concerned with understanding why deep learning works — the analyses of optimization in deep networks, the analyses of generalization, the work on the implicit bias of gradient-based training, the analyses of expressivity. *Practical infrastructure* is the layer concerned with the engineering of deep learning at scale — frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX), training infrastructure (various distributed training approaches, gradient accumulation, mixed-precision techniques), inference infrastructure (various optimization techniques for deployment).

The intellectual content of deep learning is distinctive in several ways. Its empirical character is unusually strong: some of what is known about deep learning come from empirical results that lack adequate theoretical explanation. Its architectural variety matters because deep learning is not a single algorithm but a space of architectures with different capabilities and limits. Its scale dependence matters because many phenomena observed at small scale do not generalize to large scale, and many phenomena observed at large scale are not visible at small scale. Its compute requirements matter because serious deep learning work often requires computational resources, and access to compute has become a determinant of what work can be done.

This account matters because deep learning has become central to contemporary computing, and the gap between informed and uninformed practice is increasingly consequential. As deep learning has moved from research curiosity to industrial deployment, the cost of weak foundations has grown: models deployed without appropriate evaluation, training pipelines that produce nominal results without real learning, and failures that proper foundations would have helped prevent. Deep learning as engineering practice differs from deep learning as casual application, and the difference is increasingly visible in production systems.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Four problems organize deep learning as a discipline. They cut across the spectrum from convolutional networks to contemporary transformers, and they recur whenever a new architecture or training procedure is being designed.

##### Representation learning

A central question deep learning addresses is how computational systems can develop useful representations of complex data — images, text, audio, other forms of unstructured data — through training rather than through explicit feature engineering. The classical machine learning approach required feature engineering: human experts designed features that captured task-relevant aspects of data, and the machine learning algorithms operated on the engineered features. This approach worked but was limited by the human expert's understanding of which features mattered.

Deep learning approaches the problem differently. Networks learn representations through training, with intermediate layers developing representations that capture aspects of the data useful for the task at hand. The intellectual content includes the analyses of what representations emerge in trained networks, the work on how architectural choices shape representations, the work on transfer of representations across tasks (representations learned on one task often prove useful for related tasks), and the work on self-supervised learning where representations are learned without explicit task supervision. The achievement is that representation learning has produced systems that handle complex data without the feature-engineering work that earlier approaches required.

##### Optimization at scale

Training deep networks involves optimizing high-dimensional non-convex objective functions through gradient-based methods, and the success of this procedure is mathematically remarkable. Classical optimization theory predicts that gradient descent on non-convex functions can become trapped in poor local optima, can take exponentially long to find good solutions, can fail in various ways that have been formally characterized. Deep network training routinely succeeds despite these theoretical concerns, finding parameter configurations that perform well on training data and that often generalize well to test data.

The discipline of deep learning theory has work attempting to explain this success. Analyses of the loss landscape suggest that the landscape may be more benign than worst-case analyses predict. Analyses of the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent suggest that the optimization procedure favors certain solutions over others in ways that aid generalization. Analyses of the role of overparameterization suggest that having more parameters than training examples may aid optimization rather than hindering it. This is one of the most active areas of contemporary theoretical work in machine learning.

##### Generalization in over-parameterized regimes

Deep networks are typically over-parameterized — they have far more parameters than training examples — and classical statistical learning theory predicts that such models should overfit catastrophically. They do not. Deep networks trained on data routinely generalize well despite having capacity that classical theory says should produce poor generalization.

This empirical observation has produced theoretical work attempting to understand it: analyses of double descent, analyses of implicit regularization from gradient-based optimization, and refinements of generalization theory that account for the specific structure of deep networks. The gap between classical generalization theory and what is observed empirically remains an open question. Contemporary work has produced new theoretical frameworks but not a theory fully adequate to the empirical phenomena.

##### Scaling

An empirical phenomenon of deep learning is that performance often improves predictably with scale — with more data, more parameters, more compute — and various scaling relationships have been characterized empirically. The work on scaling laws begins with Hestness et al. and continues through Kaplan et al. and various subsequent work. The implication is that deep learning has been driven by scale: the contemporary impressive systems are not fundamentally different from earlier systems in algorithmic terms but operate at larger scale.

The intellectual content includes the empirical work characterizing scaling relationships, the theoretical work attempting to explain them, and the engineering work required to train models at scale. The contemporary period has been shaped by this scaling phenomenon, and the industrial concentration of deep learning research reflects in part the compute requirements of frontier work.

#### Where it sits in the map

Deep learning depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational machine learning material (Section 5.1): everything in machine learning foundations is prerequisite for serious deep learning work. The probability theory, the optimization theory, the statistical learning theory, the methodological discipline — all are required. Practitioners who attempt deep learning without machine learning foundations work at a disadvantage that no amount of practical experience can fully overcome.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): mathematical preparation is foundational. Linear algebra is essential and is used more in deep learning than in classical machine learning. Calculus, particularly multivariable calculus, is foundational for understanding gradient-based optimization. Probability and statistics are foundational throughout. Optimization theory is foundational for understanding training procedures. Serious deep learning work requires enough mathematics to reason about the training process rather than merely operate a framework.

From the systems axis, the connection has grown enormously. Deep learning at scale is a systems engineering problem, and parts of contemporary deep learning practice intersect with HPC (Section 4.9), with cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.6), and with other systems-axis subjects. The engineering of training infrastructure for large models is work in its own right, and parts of the contemporary deep learning literature address engineering rather than algorithmic concerns.

From the data axis, the connection is foundational. Deep learning is fundamentally a data discipline, with data quality and quantity determining what models can achieve. This connects deep learning to database systems (Section 4.2), stream processing (Section 4.4), and data engineering.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: deep learning provides foundation for the contemporary large language model material (Section 5.3) and intersects with reinforcement learning (Section 5.4), with interpretability (Section 5.6), and with AI safety (Section 5.7). Classical AI (Section 5.5) historically preceded deep learning, with conceptual content that contemporary deep learning has absorbed.

The relationship to compilers (Section 4.7) is active. The compilation of deep learning workloads — for efficient execution on heterogeneous hardware, for inference at scale, for training at the largest scales — has produced new compiler infrastructure (TVM, XLA, IREE, and others, mentioned in Section 4.7). The intersection is one of the most active research areas at the boundary of compilers and machine learning.

Within this map, the subject is squarely on the Intelligence axis (definitionally), with engagement on the Data axis (deep learning is data-shape-determined to an unusual degree), engagement on the System axis (training and inference at scale are systems engineering problems), and moderate engagement on the Computation axis (theoretical content from optimization theory and from generalization theory).

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized deep learning acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat deep learning as a body of techniques to apply.

The most fundamental is **fluency with the standard architectures and their applications**. Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, transformers, and other architectures encode different inductive biases and suit different data and task types. Architecture fluency comes from reading the literature and implementing or using the architectures in practice.

Closely related is **the ability to train deep networks effectively**. This is practical knowledge: choice of architecture, optimizer, learning-rate schedule, regularization, and other decisions that determine whether training succeeds. It is acquired through practice rather than theoretical study alone.

A third capacity is **fluency with the deep learning frameworks**. Serious work usually requires fluency with at least one major framework, typically PyTorch and sometimes TensorFlow or JAX. Implementing architectures from papers, debugging training problems, and extending frameworks with custom components all require investment in the framework as a software system.

A fourth capacity is **the ability to read and implement contemporary deep learning research**. The literature is active, and practical work often requires implementing or adapting recent research. The durable skill is reading a paper, understanding what it does, evaluating the contribution, and reproducing the work when necessary.

A fifth capacity is **the disposition for empirical methodology**. Deep learning is empirical, with some of what is known coming from empirical observation rather than theoretical derivation, and competent deep learning practice requires methodological discipline: appropriate experimental design, appropriate baselines, appropriate statistical analysis of results, appropriate skepticism about results that are too good to be true. The methodological discipline is acquired through practice and through engagement with the methodological literature.

A sixth capacity is **the disposition to engage with scale**. Some contemporary deep learning phenomena become visible only when models, data, and training runs pass certain thresholds. Scale is therefore not incidental to the subject; it is part of what must be studied and engineered.

The final capacity is **appreciation for the empirical limits of theoretical understanding**. Contemporary deep learning works better than available theory predicts, and competent practice recognizes that gap honestly. It should not pretend to understand what is not understood or over-extrapolate from theoretical claims to empirical predictions.

#### How the field sees the world

Deep learning embeds a worldview that is unusually distinctive and that has shaped contemporary intelligence-axis thinking.

##### Representation learning over feature engineering

Representations should be learned from data rather than engineered by humans, when sufficient data is available to support the learning. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer explicit specification, that treat learned representations with skepticism because their structure is not directly traceable to specifications. The deep learning practitioner takes a different view: for problems where data is available, learned representations consistently outperform engineered ones, and the engineering effort is better directed at architecture design and training procedures than at feature engineering. The disposition has consequences for how problems are approached: rather than asking what features to extract, the deep learning practitioner asks what data and what architecture would support effective representation learning.

##### End-to-end training

Various components of a system should be trained jointly to optimize the overall objective rather than trained separately and combined. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer modular composition, that treat end-to-end training with skepticism because it produces systems whose behavior is not directly traceable to individual components. The deep learning practitioner takes a different view: end-to-end training routinely produces systems that perform better than systems composed of separately trained modules, because the joint training optimizes for the actual objective rather than for proxy objectives that the components were trained on. The disposition has consequences for system design and produces engineering different from the modular alternative.

##### Scale as real engineering concern

Many phenomena are scale-dependent; frontier work requires computational resources; the relationship between scale and capability is empirically. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat scale as configuration parameter rather than as real concern. The deep learning practitioner treats scale as integral to the discipline: as something that must be engineered for, that produces qualitatively different behavior at different scales, that determines what work is possible. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the engineering of scale as primary work rather than as auxiliary to algorithmic concerns.

##### Empirical methodology as primary epistemology

The appropriate way to understand deep learning phenomena is through empirical investigation rather than through theoretical derivation alone. This commitment is in some tension with traditional mathematical disciplines that prefer theoretical understanding, but it reflects the empirical character of contemporary deep learning: some of what is known are known empirically, and the theoretical work attempts to explain empirical observations rather than predicting them in advance. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat empirical investigation as integral to deep learning work, design experiments carefully, evaluate results rigorously, and update beliefs in response to evidence.

##### The recognition that deep learning is not fully understood

The gap between empirical successes and theoretical understanding is treated honestly. The open questions about why deep learning works as well as it does are treated as open questions rather than as solved or peripheral. This commitment runs against marketing-influenced presentations that suggest deep learning is well-understood. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the field's open questions honestly, do not claim more understanding than they have, and contribute to the development of theory rather than treating the existing theory as adequate.

##### The integration of architectural design with optimization design

The architecture and the training procedure are deeply integrated concerns. The choice of architecture affects what training procedures work, and the choice of training procedure affects what architectures train successfully. This commitment runs against the disciplinary separation that some adjacent fields practice. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat architecture and training as continuous concerns, with architectural choices made with awareness of training implications and training choices made with awareness of architectural constraints.

##### Data as determinant

What models learn is determined by what data they are trained on, that data quality and composition matter enormously, that forms of data bias and contamination produce model behaviors that no algorithmic sophistication can correct. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat data engineering as primary work rather than as preparatory to the more interesting modeling work.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning deep learning are characteristic, well-documented, and unusually consequential — the discipline's empirical character and scale dependence create many opportunities for misdirected learning.

##### Framework as discipline

Deep learning frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX) are pieces of software with APIs, and learners can come to treat learning the framework as the substance of learning deep learning. The result is practitioners who can use frameworks fluently but cannot reason about what they are doing, cannot adapt techniques to non-standard problems, cannot diagnose training failures, cannot evaluate when deep learning is or is not appropriate. The remedy is to engage with the underlying concepts seriously: to study the theoretical foundations, to understand what training is actually doing, to read research papers carefully, to recognize that framework fluency is necessary but not sufficient for deep learning competence. Framework knowledge is portable across frameworks, but conceptual depth requires the harder work of engaging with the foundations.

##### Architecture as magic

Much of deep learning practice involves choosing among architectures and configuring them — choosing whether to use a transformer or a CNN, choosing the number of layers and their dimensions, choosing other architectural choices. Learners can come to treat architectural choice as a kind of magic, with no reasoning about why specific architectures work for specific problems and no ability to evaluate whether a given architecture is appropriate. The result is practitioners who can replicate standard architectures but cannot adapt them to non-standard problems and cannot evaluate when architectural choices are appropriate. The remedy is to engage with architectural design as real engineering: to understand what inductive biases each architecture encodes, what problems each is suited to, what the tradeoffs among architectures are. The literature on architecture design (the analyses of why specific architectures work, the design papers and the subsequent analyses) provides the conceptual content this engagement requires.

##### Small-scale-only practice

Deep learning has scale dependence: phenomena visible at scale are not visible at smaller scales, and practical experience at small scale often does not extend to larger scales. Learners working only at small scales (the scales practical for individual practitioners with limited compute) can develop misunderstandings: techniques that appear effective at small scale fail at larger scale, techniques that appear ineffective at small scale prove essential at larger scale, various phenomena of scale (emergent capabilities, scaling laws, and others) are not visible. The remedy is to engage with the scale-dependent literature seriously even when one cannot replicate it directly, to recognize that small-scale experience is partial, and to maintain humility about what small-scale practice teaches about larger-scale behavior.

##### Optimization as solved

The standard deep learning training procedure (typically Adam or AdamW with appropriate learning rate schedule, applied to the transformer or other contemporary architecture) works well for parts of standard problems, and learners can come to treat optimization as a solved problem to be configured rather than understood. In practice, optimization in deep learning has subtleties: the choice of optimizer affects what the network learns, the learning rate schedule affects training dynamics, forms of regularization (weight decay, dropout, batch normalization) interact with optimization in non-obvious ways, various failure modes (vanishing/exploding gradients, mode collapse, and others) require diagnostic skill to address. The remedy is to engage with optimization as real engineering: to study the theoretical foundations, to understand what each component of the standard training procedure does, to develop the diagnostic skill required to address training failures.

##### Metric tunnel vision

Deep learning evaluation typically involves metrics — accuracy, loss, various task-specific metrics — and learners can come to optimize for metrics without engaging with what the metrics actually measure. The pattern is particularly common in research where benchmark performance is the standard measure of progress: methods that improve benchmark performance are taken as improvements, but the benchmark may not measure what users actually care about, and the apparent improvement may not transfer to real applications. The remedy is to treat metrics with skepticism: to ask what each metric actually measures, to consider what failure modes the metric does not detect, to evaluate methods qualitatively as well as quantitatively, to recognize that metric improvements are evidence about methods but not proof of methods' value.

##### The LLM shortcut applied to deep learning

Modern AI tools can produce deep learning code, can answer many deep learning questions, and can guide learners through standard exercises. The cost is unusually high in deep learning because the discipline depends on internalized intuition about what works and what does not, on extensive practical experience with training networks, on diagnostic skill that develops through hands-on engagement with failure modes. Learners who use LLMs to bypass the practical engagement do not develop these capacities, and the gap is visible when problems arise that the tools cannot address. The remedy is to do early deep learning work with deliberate engagement, even when LLM assistance could produce working code, and to use the tools as supplements to direct engagement rather than as replacements for it.

##### Reproducibility neglect

Deep learning has documented reproducibility issues, with parts of published results proving difficult or impossible to reproduce reliably. Learners who do not engage with reproducibility seriously can produce work that they believe is correct but that may not be — that depends on specific random seeds, on specific software versions, on specific data preprocessing details that are not adequately documented. The remedy is explicit attention to reproducibility: documenting training procedures completely, running experiments multiple times to characterize variation, releasing code and configuration when possible, recognizing that reproducibility is integral to honest engineering rather than optional.

##### Underestimation of data work

 deep learning success depends on data quality, data composition, and various data engineering concerns, and learners often underestimate this dimension of the work. The pattern is reinforced by the focus on architectures and training procedures in the deep learning literature, with data treated as relatively passive input rather than as real engineering concern. The remedy is to engage with data work as primary engineering: to understand where data comes from, what biases it might contain, what preprocessing is appropriate, what evaluation data should look like, how to detect data leakage and contamination. The literature on data-centric AI and on dataset documentation addresses this concern, and serious engagement with it produces practitioners more capable than those who treat data as preparation rather than as substance.

##### Overfitting to the current paradigm

The contemporary deep learning paradigm — transformers, large-scale pretraining, gradient descent at scale — is dominant, and learners can come to treat this paradigm as the entire field rather than as one moment in a developing discipline. The result is practitioners who cannot engage with earlier ideas (which may return, as ideas in AI have done before) and cannot evaluate alternatives (which become available as the field continues to evolve). The remedy is to engage with the broader history of neural networks and machine learning: to study the earlier neural network work (the 1980s connectionist tradition, the 1990s neural network work, other earlier efforts), to engage with the alternatives to deep learning (probabilistic methods, classical machine learning, various symbolic approaches), and to recognize that the contemporary paradigm is one phase in a longer-developing field rather than its terminus.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on deep learning has shifted over the past decade, reflecting both the field's empirical development and its reorientation around new architectural paradigms. The contemporary literature is unusually rich but also unusually unstable: textbooks become outdated quickly, and parts of the contemporary engagement require direct contact with research papers rather than through textbook mediation.

##### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text has been Goodfellow, Bengio, and Courville's *Deep Learning* (2016, freely available). The book covered the field as it stood at its publication, with mathematical depth and coverage of the major topics through 2016. The book remains valuable for its mathematical foundations and its coverage of pre-transformer deep learning, but it is increasingly dated for contemporary work — developments since 2016 (transformers, large-scale self-supervised learning, various contemporary architectures) are not adequately covered. The book is appropriate as a foundational reference but should be supplemented with more recent material for contemporary practice.

The contemporary alternative is Bishop and Bishop's *Deep Learning: Foundations and Concepts* (2024). The book is a revision of Bishop's earlier work, covering deep learning with mathematical depth and including contemporary material. Its publication date makes it the most current of the major foundational deep learning texts, and for learners beginning serious deep learning study now, it is the appropriate primary text.

For more accessible introduction with practical orientation, Howard and Gugger's *Deep Learning for Coders with fastai and PyTorch* (2020) and the associated fast.ai courses (freely available) provide pedagogically introduction. The approach is practical and bottom-up, in contrast to the more theoretical foundations of Goodfellow et al. and Bishop. The fast.ai materials are particularly appropriate for learners with programming background but limited mathematical preparation; they enable productive engagement with deep learning while building mathematical understanding alongside practice.

For PyTorch specifically (the dominant contemporary framework), the PyTorch documentation and tutorials are unusually well-developed and constitute reference material. Stevens, Antiga, and Viehmann's *Deep Learning with PyTorch* provides framework-focused introduction.

For learners interested in the historical and conceptual development of deep learning, the survey papers from major figures in the field (LeCun, Bengio, and Hinton's "Deep Learning" survey in *Nature*, other historical surveys) provide context. Schmidhuber's various surveys, while controversial in some specifics, provide unusually thorough historical engagement with the development of neural network ideas.

The reading-order recommendation: Bishop (2024) as the primary foundational text, supplemented with fast.ai materials for accessible practical entry, with Goodfellow et al. consulted as historical reference and for material it covers well. The PyTorch documentation as practical infrastructure reference throughout.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression branches according to specialization.

*Convolutional networks and computer vision.* For learners pursuing computer vision applications of deep learning, the foundational papers are. LeCun et al.'s LeNet papers founded the field. Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton's AlexNet paper launched the contemporary era. Various subsequent architecture papers (VGG, ResNet, and others) extended the picture. He et al.'s ResNet paper is particularly important. Szeliski's *Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications* (second edition, 2022, freely available) provides comprehensive coverage of computer vision including the deep learning era. Forsyth and Ponce's *Computer Vision: A Modern Approach* covers similar territory.

*Recurrent networks and the pre-transformer NLP era.* For historical context on recurrent networks, Schmidhuber's various papers and the LSTM literature (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber's foundational paper, various subsequent work) provide foundation. Karpathy's "The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks" essay (free) provides accessible introduction to the era. The pre-transformer NLP literature has been superseded but remains relevant for understanding what transformers replaced.

*Transformers and contemporary architectures.* Vaswani et al.'s "Attention is All You Need" paper is the foundational transformer paper. The subsequent literature on transformer variants and refinements (the efficient transformer papers, the papers on transformer scaling, the papers on transformer interpretability) constitutes the contemporary research foundation. Phuong and Hutter's "Formal Algorithms for Transformers" paper provides unusually clear technical treatment. Papers on state-space models (Mamba, and others) and on mixture-of-experts architectures extend the picture into very contemporary directions. The contemporary large language model literature is treated more in Section 5.3.

*Generative models.* The literature on generative models has multiple traditions. The variational autoencoder literature (Kingma and Welling's foundational paper, various subsequent work) covers one tradition. The generative adversarial network literature (Goodfellow et al.'s foundational paper, various subsequent work) covers another. The diffusion model literature (Ho, Jain, and Abbeel's DDPM paper, Song et al.'s score-based papers, various subsequent work) covers the contemporary dominant approach for image generation. Papers on the contemporary unification of generative modeling approaches extend the picture.

*Self-supervised learning.* The literature on self-supervised learning has reshaped parts of deep learning. Papers on contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo, and others), on masked reconstruction approaches (BERT for NLP, MAE for vision, and others), and on other self-supervised approaches constitute the contemporary literature. Chen et al.'s SimCLR paper and various BERT-style papers are foundational.

*Optimization and training procedures.* For depth on optimization, beyond the references in Section 5.1, the papers on Adam (Kingma and Ba), AdamW (Loshchilov and Hutter), other contemporary optimizers, the papers on learning rate schedules (cosine annealing, warmup, and others), the papers on initialization (He initialization, Glorot initialization, and others), and the papers on normalization (batch normalization, layer normalization, and others) constitute contemporary literature on training procedures.

*Theoretical foundations of deep learning.* The contemporary theoretical literature on deep learning is rapidly developing. Papers on neural tangent kernels (Jacot, Gabriel, Hongler's foundational paper, various subsequent work) provide one theoretical framework. Papers on the implicit bias of stochastic gradient descent provide another. Roberts, Yaida, and Hanin's *The Principles of Deep Learning Theory* (2022, freely available) provides recent treatment of theoretical foundations. Papers on double descent (Belkin, Hsu, Ma, Mandal's foundational paper, various subsequent work) cover the generalization-related phenomena.

*Engineering of deep learning at scale.* The contemporary literature on engineering deep learning at scale is essential for serious frontier work. Papers on distributed training (data parallelism, model parallelism, pipeline parallelism, various combinations), on mixed-precision training, on memory-efficient training, on gradient checkpointing — all constitute contemporary literature. Engineering blog posts from major industrial AI labs (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, FAIR, DeepMind) provide practical engineering content.

*Deep learning compilers.* The work on compilers for deep learning (TVM, XLA, IREE, and others, mentioned in Section 4.7) constitutes contemporary literature. The intersection with compilers and with HPC is increasingly.

*Domain-specific deep learning.* Deep learning has domain-specific literature. Papers on graph neural networks (Hamilton, Ying, and Leskovec, and others) cover graph-structured data. Papers on protein structure prediction (AlphaFold, various successors) cover the bioinformatics applications. Papers on deep learning for the sciences (deep learning for physics, for chemistry, for various scientific domains) cover the broader scientific applications.

##### Reference works

The PyTorch documentation is the authoritative reference for PyTorch and is unusually well-developed.

The Hugging Face documentation and the Transformers library are reference for working with transformer models, particularly large language models.

The JAX documentation covers the alternative framework that has use in research contexts.

The Papers With Code site provides reference for benchmark results and for paper-to-code mapping.

The major industrial AI lab publication archives (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind, FAIR, Google Research) constitute contemporary research reference.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the deep learning mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective.* critical engagement with deep learning provides perspective on the dominant paradigm's limitations. Marcus and Davis's *Rebooting AI* makes arguments about the limitations of contemporary deep learning. Papers from researchers who emphasize symbolic and hybrid approaches (the neuro-symbolic AI literature, and others) extend the picture. Pearl's various critical writings on deep learning's relationship to causal reasoning are similarly. The arguments are sometimes overstated but contain genuine insight that engagement with only the mainstream literature does not provide.

*The cognitive science perspective.* The connection between deep learning and cognitive science has heterodox literature. Rumelhart and McClelland's *Parallel Distributed Processing* is the foundational text for the connectionist tradition that produced contemporary deep learning, and engaging with it provides conceptual context. The contemporary work connecting deep learning to cognitive science (papers on neural network models of cognition, papers on the relationship between deep learning representations and cognitive representations) provides ongoing alternative framing.

*The neuroscience perspective.* The relationship between deep learning and biological neural networks is genuinely contested. The literature on this question — papers on cortical computation, on biological plausibility of backpropagation, on the relationship between deep network representations and cortical representations — provides perspective on what deep learning is and is not. Hassabis et al.'s "Neuroscience-Inspired Artificial Intelligence" provides one accessible synthesis.

*The classical machine learning persistence.* machine learning practice continues to use classical methods (decision trees, ensemble methods, and others) rather than deep learning, particularly for tabular data and small-data regimes. The argument behind continuing classical methods is that deep learning's apparent universal applicability is overstated and that classical methods outperform deep learning on problem categories. Papers comparing deep learning to classical methods on tabular data (the Kaggle competition analyses, the academic comparisons) make this argument with empirical support.

*The brittle generalization perspective.* work has documented the brittleness of deep learning generalization — forms of distribution shift, the adversarial examples, the out-of-distribution failures. The literature on this question (Goodfellow et al.'s adversarial examples papers, the distribution shift papers, the papers on deep learning failures) provides reason for skepticism about deep learning generalization claims.

*The interpretability perspective.* heterodox engagement with deep learning argues for attention to what models actually learn, rather than only to their outputs. The mechanistic interpretability literature (treated more in Section 5.6) constitutes heterodox correction to mainstream practice that treats models as black boxes evaluated on outputs.

##### What to skip and why

Books on neural networks that predate the contemporary deep learning era (various 1990s and early 2000s neural network books) should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice. The field has changed, and the appropriate primary references address the contemporary period.

Marketing-driven books on "AI" that conflate deep learning with broader claims about artificial intelligence should generally be skipped. The genre is uneven, and the canonical academic references are more reliable.

Most "deep learning for X" books, where X is some specific industry or application, should be approached with caution. Some are valuable, but many oversimplify deep learning by abstracting away concerns that serious practice requires.

Books on deep learning that focus exclusively on a single framework without engaging with the underlying concepts should be skipped as primary references. Framework knowledge is necessary but not sufficient; the underlying concepts are foundational.

Books on deep learning that promise quick mastery or that abstract over the mathematical content should be approached with skepticism. Deep learning has genuine mathematical content that serious practice requires engagement with, and treatments that abstract over it produce practitioners who cannot do what the discipline actually requires.

Most online tutorials on deep learning topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — well-maintained framework documentation, the fast.ai materials, the well-structured course materials from major universities, the talks from prominent researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth.

Pre-2020 books on transformer-based models are now dated and should be supplemented with current material. The transformer landscape has changed since 2020.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary deep learning research are conferences. NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR (mentioned in Section 5.1) are the primary venues, with proceedings freely available. The more specialized venues (CVPR for computer vision, ACL and EMNLP for NLP, and others) cover specific domain applications.

The arXiv repository is the contemporary publication venue for deep learning research, with parts of research appearing on arXiv before or instead of conference publication. The cs.LG, cs.CV, cs.CL, and stat.ML categories cover the major subareas.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. The Distill publication (now archived) provided unusually clear contemporary writing on deep learning topics; its archive remains valuable. The Gradient continues this tradition. Andrej Karpathy's various writings and tutorials are consistently valuable. Sebastian Raschka's writings provide ongoing technical content. Lilian Weng's blog posts provide deep technical writing on various topics. Chris Olah's writings (especially the historical Distill articles and the contemporary Anthropic interpretability work) are exceptionally valuable.

Various industrial AI lab blog posts and publications constitute contemporary literature. The Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, FAIR, and Google Research publication archives include research that would have appeared in academic conferences in earlier eras.

Various practitioner-focused YouTube channels provide ongoing video content. Yannic Kilcher's paper reviews provide ongoing coverage of new papers. Andrej Karpathy's various tutorial videos are consistently valuable. Various conference talk recordings (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR talks freely available) provide video content.

The deep learning newsletters (The Batch from DeepLearning.AI, Import AI from Jack Clark, Last Week in AI, The Algorithmic Bridge, and others) provide curated coverage of contemporary developments.

The practitioner-focused podcasts (Lex Fridman's podcast where it engages technical content, Machine Learning Street Talk, TWIML, and others) provide ongoing audio content.
