| Math-avoiding deep learning books | Incomplete coverage | Skip |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Deep learning is the most contemporarily active subject in computer science, with developments reshaping the field on timescales of months rather than years. Six developments are reshaping the discipline now.

1. **The rise of large language models.** Treated in detail in Section 5.3. The empirical successes of large language models — their general-purpose capabilities, their economic and social implications, various technical questions they raise — have shaped contemporary deep learning. The trajectory continues to develop, with uncertainty about where it will lead.

2. **The diversification of model types beyond pure language models.** Multimodal models that handle text, images, audio, and video together; vision-language models with reasoning capability; speech and audio models with generative capability; other multimodal directions all extend deep learning beyond the language-only frame. The trajectory points toward continued multimodal development, with implications for what deep learning systems can do.

3. **The emergence of state-space models and other transformer alternatives.** The transformer architecture has been dominant for several years, but work on alternatives — state-space models like Mamba, various structured state-space variants, other architectures — suggests possible architectural diversification. The trajectory in this area is genuinely uncertain: transformers may remain dominant, or the field may move toward a more mixed architectural landscape.

4. **The maturation of training and inference engineering.** The engineering required for training large models has produced new infrastructure, and the engineering required for inference at scale has similarly matured. The trajectory points toward continued engineering development, with implications for who can do frontier work and at what cost.

5. **The growth of mechanistic interpretability research.** Treated in detail in Section 5.6. The work on understanding what trained models actually do internally — at the level of individual neurons, circuits, and representations — has produced new approaches to deep learning that complement the empirical engineering tradition. The trajectory points toward continued interpretability development, with implications for how deep learning is understood and deployed.

6. **The growth of AI safety research.** Treated in detail in Section 5.7. The concerns about the safe deployment of increasingly capable AI systems have produced a research community focused on safety questions, and that work continues to expand.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of deep learning is robust. The core concerns — representation learning, optimization at scale, generalization in over-parameterized regimes, the relationship between scale and capability — remain as articulated in earlier work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Deep learning is changing quickly, but that speed should not obscure what remains durable. Architectures, tooling, and deployment practices will continue to move; the transferable preparation is mathematical fluency, experimental discipline, architectural judgment, and an understanding of scale. Learners building durable competence should study the contemporary systems without confusing them for the whole field.

### 5.3 — Large Language Models and Foundation Models

#### What it is, properly

Large language models and foundation models is the discipline of the contemporary class of deep neural networks that are pretrained on broad data at scale and then adapted — through fine-tuning, prompting, or various other techniques — to a wide range of downstream tasks. The subject treats these models as a new computational paradigm with characteristic engineering challenges, emergent behaviors, deployment patterns, and open questions. The discipline has emerged primarily over the past decade, with the transformer architecture providing the foundational technical substrate (introduced in 2017), the pretraining-then-adapting paradigm establishing itself through models like BERT and GPT (2018-2020), and the contemporary period beginning approximately with GPT-3 (2020) and continuing through the subsequent development of GPT-4, Claude, the Llama models, the Gemini models, and others.

The conventional account treats large language models as *AI chatbots* — systems that produce text in response to prompts, useful for various information and generation tasks. This account captures part of what these systems are but understates what makes them an intellectual subject. Four recognitions reshape how the subject is approached. Pretraining at scale produces general-purpose capability that emerges from the training process rather than being explicitly programmed. The relationship between training procedure and resulting capability is empirically visible but theoretically incomplete. The engineering of these systems involves technical challenges that smaller-scale machine learning does not surface. Their deployment raises questions about capability, reliability, safety, and societal consequences that the technical community is still working through. These recognitions make the subject a paradigm with intellectual content rather than merely a product category.

The subject divides into several distinct concerns. *Pretraining* is the layer concerned with the training of foundation models on broad data at scale — the data engineering, the training procedures, the architectural choices, the engineering of distributed training. *Adaptation and fine-tuning* is the layer concerned with how pretrained models are adapted to specific tasks — supervised fine-tuning, instruction tuning, forms of preference-based fine-tuning (RLHF, DPO, and others), parameter-efficient fine-tuning techniques (LoRA, and others). *Inference and deployment* is the layer concerned with running these models efficiently at scale — quantization, pruning, distillation, various inference optimization techniques, the systems engineering of serving large models. *Prompting and context engineering* is the layer concerned with how these models are used at inference time — prompt design, in-context learning, forms of prompt engineering, retrieval-augmented generation, various agentic patterns. *Evaluation and characterization* is the layer concerned with measuring what these models can and cannot do — various benchmarks, the work on model evaluation methodology, the work on characterizing model capabilities and limitations. *Alignment and safety* is the layer concerned with making these models behave usefully and safely — fine-tuning approaches that aim at desired behaviors, various interpretability approaches, the work on identifying and addressing failure modes. (This last concern is treated more in Section 5.7, but it intersects with the present section throughout.)

The intellectual content of the subject is distinctive in several ways. Its empirical character may be the strongest of any contemporary CS subject: much of what is known about these models comes from empirical investigation that lacks adequate theoretical explanation. Its scale dependence is sharper than in earlier deep learning: capabilities visible at one scale may be absent at smaller scales, and much frontier practice requires industrial resources. Its pace of development is unusually rapid, with new results appearing on monthly timescales and dominant systems changing within months or years. Its disciplinary breadth is also unusual: serious engagement requires fluency with deep learning, NLP and multimodal processing, systems engineering, evaluation methodology, and parts of the AI safety literature.

This account matters because foundation models have become central to contemporary computing and to parts of broader social and economic activity, and the gap between informed and uninformed engagement is increasingly consequential. Deployment in medical, legal, educational, financial, and other high-stakes contexts makes weak conceptual preparation costly. Foundation model engineering as serious practice differs from foundation model usage as casual application, and competent work requires preparation that marketing-influenced presentations often understate.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize large language models and foundation models as a discipline. The fifth problem reflects the field's engagement with concerns that other deep learning subfields engage with less directly.

##### Capability emergence from pretraining

An empirical phenomenon of foundation models is that capabilities emerge from large-scale pretraining that the training procedure was not specifically designed to produce. Models trained on next-token prediction develop general capabilities — reasoning, instruction following, code generation, and others — that are not directly the training objective.

The discipline includes work on understanding this phenomenon: analyses of what scaling produces, studies of the relationship between pretraining data and model capabilities, and theoretical frameworks attempting to explain capability emergence. This remains one of the central open questions of contemporary deep learning. The papers on emergent abilities document the phenomenon empirically; theoretical analyses attempt to explain it. The current state is that the phenomenon is documented but inadequately understood.

##### Adaptation from pretraining to deployment

A pretrained foundation model is rarely deployed directly; it is typically adapted through fine-tuning, instruction tuning, preference-based fine-tuning, or some combination of these to produce a model suitable for specific deployment.

The discipline of adaptation has intellectual content: the supervised fine-tuning techniques and their implications, the work on instruction tuning (the papers on Self-Instruct, on instruction-following datasets, on various adaptations), the work on preference-based fine-tuning (the original RLHF papers from OpenAI and Anthropic, various subsequent work on Direct Preference Optimization and its variants, various theoretical analyses), and the work on parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA, QLoRA, other adapter approaches). The intellectual content includes both engineering questions (what techniques work, when, with what tradeoffs) and conceptual questions (what fine-tuning actually changes about model behavior, what the relationship between pretraining and fine-tuning is, what forms of fine-tuning preserve and modify).

##### Inference at scale

Foundation models are typically large — billions to trillions of parameters — and serving them at scale presents engineering challenges.

The discipline includes work on inference optimization: quantization (developed over the past several years, with INT8 and INT4 inference now standard), pruning, distillation (various techniques for training smaller models to mimic larger ones), the caching strategies (KV cache, prefix caching, and others), the decoding optimizations (speculative decoding, and others). The intersection with systems engineering is: serving large models at scale is a systems engineering problem, with intersection with the cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.6), the HPC engineering (Section 4.9), and the compiler engineering (Section 4.7) covered elsewhere in this document. The contemporary inference frameworks (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and others) constitute engineering infrastructure that practitioners working in this area engage with regularly.

##### Evaluation and characterization

Evaluating what foundation models can and cannot do is genuinely difficult. Models exhibit capabilities on standard benchmarks but exhibit failures in real applications; they appear to perform well on tasks but fail on apparently similar tasks for non-obvious reasons; they exhibit capabilities that vary across prompting approaches in ways that complicate evaluation.

The discipline includes work on evaluation methodology: various standard benchmarks (MMLU, BigBench, and others), critiques of benchmark validity, evaluation that better captures real-world deployment performance, adversarial evaluation, and the evaluation of specific capabilities such as reasoning and coding. Evaluation matters because it determines what work is taken seriously, what improvements are recognized, what failures are detected, and where attention and effort are misallocated. Contemporary work on evaluation, including dangerous-capability evaluation by AI safety organizations, is among the more important research directions.

##### Alignment and safe behavior

Foundation models are deployed in contexts, and ensuring they behave usefully and safely in those contexts is engineering work.

The discipline includes work on what is sometimes called "alignment": fine-tuning approaches that aim at desired behaviors (helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty), techniques for reducing undesired behaviors, adversarial robustness work around jailbreaks and related failures, interpretability work (treated more in Section 5.6), and broader safety work (treated more in Section 5.7). The intellectual content is genuine because the question of how to make models behave reliably and safely remains open. Contemporary techniques such as RLHF and Constitutional AI are partial solutions with known limitations rather than complete solutions to a solved problem. Industrial investment and academic attention have made this one of the defining topics around foundation models.

#### Where it sits in the map

Large language models and foundation models depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the deep learning material (Section 5.2): everything in deep learning is prerequisite for serious foundation model work. The architectural fluency with transformers, the training procedure expertise, the optimization knowledge, the methodological discipline — all are required. Practitioners who attempt foundation model work without deep learning preparation operate at disadvantage.

From the machine learning foundations material (Section 5.1): the foundational material is similarly required, particularly the probabilistic and statistical foundations, the optimization theory, and the methodological discipline.

From the systems axis: the connection is constitutive. Foundation model work is systems engineering at scale: training infrastructure, inference infrastructure, and data infrastructure are part of the subject rather than support work outside it. The intersection with HPC (Section 4.9) is especially strong because training frontier foundation models requires HPC-scale engineering; the intersections with cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.6), stream processing (Section 4.4), and other systems-axis subjects are also important.

From the data axis: the connection is foundational. Foundation models are shaped by the data they are trained on, with pretraining datasets, code corpora, synthetic data, filtering choices, and data composition determining what models learn. Data engineering for pretraining is therefore a discipline in its own right.

From other intelligence-axis subjects: interpretability (Section 5.6) and AI safety (Section 5.7) are active points of contact. Portions of contemporary interpretability work are directed at understanding foundation models specifically, and parts of contemporary AI safety work are directed at foundation models specifically. The relationship to reinforcement learning (Section 5.4) runs through RLHF and related alignment work.

From the broader CS context: software engineering becomes increasingly relevant as foundation models become components of software systems. HCI becomes increasingly relevant as foundation models reshape what user interfaces can do. Broader societal questions — economic, ethical, regulatory — also matter, though this chapter treats them only where they directly affect technical practice.

Within this map, the subject is squarely on the Intelligence axis, with engagement across all other axes. The Data axis matters because foundation models are data-shape-determined to an unusual degree. The System axis matters because training and inference at scale are systems engineering. The Computation axis matters more as theoretical work on foundation models develops.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized large language models and foundation models acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat foundation models as products to be used rather than as systems to be understood.

The most fundamental is **fluency with the contemporary foundation model landscape**. The major model families — GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama, and open-weight families — differ in characteristic ways. Landscape fluency means knowing those differences, matching systems to work, and staying current as the field changes.

A second capacity is **the ability to evaluate foundation models seriously**. Evaluation asks whether a model is appropriate for a deployment context: what its capabilities are, what limitations it has, what failure modes are likely, and what evaluation procedures would be informative. Benchmark literature, evaluation-methodology papers, and capability-characterization work provide the conceptual content.

A third capacity is **fluency with the adaptation techniques**. Prompt engineering, in-context learning, retrieval-augmented generation, supervised fine-tuning, instruction tuning, preference-based fine-tuning, and parameter-efficient adaptation such as LoRA all serve different purposes. Practical work requires knowing when each is appropriate and what tradeoffs it brings.

A fourth capacity is **the discipline for using foundation models in production**. Production use requires evaluation, failure-mode handling, cost management, monitoring, and attention to security and safety concerns. It is engineering discipline, not just model access.

A fifth capacity is **the capacity for honest engagement with the field's open questions**. Foundation models raise open questions about capabilities, limitations, safety, and societal implications. Serious work engages these questions instead of accepting marketing-shaped confidence.

The final capacity is **appreciation for the boundary between what can be done and what should be done**. AI safety, ethics, and the broader implications of foundation model deployment belong inside engineering judgment, especially as these systems move into consequential contexts.

#### How the field sees the world

Large language models and foundation models embeds a worldview that has emerged rapidly and that is distinctive among CS subjects.

##### General-purpose pretraining as a paradigm

 pretraining on broad data produces models with general-purpose capability, and this approach has advantages over the task-specific training that earlier paradigms practiced. This commitment runs against earlier engineering dispositions that preferred task-specific systems built for specific purposes. The foundation model practitioner takes a different view: a sufficiently capable general-purpose model serves some of what task-specific systems served, with advantages in development cost, in capability transfer, and in other dimensions where general-purpose systems can be advantageous. The disposition has consequences for system architecture: rather than building specialized models for each task, the foundation model practitioner asks how a general-purpose model can be adapted to the specific task, and the question has different answers and different engineering implications.

##### Scale as constitutive

Scale — training data, parameters, compute — is constitutive of contemporary capability rather than incidental to it. This commitment is shaped by the empirical scaling phenomena: capability appears to depend on scale, with smaller models often qualitatively different from larger models in what they can and cannot do. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat scale as integral to the discipline, engineer for scale, and recognize that small-scale work has limitations as guidance for large-scale work. The commitment has consequences for who can do frontier work: the compute requirements of frontier foundation models limit frontier work to organizations with computational resources, and this concentration has implications for the field's character.

##### Empirical and methodological discipline as primary epistemology

Contemporary models are complex and poorly understood, and competent engagement requires empirical and methodological discipline. This commitment is in some tension with the marketing-influenced presentations of foundation models, which tend toward confident claims about capabilities and limited engagement with limitations. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat foundation models with empirical care: evaluating systematically, measuring rigorously, distinguishing what is known from what is assumed, treating apparent capabilities with appropriate skepticism until validated.

##### Honest engagement with uncertainty about capability

 uncertainty exists about what these models can and cannot do, and this uncertainty is integral to engineering practice rather than transient ignorance to be ignored. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat capability claims with care, design systems that handle capability uncertainty, and contribute to the development of evaluation rather than treating evaluation as solved.

##### The broader implications

The broader implications of foundation model deployment — economic, social, political, ethical — are integral to engineering practice rather than separate concern. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat broader implications as outside the scope of engineering. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with the AI safety, AI ethics, and broader policy literatures as integral to their engineering work, recognizing that foundation model engineering is consequential and that engineering decisions have implications beyond technical performance.

##### The field as incomplete

Contemporary foundation models are poorly understood, open questions remain about capabilities and limitations and behavior, and parts of contemporary practice rely on empirical observations that lack adequate theoretical explanation. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the field's incompleteness honestly, engage with the open questions, and contribute to the development of understanding rather than treating contemporary techniques as final.

##### Integrated capability, evaluation, and safety thinking

Capability development, evaluation, and safety are integrated concerns rather than separable ones. Capability without appropriate evaluation cannot be safely deployed. Evaluation without engagement with capability is shallow. Safety without engagement with capability cannot accomplish its goals. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat these concerns as continuous activities, integrate them in their work, and produce engineering that addresses them coherently rather than separately.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning large language models and foundation models are characteristic, well-documented, and unusually consequential — the discipline's empirical character, its rapid pace, and the marketing-influenced surrounding discourse create many opportunities for misdirected learning.

##### API as discipline

The most common pitfall is the API-as-discipline misconception. Foundation models are most commonly accessed through APIs — the OpenAI API, the Anthropic API, and others — and learners can come to treat learning the APIs as the substance of learning foundation models. The result is practitioners who can call APIs but cannot reason about what models are doing, cannot evaluate whether models are appropriate for specific tasks, cannot diagnose failures, cannot adapt approaches when default APIs do not suffice. The remedy is to engage with the underlying technical content seriously: to study transformer architectures, to understand pretraining and fine-tuning, to engage with the research literature, to recognize that API fluency is necessary but not sufficient for foundation model competence.

##### Prompt engineering as discipline

Some contemporary foundation model practice involves crafting prompts to elicit desired behaviors from models, and learners can come to treat prompt engineering as the substance of foundation model work. In practice, prompt engineering is one technique among many, and work requires engagement with deeper concerns: when prompting suffices versus when fine-tuning is required, when retrieval-augmented generation is appropriate, when other adaptation approaches work better, when foundation models are appropriate at all. The remedy is to treat prompt engineering as one element in a broader toolkit, to engage with the alternatives and complementary approaches, and to recognize that prompt-only engagement limits what work can be done.

##### Capability overestimation

Foundation models exhibit capabilities, and learners can come to overestimate what they can do — believing claims about capabilities without verification, applying models to tasks they cannot reliably handle, treating model outputs with insufficient skepticism. The pattern is reinforced by marketing-influenced presentations that emphasize capabilities and understate limitations. The remedy is empirical engagement: actually testing models on the specific tasks of interest, with appropriate evaluation methodology, before relying on them. Models that appear to handle tasks may handle them unreliably, may handle them well in some cases and fail in others, may produce outputs that look correct but are systematically wrong. The discipline of empirical evaluation is the antidote to capability overestimation.

##### Capability underestimation

The opposite pattern — dismissing foundation models as fundamentally limited, treating their failures as evidence that they cannot do serious work — is also common. The pattern is reinforced by skeptical critics who emphasize failures and understate capabilities. In practice, contemporary foundation models can do work, and dismissing them produces engineering that fails to use available tools effectively. The remedy is the same as for overestimation: empirical engagement, with appropriate evaluation methodology, to establish what models can and cannot do for the specific tasks of interest. The discipline produces calibrated assessment that neither overestimates nor underestimates capability.

##### Hallucination as aberration

Foundation models routinely produce outputs that are confidently wrong — the phenomenon commonly called *hallucination* — and learners often treat this as an aberration to be eliminated rather than as a fundamental characteristic of the technology. In practice, hallucination is integral to how these models work: they produce outputs based on patterns in training data without grounding in factual reality, and various techniques for reducing hallucination (RAG, fine-tuning, and others) reduce frequency without eliminating the phenomenon. The remedy is to engage with hallucination as a fundamental characteristic to be designed around rather than as a bug to be fixed: to build systems that handle hallucination, that verify model outputs when verification matters, that present model outputs with appropriate uncertainty.

##### Benchmark-driven understanding

Foundation models are evaluated extensively on benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, and others), and learners can come to treat benchmark performance as a complete measure of model capability. In practice, benchmarks measure specific capabilities under specific conditions, and parts of real-world performance are not captured by standard benchmarks. The remedy is to engage with benchmarks critically: to ask what specific capabilities each benchmark measures, what failure modes the benchmarks do not detect, what real-world capabilities differ from benchmark capabilities. The contemporary literature on benchmark validity (various papers documenting benchmark contamination, various papers on benchmark gaming, various papers on evaluation that better captures deployment performance) provides conceptual content for this critical engagement.

##### Closed-source-only practice

Some contemporary foundation model practice operates exclusively with proprietary models accessed through APIs, without engagement with the open-weight alternatives. This pattern produces limitations: practitioners cannot examine model internals, cannot fine-tune in non-standard ways, cannot study the research questions that require model access, cannot engage with the open-source ecosystem. The remedy is to engage with open-weight models alongside proprietary ones: to use Llama, Mistral, other open-weight families, to engage with the open-source tooling (Hugging Face Transformers, various inference frameworks), to work with model internals when the work requires it. The open-source ecosystem provides capability that closed-source-only practice misses.

##### Retrieval-augmented generation as solved problem

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) — the technique of augmenting model context with retrieved information from a knowledge base — has been marketed as solving various limitations of foundation models, particularly the hallucination problem. In practice, RAG helps but does not solve the underlying problems: models can still hallucinate within retrieved context, retrieval quality affects results, various engineering challenges of RAG (chunking, embedding, retrieval quality, and others) are. The remedy is to treat RAG as a technique with engineering depth rather than as a finished solution: to engage with the RAG literature, to understand what RAG does and does not provide, to engineer RAG systems with appropriate care.

##### Safety as someone else's problem

Foundation models have safety implications, and engineers building applications with foundation models often treat safety as a concern of the foundation model providers rather than of the application engineers. This disposition produces applications that fail at safety even when the underlying foundation models are reasonably aligned: applications that ask models to do things outside their reliable capability, that present model outputs as authoritative when they are not, that exhibit failure modes that the foundation model providers cannot anticipate without seeing the application. The remedy is to take application-level safety seriously: to engage with the AI safety literature, to evaluate applications for safety as well as capability, to recognize that foundation model providers' alignment work is necessary but not sufficient for application safety.

##### Rapid-obsolescence-induced paralysis

The foundation model field develops rapidly, with new models, techniques, and approaches appearing on monthly timescales. Learners can come to feel that any specific knowledge will be obsolete soon and that learning the field is therefore not worthwhile. The remedy is to recognize what is and is not durable in the field: the foundational understanding (transformers, pretraining, fine-tuning, evaluation methodology) is durable, while the specific systems and benchmarks evolve quickly. Investing in the durable foundations and maintaining currency through ongoing engagement with contemporary sources is more productive than either treating contemporary specifics as durable or dismissing the field as too fast-moving to engage with.

##### LLM as teacher overuse

An eleventh pitfall, applicable specifically to this subject, is LLM-as-teacher overuse. Foundation models can be used to learn about foundation models — answering questions, explaining concepts, providing examples — and learners can come to rely on this self-referential learning. The cost is serious: the models' explanations of their own behavior are not necessarily reliable (the literature on this question documents failures), the models can produce confident explanations of phenomena they do not actually understand, the models cannot teach the empirical methodology and the diagnostic skill that hands-on engagement develops. The remedy is to use foundation models as supplements to serious engagement with primary sources rather than as replacements for that engagement, particularly for foundational understanding.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on large language models and foundation models is unusually rapid-moving, with new papers appearing weekly and the dominant systems changing within timeframes of months to years. The literature is also diverse: research papers from academic and industrial sources, technical reports from major AI labs, blog posts and informal writing from practitioners, evaluations and benchmarks, other forms. Engagement with the field requires fluency with this diverse literature.

##### Canonical entry points

The standard foundational text is currently emerging — no single book has yet established itself as the canonical introduction to foundation models with the authority that earlier books achieved for earlier deep learning material. Several efforts are in progress, and the situation is likely to clarify in coming years.

The most authoritative current introduction is the Stanford CS336 / Center for Research on Foundation Models materials (freely available). The "On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models" report (Bommasani et al., 2021) provided the articulation of the foundation model framing. The course materials and lecture notes from major universities (Stanford, MIT, CMU, Berkeley, and others) provide pedagogical material on foundation models, and these materials are frequently updated to reflect the rapidly evolving field.

For learners with deep learning preparation, the foundational research papers are the appropriate primary literature. The Vaswani et al. transformer paper (mentioned in Section 5.2) is foundational. The scaling laws papers (Kaplan et al. 2020, Hoffmann et al. 2022 on Chinchilla) are. The GPT-3 paper (Brown et al. 2020) is foundational for the contemporary era. The InstructGPT paper (Ouyang et al. 2022) and various subsequent RLHF papers establish the alignment-focused fine-tuning paradigm. The Constitutional AI paper (Bai et al. 2022) covers Anthropic's approach.

For practical introduction, Howard's various subsequent fast.ai materials covering foundation models are useful. The Hugging Face course (free) covers practical foundation model work with the dominant open-source ecosystem. Various tutorials from major AI labs (OpenAI's documentation, Anthropic's documentation, and others) provide practical introduction to specific systems.

For learners interested in the broader foundation model context, Russell and Norvig's *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* (fourth edition, 2020) provides coverage of AI broadly, with the more recent material covering foundation models. The book is comprehensive across AI rather than focused on foundation models specifically, but it provides context.

The reading-order recommendation: the foundational research papers (transformer, scaling laws, GPT-3, InstructGPT) as primary literature, supplemented with course materials from major universities for pedagogical structure, with the Hugging Face course and similar practical materials for hands-on engagement.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression branches according to specialization.

*Pretraining at depth.* For learners pursuing pretraining specifically, the literature is broad and fast-moving. Papers on pretraining data engineering (papers on Common Crawl filtering, on data quality, on data composition) cover the data engineering. Papers on training infrastructure (Megatron-LM, DeepSpeed, and others) cover the systems engineering. Various scaling laws papers establish the empirical relationships. Papers on training stability and on various practical concerns (gradient clipping, learning rate schedules at scale, and others) cover the practical engineering. The industrial AI lab publications (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI, Google DeepMind) provide practitioner literature on pretraining.

*Fine-tuning and alignment.* The literature on fine-tuning has depth. The InstructGPT paper and the original RLHF work establish the foundation. The Constitutional AI paper covers an alternative approach. The Direct Preference Optimization paper (Rafailov et al. 2023) and various subsequent variants cover the more recent approaches that simplify the RLHF pipeline. Papers on Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF) and on other variants extend the picture. The parameter-efficient fine-tuning literature (the LoRA paper from Hu et al. 2021, the QLoRA paper from Dettmers et al. 2023, various subsequent variants) covers efficient adaptation. The Hugging Face PEFT library documentation provides practical reference.

*Inference at depth.* The literature on inference optimization for foundation models has depth. The quantization literature (papers on INT8 quantization, the GPTQ paper, the AWQ paper, various subsequent work) covers numerical optimization. Papers on speculative decoding (Leviathan et al., Chen et al.'s contemporary work) cover decoding optimization. Papers on attention optimization (Flash Attention from Dao et al., other papers) cover the attention computation. The vLLM paper and documentation, the TensorRT-LLM documentation, and other inference framework documentation cover the systems engineering.

*Evaluation at depth.* The evaluation literature is developing rapidly. The standard benchmark papers (MMLU, HumanEval, and others) establish the contemporary evaluation infrastructure. Papers on benchmark validity and evaluation methodology cover benchmark contamination, benchmark gaming, and the ways benchmarks can mislead. Papers on capability-specific evaluation cover reasoning, coding, mathematical performance, and other specific capabilities. Papers on adversarial evaluation and red-teaming cover safety-relevant evaluation. The contemporary work on dangerous capability evaluation from AI safety organizations is increasingly important.

*Multimodal foundation models.* The literature on multimodal foundation models has depth. The CLIP paper (Radford et al. 2021) is foundational for vision-language work. Papers on multimodal models (Flamingo from DeepMind, various GPT-4V style papers, the multimodal Llama papers, various Anthropic and Google multimodal work) cover the contemporary multimodal landscape. The audio and speech work (Whisper, and others) extends the multimodal picture. Work on video understanding and generation covers the growing video dimension.

*Code generation and code models.* The literature on code-generating foundation models has its own depth. The Codex paper (Chen et al. 2021) is foundational. Various subsequent code model papers (the StarCoder papers, the Code Llama papers, and others) extend the picture. The empirical work on coding capabilities and limitations (the HumanEval evaluations, papers on coding evaluation methodology, papers on real-world coding capability) covers what these models can and cannot do.

*Retrieval-augmented generation.* The RAG literature has emerged rapidly. The original RAG paper (Lewis et al. 2020) established the framework. Various subsequent papers on retrieval techniques, on chunking strategies, on various engineering choices, on evaluation of RAG systems constitute contemporary literature. The practitioner writings on RAG (blog posts from organizations operating RAG systems) provide engineering content.

*Agentic and multi-step systems.* The contemporary work on agentic systems built on foundation models is rapidly developing. Papers on tool use (the original tool use papers, various subsequent work), on multi-step reasoning (chain-of-thought, various subsequent variants), on agent architectures (various papers on ReAct, on various agent frameworks) constitute the contemporary literature. The practitioner literature on agent engineering is growing rapidly.

*Theoretical foundations.* The theoretical literature on foundation models is rapidly developing. Papers on in-context learning theory, scaling phenomena, and interpretability-related theory constitute contemporary literature. The intersection with the broader deep learning theory mentioned in Section 5.2 is direct.

*Safety and alignment.* The alignment literature is treated in detail in Section 5.7 but intersects with foundation model work. Papers on jailbreaking and on adversarial robustness, on forms of model behavior modification, on the capability and safety evaluation work constitute contemporary literature relevant to this section.

##### Reference works

The Hugging Face documentation and the Transformers library are the reference for working with open-weight foundation models. The documentation is unusually well-developed and constitutes reference material that practitioners consult continuously.

The OpenAI API documentation, the Anthropic API documentation, the Google API documentation, and other proprietary API documentation constitute reference for working with the major commercial foundation models.

Model cards and technical reports from major models (the GPT-4 technical report, various Claude model cards, the Gemini technical reports, the Llama technical reports) constitute reference material on specific systems.

The Papers With Code site (mentioned in earlier sections) provides reference for benchmark results.

Contemporary survey papers attempt to map the rapidly developing field. These surveys age quickly but provide useful synthesis at points in time.

The arXiv repository, particularly the cs.CL category, is the contemporary publication venue and constitutes ongoing reference for practitioners working at the frontier.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives to the foundation model mainstream and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective on foundation model capabilities.* critical engagement with foundation models provides perspective on what these systems can and cannot reliably do. Papers from researchers like Bender, Gebru, and others raise concerns about claimed capabilities. Papers documenting failure modes of foundation models (papers on hallucination characterization, on forms of brittle behavior, on forms of distribution-sensitive failure) constitute heterodox literature. Marcus and Davis's continued critical writing provides ongoing skeptical perspective.

*The classical NLP perspective.* The classical NLP literature that preceded the foundation model era covers techniques and approaches that contemporary literature sometimes treats too briefly. Manning and Schütze's *Foundations of Statistical Natural Language Processing* and Jurafsky and Martin's *Speech and Language Processing* (third edition, freely available) provide coverage of pre-foundation-model NLP. Engagement with this tradition gives perspective on what foundation models have replaced and what classical approaches still offer.

*The symbolic AI tradition.* The symbolic AI tradition (treated more in Section 5.5) represents an alternative to the foundation model paradigm. Engagement with classical AI gives perspective on what foundation models do and do not provide, and on what alternative approaches to intelligence might look like. The contemporary neuro-symbolic approaches that combine symbolic and neural methods provide hybrid perspective.

*The cognitive science perspective on language models.* The cognitive science engagement with language models provides perspective on the relationship between these systems and human language understanding. Papers on the cognitive plausibility of language models, on what they can and cannot model about human language processing, on the differences between language model behavior and human behavior, constitute heterodox literature.

*The retrieval-and-symbolic-reasoning emphasis.* work argues that the limitations of foundation models can be addressed by combining them with retrieval systems and with symbolic reasoning components, rather than by scaling foundation models alone. Papers in this tradition (Karpas et al.'s work, various papers on Toolformer-style approaches, the papers on symbolic-neural hybrid systems) constitute alternative direction.

*The AI safety critical perspectives.* The AI safety literature includes critical engagement with contemporary foundation model deployment. Papers from AI safety researchers (treated more in Section 5.7) raise concerns about contemporary practice. Engagement with this literature gives perspective on what concerns are real and what mitigations are available.

##### What to skip and why

Marketing-influenced books on "AI" or "ChatGPT" or similar that conflate foundation models with broader claims about artificial intelligence should generally be skipped. The genre is uneven, and the canonical research literature is more reliable.

Most introductory books on prompt engineering should be approached with caution. Some are valuable, but many treat prompt engineering as a more formal discipline than it is, with parts of their content being heuristics that age quickly.

Books on specific foundation model APIs (OpenAI API, and others) should be approached as practical references rather than as foundational learning. The APIs evolve, and the books about them age quickly.

"Generative AI for Business" or similar industry-focused books should be skipped except as practical references. The genre exists and is uneven.

Books on foundation models published before 2023 are now dated and should be supplemented with current material. The field has changed since 2023, and pre-2023 books may cover important foundational material but do not adequately cover contemporary practice.

Most online tutorials on foundation models are uneven in quality. The exceptions — well-maintained materials from major universities, the Hugging Face course, well-developed framework documentation, talks from prominent researchers — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory material without depth and often perpetuates misunderstandings rather than addressing them.

Books that promise to teach *how AI thinks* or that anthropomorphize foundation models should be approached with skepticism. The science of what foundation models actually do internally is incomplete (the interpretability work in Section 5.6 is the relevant literature), and confident claims about model internal processes that lack interpretability grounding are typically not reliable.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary foundation model research are conferences. NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR are the primary venues. ACL, EMNLP, and various NLP-specific venues cover language model work specifically. The more specialized workshops at these conferences (the alignment workshops, the interpretability workshops, and others) cover specific subareas.

The arXiv repository is the contemporary publication venue. The cs.CL, cs.LG, and cs.AI categories cover the major subareas. Some foundation model research appears on arXiv before or instead of conference publication.

The major industrial AI lab publications constitute contemporary research literature. Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta AI (FAIR), Google DeepMind, and Microsoft Research all publish work on foundation models, often work that would have appeared in academic conferences in earlier eras. These labs' technical reports and blog posts frequently contain research content.

Several blogs and online resources are valuable for ongoing engagement. Various AI lab blog posts (Anthropic's research and blog, OpenAI's research and blog, and others) provide contemporary content. Various practitioner blogs (Sebastian Raschka's writing, Lilian Weng's deep technical posts, Andrej Karpathy's various writings, Simon Willison's blog, and others) provide ongoing perspective.

Various AI newsletters (Import AI from Jack Clark, The Batch, AI Snake Oil, Last Week in AI, and others) provide curated coverage. The Import AI newsletter is particularly and consistently valuable.

Various practitioner-focused podcasts and YouTube channels provide ongoing audio and video content. Lex Fridman's interviews with foundation model researchers often contain technical content. The conference talk recordings (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ACL, EMNLP talks freely available) provide content.

Model cards, technical reports, and system cards from major foundation models are contemporary reference. Reading these systematically as new models are released gives ongoing engagement with the contemporary field.

The AI safety organization publications (from Anthropic's safety team, from MIRI, from various academic AI safety groups, from CSER, from FHI, from others) constitute contemporary literature relevant to foundation models.
