| Anthropomorphizing books | Often unreliable | Skip (with caution) |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

AI safety and alignment is among the most contemporarily active subjects on the intelligence axis, with developments reshaping the discipline on timescales of months.

1. **The deployment of foundation models in increasingly consequential contexts and the corresponding growth of AI safety as both a research field and a practical concern.** The scaling of contemporary models, the growth of agentic AI systems, the deployment in application areas — all have elevated the practical importance of AI safety work. The trajectory suggests continued growth in practical importance.

2. **The maturation of dangerous capability evaluation as a discipline.** The work over 2023-2025 on evaluating frontier models for potentially concerning capabilities — biological capabilities, cyberweapon capabilities, forms of autonomous action capabilities — has produced methodological development. Various AI safety organizations (METR, Apollo Research, various government organizations) have developed evaluation methodology that did not previously exist. The trajectory suggests continued development in this area, with implications for how frontier models are evaluated before deployment.

3. **The growth of regulatory and policy engagement.** The EU AI Act has come into force; various US executive orders and proposed legislation have developed; the UK AI Safety Institute and similar government organizations have developed; various international AI governance discussions have advanced. The trajectory suggests continued regulatory development, with implications for how AI development proceeds.

4. **The maturation of AI safety as a research field.** The growth in the number of AI safety researchers, published research, methodological development, and diversity of approaches has advanced the field. The integration of AI safety with mechanistic interpretability has been especially important.

5. **The growing concern about AI agents.** The development of contemporary AI systems that take actions in the world (using tools, executing code, communicating with external services, other agentic capabilities) has produced new safety concerns. The work on agent safety, on forms of action authorization, on the deployment concerns specific to agents constitutes contemporary research. The trajectory suggests continued agent development, with continued safety implications.

6. **The increasing concern about deceptive alignment and related inner alignment problems.** The work on whether contemporary models exhibit, or could come to exhibit, deceptive behavior, hidden goals, or other concerning internal properties has advanced. The intersection with mechanistic interpretability is central here, particularly as model capabilities continue to increase.

7. **The growth of AI safety organizations beyond the industrial AI labs.** METR, Apollo Research, the number of academic AI safety groups, the nonprofit AI safety organizations have all grown. The diversification of AI safety research beyond industrial labs is important and ongoing. The trajectory suggests continued growth and continued diversification.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of AI safety is robust. The core concerns — specification, evaluation, robustness, scalable oversight, alignment as capabilities grow — remain as articulated in earlier work. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

AI safety is changing quickly, but durable competence depends on foundations: evaluation methodology, critical thinking about contested questions, and the integration of technical and conceptual work. The field is unusual because engineering decisions affect deployment contexts, institutional behavior, and broader social consequences. It is also genuinely plural: disagreement about priorities and assumptions reflects the difficulty of the problems rather than simple confusion. Practitioners should engage multiple perspectives, maintain humility about open questions, and recognize that many safety problems require coordination beyond any individual contribution.

## Chapter 6 — Applied and Integrative Fields

This chapter covers fields whose intellectual content comes from combining several kinds of CS knowledge in concrete domains. Web and application development, computer graphics and games, security engineering, human-computer interaction, robotics, and scientific computing are not a single axis of the field. They are applied and integrative regions: each combines formal reasoning, system design, data representation, learned behavior, user context, physical constraints, or domain knowledge in its own way.

This matters because applied CS fields are often underestimated. Their difficulty is not always that one central theory is hard; it is that many concerns have to be held together. Web development combines networking, databases, user interfaces, security, deployment, and increasingly AI integration. Security engineering combines cryptography, systems, adversarial thinking, privacy, and organizational practice. Robotics combines mathematics, control, perception, embedded systems, physical interaction, and learning. Scientific computing combines numerical methods, software engineering, high-performance computing, and the scientific domain being studied. The integration itself is part of the discipline.

The chapter also has a different character because the rate of change differs across the application domains. Some — notably web development and the contemporary AI-augmented version of application development — change on timescales of months to years, with parts of the canonical references aging quickly. Others — security engineering, scientific computing — have cores that remain stable across decades while the surface details evolve more rapidly. The treatments accordingly emphasize the durable conceptual content alongside the more time-sensitive specifics, with the recognition that the time-sensitive material requires ongoing refresh.

A note on the relationship between application development and software engineering as a profession. The chapter covers application development as the intellectual discipline of engineering applications in specific domains; it does not cover software engineering as a profession (the practices of code review, agile methodology, on-call rotation, and others) which is a different subject treated in different literature. The two subjects overlap, but they are distinct, and the chapter focuses on the application-domain intellectual content rather than on the broader professional practices.

A further note on the contemporary moment. The deployment of foundation models has affected parts of application development. Web development now involves AI-augmented development tools and AI-integrated applications. Security engineering now involves AI threats and AI-assisted defense. Human-computer interaction now involves AI-mediated interaction patterns. Each of these intersections is important, and the chapter treats them where they appear rather than relegating them to a separate discussion. The contemporary moment is not a separate concern from the application disciplines but is integral to their contemporary practice.

The chapter does not prescribe an order in which the application domains should be studied. The domains are largely independent of each other: a learner can pursue web development without engaging with robotics, can pursue security engineering without engaging with graphics, can pursue scientific computing without engaging with HCI. The choice depends on the learner's specific interests and the specific work they expect to engage with. A reasonable order is the one used here, which proceeds from the most general application domains (web and application development, which most CS practitioners encounter regardless of specialization) through the more specialized domains. Other orders work; the conceptual relations remain the same.

A reader pursuing application development as primary specialization typically focuses on one or two domains rather than engaging with all of them. The domain knowledge each application area requires makes broad coverage impractical, and competent practitioners typically develop depth in specific areas rather than shallow coverage of many. The chapter is written to support both focused specialization and broader survey reading, with each section self-contained.

A final note: among all the chapters of this document, this chapter is most likely to feel incomplete to specialists in any specific application domain. Each domain has literature, communities of practice, and accumulated wisdom that no single section can adequately convey. The treatments aim at providing orientation to the discipline — sufficient context for productive engagement with the more specialized literature — rather than at providing comprehensive coverage. Specialists in any domain will find the treatment of their domain incomplete, and this is appropriate. The function of this chapter is to provide the orientation; the depth is in the literatures the chapter points to.

### 6.1 — Web and Application Development

#### What it is, properly

Web and application development is the discipline concerned with constructing the software applications that operate over networks — websites, web services, mobile applications, forms of cloud-deployed software — that constitute the majority of contemporary software engineering work. The subject treats application development as the integration of concerns across multiple regions of computer science: client-side engineering (browsers, mobile platforms, various rendering and interaction concerns), server-side engineering (various backend frameworks, request handling, the concurrency concerns), data engineering (database integration, forms of data flow, caching, various performance concerns), networking (various protocols, the concerns of operating over unreliable networks), security (the security concerns of network-deployed applications), and increasingly intelligence (the contemporary integration of foundation models into applications). The discipline has historical depth, with development from the 1990s onward as the web platform emerged, growth through the web 2.0 era of dynamic applications, subsequent development through the mobile era, and contemporary development driven by the deployment of cloud infrastructure and the integration of AI capabilities.

The conventional account treats web development as essentially the practice of building websites — choosing frameworks, writing code that handles HTTP requests, integrating with databases, deploying to hosting platforms. This account captures the surface activity but understates what web and application development has become. The conceptual core is the recognition that * application development integrates concerns from across computer science*, that *the complexity of contemporary applications arises from the integration rather than from any single concern*, that *the engineering practices of application development have intellectual content beyond the surface mechanics of writing application code*, and that *the deployment context of contemporary applications — cloud infrastructure, distributed users, the security and privacy concerns — shapes what application development actually requires*. These recognitions distinguish application engineering from the surface mechanics that the introductory literature emphasizes.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Frontend engineering* covers the work of building user interfaces that operate in browsers and on mobile devices — the JavaScript and TypeScript ecosystems, various rendering frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte, and others), the concerns of state management, various performance and accessibility concerns, the work on responsive and adaptive design. *Backend engineering* covers the work of building server-side application logic — various backend frameworks across multiple languages (Node.js, Python frameworks, Ruby on Rails, various Go and Rust frameworks, various Java frameworks), the work on request handling, the concerns of data persistence and integration. *API design and engineering* covers the work of designing the interfaces between application components — REST as the dominant style for parts of contemporary practice, GraphQL as an alternative with adoption, various RPC styles, the work on API versioning and evolution. *Database integration* covers the work of connecting applications to data stores — overlapping with the database material in Sections 2.8 and 4.2 but with specific concerns for application development including ORM design, query optimization in application context, forms of caching. *Deployment and infrastructure* covers the work of deploying applications to production — overlapping with the cloud-native infrastructure material in Section 4.6 but with application-specific concerns. *Security in application development* covers the application-specific security concerns — input validation, authentication and authorization, the work on common vulnerability classes, various concerns of secure communication. *Performance engineering* covers the work of making applications perform adequately at scale — forms of caching, the concerns of database query optimization, various frontend performance concerns, the work on observability for application performance. *Accessibility and internationalization* cover the work of making applications usable by diverse populations across languages and abilities. *Mobile-specific concerns* cover the differences in mobile application development — platform-specific concerns (iOS, Android), various cross-platform approaches, the concerns of offline operation and intermittent connectivity. *AI integration in applications* covers the contemporary work on integrating foundation model capabilities into applications — the engineering practices that have emerged over the past several years.

The intellectual content of application development is distinctive because it is broad, fast-moving, and empirical. Many CS subjects come into play in application work, and integrating them is itself an intellectual task. Frameworks, tools, and practices change on the scale of years rather than decades, so canonical knowledge looks different here than in more stable subjects. Effective practice also depends on production experience: what works under real users, real deployments, and real organizational constraints often differs from what abstract reasoning alone would suggest. Application development overlaps with software engineering as a profession, but the emphasis is different: application development concerns the construction of specific applications, while software engineering concerns the broader practices of software organizations.

The reason this account matters is that application development constitutes parts of contemporary software work, and the gap between informed practice and uninformed practice is large. The deployment of applications in consequential contexts produces demand for engineering competence that goes beyond the surface mechanics that introductory materials emphasize. The discipline of application development as serious engineering practice differs from application development as collection of framework knowledge, and competent work requires preparation that the framework-tutorial-driven introductory ecosystem often understates.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize web and application development as a discipline.

**The problem of integrating across complexity.** Contemporary applications are integrative artifacts — combining frontend engineering, backend engineering, data persistence, networking, security, deployment infrastructure, monitoring, and other concerns. Each of these is in its own right, and the difficulty arises in integrating them coherently. The discipline includes work on this problem: the architectural patterns that organize the integration (various MVC variants, various clean-architecture patterns, various hexagonal architecture work, various contemporary patterns), the work on dependency management and on managing the complexity of contemporary applications, the work on building applications that remain comprehensible as they grow. The intellectual content includes both architectural questions (how to organize applications coherently) and practical questions (how various concerns interact, what trade-offs arise in the integration). The contemporary work on application architecture continues to develop, with debate about which patterns work best for which application categories.

**The problem of operating over unreliable networks.** Web and mobile applications operate over networks that are unreliable — connections drop, latency varies, bandwidth is limited in various contexts, various failure modes of distributed systems apply. The difficulty has implications for how applications must be engineered. The discipline includes work on this problem: the work on offline-first design, on optimistic UI patterns, on forms of request retry and idempotency, on the concerns of state synchronization across unreliable networks, on various caching strategies that operate at multiple layers. The intellectual content includes both protocol-level questions (treated in Section 2.6) and application-level questions about how to design applications that handle network unreliability gracefully. The contemporary work on this problem includes work on various offline-first and local-first approaches, on the CRDT (conflict-free replicated data types) work for synchronizing state, and on other approaches to operating over unreliable networks.

**The problem of -scale concurrency.** Contemporary applications handle concurrent users — websites handle thousands to millions of concurrent users, with implications for how applications must be engineered to handle concurrency correctly and efficiently. The difficulty has implications across the full application stack: the concerns of request handling under concurrency, the database concurrency concerns, the caching concerns, the work on various asynchronous programming patterns. The discipline includes work on this problem: various server-side concurrency models (thread-per-request, event-driven, async/await, and others), the work on database transaction handling under concurrency, the work on caching to reduce concurrency demands on backend systems, forms of horizontal scaling. The contemporary work on this problem includes the development of various asynchronous programming models in major languages, the work on forms of serverless computing that abstract concurrency concerns, the work on edge computing approaches.

**The problem of security in deployed contexts.** Web applications are deployed in adversarial environments — exposed to malicious traffic, handling sensitive data, integrating with external services that may be compromised. The security concerns are enough to constitute a subdiscipline (treated more in Section 6.3). For application development specifically, the security concerns include the common vulnerability classes (injection attacks, forms of XSS, CSRF, various authentication and authorization vulnerabilities, the supply chain security concerns), the work on secure-by-design application engineering, the work on various security headers and configurations that contemporary web applications require. The intellectual content includes both vulnerability-specific knowledge and broader engineering practices for secure application development.

**The problem of evolving applications over time.** Applications change over time — features are added, requirements change, dependencies update, the codebase evolves through maintenance over periods. The difficulty of evolving applications without degradation is among the central practical concerns of application engineering. The discipline includes work on this problem: the testing practices that enable safe evolution (unit testing, integration testing, end-to-end testing, other testing levels), the refactoring practices, the work on managing technical debt, the work on dependency management as dependencies change, the work on database migrations and on data evolution. The intellectual content includes both specific practices and broader engineering principles about how applications can be engineered to remain evolvable. The contemporary work on this problem includes work on testing strategies, on forms of continuous integration and deployment that support safe evolution, on the observability practices that detect problems as they arise.

#### Where it sits in the map

Web and application development depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational trunk: programming as conceptual practice (Section 2.1) is foundational — application engineering is programming, and the practices of programming as conceptual practice apply to application development. Algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation, particularly for various algorithmic concerns that arise in application work. Operating systems (Section 2.5) provides foundation, particularly for understanding the deployment context. Computer networks (Section 2.6) provides essential foundation — application development is network programming. Databases (Section 2.8) provides essential foundation — applications integrate with databases. Distributed systems (Section 2.9) provides foundation, particularly for cloud-deployed applications.

From the systems axis: the engagement is strong. Cloud-native infrastructure (Section 4.6) is foundational for contemporary application deployment. Modern database internals (Section 4.2) provides depth on the database side. Observability and reliability engineering (Section 4.5) provides foundation for production application operation. Stream processing (Section 4.4) is increasingly relevant for event-driven applications.

From the intelligence axis: the engagement is increasingly through the contemporary integration of foundation models into applications. The work on application AI integration draws on foundation models (Section 5.3) and on the deployment concerns of AI-integrated applications.

From other application domains: security engineering (Section 6.3) overlaps with application security; HCI (Section 6.4) is central whenever applications expose interfaces to users; graphics (Section 6.2) matters for applications with visual content.

From outside computer science: the relationship to design disciplines is direct. The UX and UI design work that applications require is distinct from but complementary to the engineering work. The relationship to product management is through the concerns of building applications that serve user needs.

The position within this map is characteristic of application development: engagement with the system axis (deployment and operational concerns), engagement with the data axis (data engineering concerns), moderate engagement with the computation axis (some work on various concurrency and language concerns), and increasingly engagement with the intelligence axis (the contemporary AI integration). The discipline is configurational rather than primarily on any single axis, and the intellectual content arises from the configuration itself.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized web and application development acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat application development as collection of framework knowledge.

1. **Fluency with application architecture.** Application development requires reasoning about structure, choosing architectural patterns appropriate to requirements, articulating tradeoffs, and adapting architectures as requirements evolve. This fluency transfers across frameworks and technologies.

2. **Fluency with the integration of concerns.** Frontend, backend, data, networking, security, and deployment concerns interact constantly. Application engineers need to debug across layers and design solutions that integrate multiple concerns coherently.

3. **Fluency with production deployment.** Production work involves cloud infrastructure, deployment pipelines, monitoring, observability, and operational concerns. Application development is incomplete if deployment remains an afterthought.

4. **Engineering for evolution.** Applications change over time. Designing for changeability, testing, refactoring, dependency management, and maintenance practices matter because most application work happens after the first version ships.

5. **Security awareness.** Application engineers need to recognize common vulnerability classes, avoid them in design and implementation, and use secure development practices. This matters more as applications operate in adversarial contexts.

6. **The ability to operate at scale.** Scale involves concurrent users, data volume, deployment complexity, and operational practices. It is not just a matter of adding resources after the system is built.

7. **Broader concerns.** Accessibility, internationalization, bandwidth and device constraints, privacy, and broader social implications shape real applications. Application engineering should not optimize only for narrow user contexts.

8. **Fluency with the contemporary AI-integrated application landscape.** AI capabilities are increasingly part of application work. Integrating them thoughtfully requires knowledge of emerging engineering practices rather than treating AI calls as ordinary API calls.

#### How the field sees the world

Web and application development embeds a worldview that has emerged over decades of practice.

The first commitment is *to integration as engineering work*. The application development worldview holds that integrating across various concerns of applications is itself engineering work, not auxiliary to the more interesting specialized work. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer specialization to integration. The application engineer takes a different view: value arises from the integration, and the integration requires engineering competence in its own right. The disposition produces engineering different from purely specialized approaches.

The second commitment is *to taking the production deployment context seriously*. The application development worldview takes seriously that applications are deployed in production contexts with implications for engineering — scale concerns, operational concerns, security concerns, other production realities. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that focus on the development phase while neglecting the deployment phase. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat production realities as integral to engineering work from the start rather than as concerns to be addressed later.

The third commitment is *to engineering for change*. The application development worldview recognizes that applications change over time, and that engineering applications without designing for change produces problems as requirements evolve. The disposition produces engineering that prioritizes maintainability, that uses the practices supporting evolution, that resists premature optimization for current requirements at the expense of future flexibility.

The fourth commitment is *to the broader user concerns*. The application development worldview takes seriously that applications serve users with diversity — different bandwidths, different devices, different abilities, different languages, different contexts. The practices for accessibility, internationalization, performance for diverse contexts, and other broader concerns are integral to application engineering. The disposition runs against engineering dispositions that focus on narrow user contexts.

The fifth commitment is *to empirical engineering*. The application development worldview takes seriously that parts of effective practice depend on empirical observations about what works in production, that theoretical reasoning often underdetermines what production deployment actually requires, that methodology depends on empirical observation. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat empirical evaluation as integral to engineering, monitor production behavior carefully, and update practices in response to evidence rather than only to fashion.

The sixth commitment is *to pragmatism about technology choices*. The application development worldview takes seriously that the diversity of frameworks, tools, and approaches in contemporary application development reflects trade-offs rather than the dominance of objectively best choices. The disposition produces engineering that engages with trade-offs rather than reaching reflexively for the currently-fashionable choices. The diversity of approaches in contemporary practice is itself informative about the trade-offs involved.

The seventh commitment is *to professional discipline*. The application development worldview takes seriously that application engineering requires professional discipline — testing, code review, documentation, version control practice, debugging discipline, communication. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that focus exclusively on the coding work while neglecting the broader professional practices. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat the broader practices as integral to engineering work.

These seven commitments — to integration, to production deployment, to engineering for change, to broader user concerns, to empirical engineering, to pragmatism about technology, and to professional discipline — distinguish the application development worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in application development as professional practice rather than as application of framework tutorials.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning web and application development are characteristic and distinctive — the breadth of the discipline, the pace of change, and the framework-tutorial-driven introductory ecosystem produce many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the framework-as-discipline misconception*. Parts of application development are accessed through frameworks (React, Django, Rails, and others), and learners can come to treat learning the framework as the substance of learning application development. The result is practitioners who can use the framework but cannot reason about what the framework is doing, cannot debug issues that arise outside the framework's standard usage, cannot adapt approaches when the framework does not suffice. The countermeasure is to engage with the underlying concepts seriously: to study HTTP and networking foundationally, to understand how frameworks actually handle requests, to engage with what databases are doing rather than only with ORM abstractions. Framework knowledge is portable across frameworks but only if the underlying concepts are understood.

A second pitfall is *the new-shiny-thing distraction*. The pace of change in application development produces flow of new frameworks, tools, and approaches, and learners can come to focus on always learning the newest things while neglecting the durable concepts. The result is framework familiarity without depth in any framework, with the cost that adaptation to new frameworks remains shallow. The countermeasure is to invest in foundational concepts that transfer across frameworks: HTTP and the web protocols, the database concepts, the concurrency concepts, other foundations. The diversity of frameworks tracks diversity of practice; the foundations transfer.

A third pitfall is *the tutorial-driven understanding*. Parts of contemporary application development education proceed through tutorials — tutorials for specific frameworks, tutorials for specific tasks, tutorials for specific tools. Tutorials are valuable for getting started but limited as foundation for engineering practice. Learners can come to treat tutorial-driven knowledge as competence and find themselves unprepared for problems that the tutorials did not anticipate. The countermeasure is to supplement tutorials with reading of documentation, with reading of books that go beyond tutorial format, with practice on serious projects rather than only tutorial exercises.

A fourth pitfall is *the production-naive practice*. application development practice happens in development environments that differ from production environments — different scale, different concurrency, different reliability requirements, different security exposure. Learners can come to develop competence with development-environment work while remaining unprepared for production realities. The result is gap between development competence and production capability. The countermeasure is to engage with production concerns from early in learning: to deploy applications to production environments, to engage with the production-related literature, to recognize that production-readiness is different from feature-completeness.

A fifth pitfall is *the security-as-afterthought disposition*. Parts of application development can be done without security engagement — applications work without security attention, and security concerns become visible only when problems arise. Learners can come to treat security as concern to be addressed after applications work, rather than as concern integral to building applications correctly. The countermeasure is to engage with security from early in learning: to study common vulnerability classes, to use security analysis tools as routine part of development, to recognize that security concerns are integral to application engineering rather than auxiliary.

A sixth pitfall is *the testing-as-overhead misconception*. application development can produce working code without testing, and learners can come to treat testing as overhead rather than as integral to engineering. The result is applications that work in initial development but become difficult to evolve as they grow, and that fail in production scenarios that adequate testing would have caught. The countermeasure is to engage with testing as engineering practice from early in learning: to study the testing strategies, to use testing in development practice rather than only as occasional addition, to recognize that application engineering requires testing discipline.

A seventh pitfall is *the dependency-management neglect problem*. Contemporary applications depend on external dependencies — frameworks, libraries, the transitive dependencies of these. Learners can come to add dependencies casually without engaging with what they introduce — various security implications, the maintenance implications, the supply chain implications. The dependency-related security problems (various supply chain attacks, forms of dependency-related vulnerabilities) demonstrate the cost of dependency management neglect. The countermeasure is to engage with dependencies deliberately: to evaluate dependencies before adopting them, to maintain dependency hygiene, to understand the dependency-related security concerns.

An eighth pitfall is *the accessibility-and-i18n neglect problem*. Parts of application development can produce applications that work for narrow user contexts (specific languages, specific abilities, specific devices, specific bandwidth) while failing for broader user populations. Learners can come to treat accessibility and internationalization as nice-to-have additions rather than as integral to application engineering. The cost falls disproportionately on users with disabilities, on users in different language contexts, on users in resource-constrained contexts. The countermeasure is to engage with accessibility and internationalization from early in learning: to study the standards (WCAG for accessibility, various internationalization standards), to use the tools that support these concerns, to recognize that application engineering for diverse populations requires this engagement.

A ninth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied specifically to application development. Modern AI tools can produce application code, can answer many application development questions, and can guide learners through standard tasks. The benefits are real — AI-augmented application development can be productive — but the cost can also be high when learning is bypassed. Learners who use AI tools to bypass learning produce code without understanding what it does, cannot debug issues that arise, cannot evaluate whether the AI-produced code is appropriate. The countermeasure is to use AI tools as supplements to learning rather than as replacements for it: to do early work with deliberate engagement with the underlying concepts, to use AI tools to accelerate work in areas where competence has been established, to recognize that the productivity benefits of AI-augmented development depend on having competence to direct and evaluate the AI's output.

A tenth pitfall is *the fashion-driven architecture problem*. Application architecture has fashions — microservices became fashionable, then provoked backlash; serverless became fashionable; other architectural fashions. Learners can come to follow architectural fashion without engaging with the trade-offs. The result is applications that adopt architectures inappropriate to their actual requirements. The countermeasure is to engage with architectural choices deliberately: to understand the trade-offs of different architectural approaches, to choose architectures based on actual requirements rather than on fashion, to recognize that architectural choices have long-term implications that fashion-driven choices may not anticipate.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on web and application development has distinctive shape. The pace of change produces canonical-reference instability — books age within years rather than decades, and parts of the contemporary literature consists of more rapidly-publishable forms (blog posts, online tutorials, framework documentation, conference talks). The diversity of frameworks and approaches produces diversity of literature — sub-literatures for each major framework, with limited cross-framework synthesis.

##### Canonical entry points

A canonical introduction to web and application development as unified discipline does not exist — the diversity of paths into the discipline and the pace of change make any single canonical introduction limited. Several efforts at synthesis have appeared, with limitations.

For foundational coverage of web technologies, MDN Web Docs (Mozilla Developer Network, freely available) is the authoritative reference for web standards (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, various Web APIs). MDN is unusually comprehensive and unusually well-maintained, and serves as reference throughout application development careers.

For accessible introduction with conceptual depth, Wilson's *The Web Application Hacker's Handbook* (second edition, 2011) is dated but covers foundational web application concepts particularly well from the security perspective. The book is older and portions are dated, but the conceptual content remains valuable.

For more contemporary coverage, the *Refactoring UI* book by Schoger and Wathan provides accessible introduction to UI design that complements the engineering-focused material. The Frontend Masters and similar online education platforms offer structured curriculum for contemporary frontend work.

For backend engineering, Kleppmann's *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* (2017) is. Though framed as data-intensive applications, the book covers concerns of backend engineering — the database concerns, the distributed systems concerns, the reliability concerns. The book has achieved canonical status for backend application engineering and is among the most valuable references in the field. For learners pursuing backend work, working through Kleppmann is valuable.

For full-stack perspective on application engineering, various *Web Application Architecture* and similar books provide coverage. Tilkov's *Web Application Architecture: Principles, Protocols and Practices* (second edition, 2009) is dated but covers foundational architectural concepts. The contemporary architectural literature is scattered across blog posts and conference talks rather than in canonical book form.

For the intersection with cloud-native infrastructure, the material in Section 4.6 is relevant. Burns's *Designing Distributed Systems* (mentioned in Section 4.6) covers application architecture patterns that contemporary cloud-deployed applications use.

For learners interested in mobile application development, Apple's documentation and Android's documentation are the authoritative references for the major platforms. The books on iOS and Android development are platform-specific and age with platform updates.

The reading-order recommendation depends on focus area. For full-stack developers: MDN as foundational reference, Kleppmann for backend depth, framework-specific documentation for the specific frameworks of interest, with supplementation through contemporary online resources and through production experience. For specialists in specific subareas: specialization-specific resources alongside the foundational material.

Major online education resources provide structured curriculum. Various Frontend Masters, Egghead.io, Pluralsight, Udemy courses provide structured material. Various university programs in software engineering and the online courses (various Coursera and edX offerings) provide alternative pedagogical structure.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in web and application development branches according to specialization.

*Frontend engineering at depth.* For learners pursuing frontend work specifically, the JavaScript and TypeScript ecosystems are the starting point. Crockford's *JavaScript: The Good Parts* covers JavaScript fundamentals; Simpson's *You Don't Know JS* series provides deeper coverage; the TypeScript handbook and current TypeScript books cover typed JavaScript work. Framework-specific study should use React, Vue, Svelte, and related documentation alongside books where they remain current. Rendering frameworks (Next.js, Nuxt, SvelteKit), state-management approaches, and build tools such as Vite form the contemporary literature of practice.

*Backend engineering at depth.* For learners pursuing backend work specifically, the framework-specific literature for major backend frameworks is useful but fragmented. The Node.js ecosystem material, the Python framework material (Django, FastAPI, Flask, and others), the Ruby on Rails material, the Go framework material, the Java backend material, the Rust backend material — each has its own sub-literature. Beyond framework-specific material, the books on backend architecture (Fowler's *Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture*, various Domain-Driven Design literature, various Clean Architecture material) provide architectural perspective.

*API design at depth.* For learners pursuing API design specifically, the REST literature includes Fielding's foundational dissertation and Massé's *REST API Design Rulebook*. The GraphQL literature includes the GraphQL specification and various GraphQL practitioner books. The work on API versioning, on API documentation (OpenAPI/Swagger), on various RPC alternatives constitutes sub-literature.

*Performance engineering at depth.* For learners pursuing performance specifically, the frontend performance literature includes Souders' *High Performance Web Sites* (now dated but conceptually foundational) and the contemporary work on frontend performance (various Google web.dev publications, the Lighthouse documentation, various contemporary frontend performance resources). The backend performance literature overlaps with database performance and with the distributed systems performance work.

*Security in application development at depth.* For learners pursuing application security, OWASP (Open Worldwide Application Security Project) provides reference material — the OWASP Top 10, various OWASP cheat sheets, the OWASP testing guide. The intersection with security engineering (Section 6.3) is direct, and learners pursuing application security seriously should engage with that section's material.

*Mobile development at depth.* For learners pursuing mobile development, the platform-specific literature is central. Apple's Swift and SwiftUI documentation and the third-party Swift books cover iOS work. Google's Android documentation and the Kotlin and Android material cover Android work. The cross-platform approaches (React Native, Flutter, and others) have their own sub-literatures.

*DevOps and deployment at depth.* The DevOps and deployment literature is broad. Kim et al.'s *The DevOps Handbook* is a standard entry. The work on continuous integration and continuous deployment, on deployment automation, on infrastructure as code (treated in Section 4.6), constitutes literature.

*Testing at depth.* The testing literature is broad. Beck's *Test-Driven Development* is foundational. Various books on various testing strategies (unit testing, integration testing, end-to-end testing, and others) provide coverage. The framework-specific testing literature (testing in Jest, Pytest, and others) is useful but uneven.

*Database integration at depth.* The database integration literature overlaps with the database material in Sections 2.8 and 4.2. The ORM-specific literature (various ActiveRecord books, various SQLAlchemy material, other ORM material) covers application-specific database concerns. The work on database query optimization in application contexts, on various caching strategies, on various data access patterns constitutes sub-literature.

*Frontend architecture at depth.* The frontend architecture literature includes work on state management patterns (Redux, and others), on component architecture, on various module organization approaches. Reginald Braithwaite's *JavaScript Allongé* and similar conceptually deep JavaScript books provide perspective.

*The contemporary AI integration at depth.* For learners pursuing AI-integrated application development specifically, the contemporary literature is rapidly developing. The Hugging Face documentation, the OpenAI and Anthropic API documentation and tutorials, the books emerging on AI-augmented application development, the work on RAG systems, on AI agents in applications, on other AI integration patterns constitute the contemporary literature. The intersection with foundation models (Section 5.3) is direct.

*Accessibility and internationalization at depth.* The WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) documentation is reference. The books on accessible web development (Inclusive Components by Pickering, and others) provide coverage. The internationalization work has its own standards (Unicode, ICU, and others) and its own sub-literature.

*Real-time application development.* For learners pursuing real-time applications, the work on WebSocket protocols, on various server-sent events approaches, on various WebRTC work, on the real-time framework work constitutes sub-literature.

##### Reference works

MDN Web Docs (developer.mozilla.org, free) is the authoritative reference for web standards. The reference is unusually comprehensive and unusually well-maintained, and serves as reference throughout application development careers.

Various framework documentation (React documentation, Vue documentation, Django documentation, Rails guides, and others) constitutes reference for the major frameworks. Quality varies but the popular frameworks generally have documentation.

The W3C and WHATWG specifications constitute the authoritative reference for web standards. Specifications are dense but authoritative for production work.

The HTTP specifications (various RFCs from RFC 7230 onward) constitute authoritative reference for the HTTP protocol that application development depends on.

The OWASP documentation constitutes reference for application security.

The cloud platform documentation (AWS, GCP, Azure, and others) constitutes reference for cloud deployment.

The Stack Overflow archive, while of variable quality, constitutes reference for specific questions that arise in application development. The age of much Stack Overflow content makes care necessary, but the resource remains valuable.

The GitHub repositories of popular open source applications constitute reference for how applications are actually built. Reading open-source application code is among the more durable ways to develop application engineering judgment.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within application development and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The minimalist counter-tradition.* work argues that contemporary application development has become over-engineered, with the frameworks, build tools, and architectural complexity producing applications that are harder to build and maintain than necessary. Various writings advocating for simpler approaches (various server-rendered approaches that resist excessive client-side complexity, various "boring technology" arguments, various Hypermedia Systems and HTMX work that returns to simpler architectural patterns) constitute heterodox literature. The arguments contain insight and provide perspective on what alternatives to the dominant complexity might look like.

*The local-first / offline-first perspective.* The work on local-first software argues that contemporary cloud-centric application architecture has costs (privacy concerns, dependency on internet connectivity, the centralization implications) and that alternative architectures emphasizing local data and synchronization are better for application categories. Various papers and writings on local-first software (Kleppmann's work, various CRDT-related work, and others) constitute heterodox literature with intellectual depth.

*The functional programming perspective on application development.* work in functional programming (associated with the Haskell, Elm, ClojureScript, Elixir, and similar communities) argues that the state management complexity of contemporary applications is the result of using imperative and object-oriented approaches when functional approaches would simplify the work. Various functional application development literature constitutes alternative tradition.

*The static-site-generator and content-focused tradition.* work argues that the dynamic-application complexity of contemporary application development is unnecessary for application categories — that many applications are content sites that benefit from static generation rather than from dynamic infrastructure. Various static site generators (Jekyll, Hugo, and others), the Jamstack literature, and various content-focused approaches constitute alternative tradition.

*The native-application perspective.* work argues that web applications have won at the cost of user experience quality, and that native applications (desktop and mobile) better serve use cases. Various native application development traditions (the Apple platform development, the Windows desktop development, various Linux desktop traditions) constitute alternative perspective on what application development can be.

*The "serverless is overhyped" position.* Various practitioners argue that parts of the serverless and cloud-native enthusiasm are misplaced — that applications would be better served by simpler infrastructure than by the complexity that contemporary cloud-native approaches involve. The position has proponents and argument behind it.

*The "front-end fatigue" critical perspective.* writing critiques the pace of change in frontend development specifically, arguing that the framework churn produces cost without corresponding benefit. Various essays on this concern (the "How it feels to learn JavaScript in 2016" and similar pieces from various periods) constitute critical literature. The concerns are legitimate, though the field has responded with frameworks (React, Vue, and others) achieving stability over periods.

##### What to skip and why

Most books on web development published before approximately 2018 are dated for contemporary practice, with the exceptions of foundational conceptual books (Kleppmann's *Designing Data-Intensive Applications* remaining valuable, various foundational architectural books retaining value) and reference material that predates contemporary changes.

Most marketing-driven books on specific frameworks should be approached as practical references rather than as foundational learning. The framework-specific books often duplicate documentation while aging more quickly than documentation does.

Most "X for Y" books (web development for designers, web development for marketers, and others) should be approached with caution. The genre exists and is uneven; the exceptions exist but are not the norm.

Books on specific old framework versions are obsolete when the framework has evolved. Major frameworks (React, Vue, Django, Rails, and others) have evolved through major versions, and books on old versions provide limited value.

Most "10x developer" or productivity-focused books in application development should be approached with skepticism. The genre tends toward generic advice that applies poorly to specific application development contexts.

Books that promise to teach application development "without coding" or "with minimal coding" should generally be skipped. Application development requires coding competence, and treatments that abstract over coding produce practitioners who cannot do what application development requires.

Most online tutorials on application development topics are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained framework documentation, the educational content from major framework maintainers, the talks from prominent practitioners — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory mechanics without depth.

Books on application development that present specific architectural approaches (microservices, monoliths, and others) as universally appropriate should be supplemented with material providing perspective on trade-offs. No architectural approach is universally appropriate, and books that present approaches without trade-off discussion produce practitioners who apply approaches inappropriately.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary web and application development content are online. Various conference recordings (Google I/O, Microsoft Build, various JSConf events, various Ruby and Python and other language-specific conferences, various framework-specific conferences) provide content with recordings on YouTube.

Several blogs and online resources provide ongoing engagement. The web.dev publication from Google provides frontend-focused content. The CSS-Tricks publication provides CSS and frontend content. Smashing Magazine provides broader frontend content. Various framework-specific blogs (the React blog, the Vue blog, and others) provide framework-specific content. Various practitioner blogs from prominent application developers (Dan Abramov's writings, Kent C. Dodds, and others) provide ongoing perspective.

Various practitioner-focused podcasts (the JavaScript Jabber podcast, the Ruby Rogues podcast, other language-specific podcasts, various general application development podcasts) provide ongoing audio content.

The Hacker News aggregator, while of variable quality, provides ongoing exposure to contemporary application development discussion. The discussions accompanying posts often provide contemporary perspective.

Various developer survey publications (Stack Overflow Annual Developer Survey, State of JavaScript, State of CSS, and others) provide reference for the contemporary state of application development practice and tooling.

Various GitHub repositories of major frameworks and their issues and discussions constitute contemporary reference for what major frameworks are doing and what concerns are active.

Various AI tool documentation (the Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and others) provides reference for the contemporary AI-augmented application development practices.

The open-source applications (various major web applications that publish their code) constitute reference for how applications are built in practice. Reading production application code is among the more valuable contemporary engagement.

| Pre-2018 application development books | dated | Skip (with exceptions) |
| Marketing-driven framework books | Mixed quality | Skip (in favor of documentation) |
| Old framework version books | Obsolete | Skip (in favor of current versions) |
| "Without coding" application books | Limited utility | Skip (in favor of real learning) |
| Tutorials covering only introductory mechanics | Insufficient depth | Skip (in favor of canonical sources) |
| Books advocating single architectural approach as universal | Misleading | Skip (in favor of trade-off-aware treatments) |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Web and application development is one of the most active application domains in this map. Current practice is being reshaped by several forces:

1. **AI assistance is changing development practice itself.** Tools such as GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code, and related systems affect productivity, skill development, review discipline, and what counts as competence.
2. **AI capabilities are being built into applications.** Conversational interfaces and AI-assisted features change what applications are expected to do and what application engineers must understand.
3. **TypeScript has become standard in much of the JavaScript ecosystem.** Frontend and Node.js work increasingly assumes typed JavaScript as normal practice.
4. **Edge deployment has changed application architecture.** Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge Functions, and related platforms move some computation closer to users and alter latency, state, and deployment tradeoffs.
5. **Frameworks continue to mature and turn over.** Next.js, SvelteKit, Astro, and adjacent tools shape contemporary practice while leaving the framework landscape in motion.
6. **Application complexity has produced a counter-movement.** HTMX, renewed server-side rendering, and simpler tooling all respond to fatigue with excessive client-side complexity.
7. **Accessibility and inclusive design are becoming mainstream expectations.** They increasingly belong to ordinary application engineering rather than to a late-stage specialty pass.
8. **Sustainability concerns are entering the field.** Energy use, infrastructure cost, and the economic sustainability of advertising-supported application models are now part of the surrounding discussion.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of application development is robust. The core concerns — integration across concerns, production deployment, engineering for change, security, broader user concerns — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Among the application chapters, this one has the broadest day-to-day relevance: many CS practitioners touch application development even when it is not their specialization. Its pace of change makes currency part of competence, but durable competence still depends on foundations: architecture, security, data modeling, interface judgment, and production operations. AI-assisted practice makes that distinction sharper rather than less important.

### 6.2 — Computer Graphics and Games

#### What it is, properly

Computer graphics and games is the discipline concerned with generating, manipulating, and displaying visual content through computation — covering the range from the fundamental mathematics and algorithms of visual representation through real-time rendering systems for interactive applications through offline rendering for film and other production contexts. The subject treats visual computation as intellectual work in its own right, with characteristic mathematical foundations (linear algebra for transformations, calculus and differential geometry for surfaces and lighting, probability theory for sampling and Monte Carlo methods), characteristic algorithmic content (work on rendering algorithms, on geometric algorithms, on forms of acceleration structures), characteristic systems engineering challenges (GPU programming, real-time performance constraints, concerns of contemporary game engines), and application domains (game development, film and animation, scientific visualization, virtual and augmented reality, various contemporary applications of generative visual AI). The discipline has historical depth, with development from the 1960s onward through the foundational work on rendering, modeling, and animation, subsequent development through improvements in real-time rendering capabilities, and contemporary development driven by advances in GPU capability, by maturation of physically-based rendering, and by the recent emergence of generative visual AI as a active subdiscipline.

The conventional account treats computer graphics as essentially the practice of drawing things on screens — pixels, polygons, various rendering operations that make computer-generated visuals appear. This account captures the surface activity but understates what graphics has become as intellectual subject. The conceptual core is the recognition that *visual representation involves mathematical content that goes far beyond surface mechanics*, that *the physics of light and the geometry of surfaces produce computational problems with intellectual depth*, that *real-time graphics involves systems engineering at the interface of hardware capabilities and algorithmic content*, and that *the application domains (games, film, visualization, and others) each impose domain-specific concerns that shape what graphics practice involves in those contexts*. These recognitions distinguish graphics engineering from the surface mechanics that introductory materials sometimes emphasize.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Rendering* is the subdiscipline concerned with computing what surfaces and volumes look like when illuminated — including the work on rasterization (the dominant approach for real-time rendering), on ray tracing and path tracing (for offline rendering and increasingly for real-time work), on the work on lighting and material models, on various global illumination approaches. *Modeling* is the subdiscipline concerned with representing geometry computationally — including the work on polygon meshes, on parametric surfaces (NURBS and similar), on subdivision surfaces, on signed distance fields, on various procedural modeling approaches. *Animation* is the subdiscipline concerned with how things move and change over time — including the work on character animation, on physics simulation for animation, on forms of procedural animation, on motion capture and its integration with computer animation. *Geometric processing* covers the algorithmic work on operating on geometric data — mesh simplification, parameterization, deformation, forms of geometric analysis. *Real-time graphics and game engines* covers the systems engineering of contemporary real-time rendering — including GPU programming, work on game engine architecture, the work on various rendering pipelines that contemporary games use. *Physics simulation* covers computational simulation of physical phenomena for graphics applications — rigid body dynamics, soft body dynamics, fluid simulation, other forms of physics simulation that animations and games use. *Visualization* is the subdiscipline concerned with using graphics to convey data or information — distinct from but overlapping with rendering and animation, with concerns specific to communicating information visually rather than to producing visual realism. *Image processing and computer vision* covers the work on operating on existing images rather than on generating new visual content — overlapping with computer vision as subdiscipline, with concerns specific to graphics applications. *Virtual and augmented reality* covers the work on immersive interactive graphics — including the concerns of low-latency rendering, of accommodation of head and body tracking, of various challenges specific to immersive interactive contexts. *Generative visual AI* covers the recent work on producing visual content through machine learning approaches — diffusion models, the GANs that preceded them, other generative approaches that have transformed parts of visual content production.

The intellectual content of graphics is distinctive because it combines mathematics, physics, systems engineering, and visual judgment. Linear algebra, calculus, differential geometry, and probability theory are load-bearing. Physics enters through models of light, material, surfaces, and volumes. Real-time graphics is systems engineering at scale, involving GPU programming, pipeline design, and performance optimization. The field also serves artistic and communicative purposes, so competent practice requires attention to visual and design concerns alongside technical content. Contemporary machine learning adds another layer, especially in neural rendering, content generation, denoising, reconstruction, and asset pipelines.

The reason this account matters is that graphics has become central to parts of contemporary computing — parts of contemporary entertainment depend on graphics, parts of contemporary scientific work depend on visualization, parts of contemporary AI work depend on generative visual capabilities. The gap between informed graphics practice and uninformed practice is large, and the cost of the gap has grown as graphics applications have become more demanding. The discipline of graphics as serious engineering and intellectual practice differs from graphics as collection of API calls, and competent work requires preparation that the tutorial-driven introductory ecosystem often understates.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize computer graphics and games as a discipline.

**The problem of representing visual content computationally.** A central question graphics addresses is how visual content can be represented in computer memory and manipulated through algorithms. The difficulty arises because visual content has complexity — geometry can be detailed, surfaces can have material variation, lighting can vary, and the range of visual content that humans encounter in the world produces variety of representational challenges. The discipline includes work on this problem: various polygon mesh representations and the mesh processing literature, various parametric and implicit surface representations, the volumetric representations, various procedural and generative approaches that compute geometry rather than storing it explicitly, various texture and material representations. The intellectual content includes both representational questions (what representations work for what kinds of content, what trade-offs different representations entail) and algorithmic questions (what operations on each representation are tractable, how representations can be converted between forms). The contemporary work on neural representations of visual content (NeRFs, Gaussian splatting, and others) extends the representational tradition into machine learning contexts.

**The problem of simulating light and material interaction.** A portion of graphics concerns computing what surfaces look like when illuminated — how light from sources interacts with materials to produce the appearance that we see. The difficulty arises because the physics of light is complex: light interacts with materials in varied ways, with mathematical models for different interaction types (work on diffuse and specular reflection, on subsurface scattering, on transparent and translucent materials, on various complex material types that real-world surfaces exhibit). The discipline includes work on this problem: the physically-based rendering literature, the Monte Carlo rendering methods (path tracing, bidirectional path tracing, photon mapping, and others), the real-time approximations that achieve adequate visual quality within real-time performance constraints, the work on various BRDFs (bidirectional reflectance distribution functions) and material models. The intellectual content includes mathematical content (integration over spaces, Monte Carlo methods, various sampling approaches), physical content (models of how light actually behaves), and algorithmic content (efficient computation of the integrals that lighting requires).

**The problem of real-time performance constraints.** A portion of graphics work operates under real-time constraints — game frames must be rendered within milliseconds, virtual reality requires submillisecond response times, other real-time graphics applications impose performance requirements. The difficulty has implications throughout the graphics stack. The discipline includes work on real-time rendering: the rasterization pipeline that has been optimized over decades, the GPU architecture and programming, the level-of-detail systems that maintain visual quality while reducing computational load, the caching and culling approaches, the recent work on integrating ray tracing into real-time rendering pipelines through hardware support. The intellectual content includes systems engineering, algorithmic engineering, GPU programming, and appreciation for the ways that real-time constraints shape what can be done.

**The problem of geometric processing at complexity.** Graphics operations require processing of geometric data — meshes with polygon counts, point clouds from scanning and reconstruction, other forms of geometric data. The difficulty arises in operating on this geometric data efficiently and correctly: work on intersection and collision detection, on geometric simplification, on parameterization, on forms of geometric analysis. The discipline includes algorithmic work on these problems: the spatial data structures (BVH, kd-trees, octrees, and others), the algorithms for various geometric operations, the work on robust geometric computation (which is genuinely difficult due to floating-point precision issues), the work on parallel geometric algorithms for GPU implementation. The intellectual content includes computational geometry content, algorithmic content, and systems content.

**The problem of integrating graphics with application requirements.** Graphics serves applications — games, film, scientific visualization, virtual reality, and others — and each application context imposes requirements that shape what graphics practice involves. Games require real-time performance, integration with gameplay systems, accommodation of creative direction, integration with audio and physics systems. Film requires visual quality at offline rendering times, pipeline integration with production workflows, accommodation of creative iteration. Scientific visualization requires accuracy in conveying data, accommodation of domain-specific data types and domain-specific user needs. Virtual reality requires low-latency rendering, accommodation of tracking systems, concerns specific to immersive interactive contexts. The discipline includes work on each of these application contexts and on the general principles that apply across them. The intellectual content includes both application-specific knowledge and broader engineering practices for graphics in application contexts.

#### Where it sits in the map

Computer graphics and games depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation, particularly for the algorithmic work on rendering, on geometric processing, and on various spatial data structures. Computer architecture (Section 2.4) provides foundation, particularly for understanding GPU architecture and the parallelism that contemporary graphics depends on. Programming as conceptual practice (Section 2.1) is foundational for programming work that graphics involves.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): mathematical preparation is essential. Linear algebra is foundational and load-bearing throughout — parts of graphics are linear algebra applied to geometric and visual problems. Calculus, particularly multivariable calculus and the work on integration over surfaces and volumes, is foundational for rendering work. Probability theory is foundational for Monte Carlo rendering methods and for parts of contemporary graphics. Differential geometry provides foundation for work on surfaces. The mathematical preparation required for serious graphics work is significant.

From the systems axis: the engagement is strong. High-performance computing (Section 4.9) is relevant — graphics is HPC work, particularly through GPU programming. Modern compilers and language runtimes (Section 4.7) are relevant through the GPU compilation infrastructure (CUDA, various shader compilation pipelines).

From the intelligence axis: the engagement has grown with the contemporary integration of generative visual AI. Deep learning (Section 5.2) is relevant for parts of contemporary graphics that involve learned components. Foundation models (Section 5.3) are increasingly relevant through the multimodal foundation models that handle visual content.

From the data axis: the engagement is moderate but through the textures, geometry data, and other graphics data that applications work with at scale.

From other application domains: HCI (Section 6.4) matters because graphics applications still require interfaces; robotics (Section 6.5) connects through robot vision, simulation, and perception; scientific computing (Section 6.6) connects through visualization.

From outside computer science: physics matters because physically-based rendering and simulation depend on physical models; art and design matter because graphics serves expressive and communicative purposes; mathematics matters because graphics draws on several mathematical subdisciplines beyond the CS-specific substrate.

The position within this map is characteristic of application development: engagement with the system axis (GPU programming and systems engineering), engagement with the computation axis (algorithmic and mathematical content), moderate engagement with the data axis (graphics data engineering), and increasingly engagement with the intelligence axis (the contemporary generative visual AI integration). The discipline is configurational rather than primarily on any single axis.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized computer graphics and games acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat graphics as collection of API calls.

1. **Fluency with graphics mathematics.** Graphics depends on linear algebra, coordinate transformations, lighting and material models, and the mathematical content of rendering algorithms. This preparation transfers across APIs and remains relevant as specific techniques evolve.

2. **Fluency with graphics programming.** GPU programming, shader writing, parallel computation in graphics contexts, and productive use of APIs such as Vulkan, DirectX, and Metal are essential for serious graphics work.

3. **The ability to reason about visual quality and visual artifacts.** Graphics practitioners need to look at rendered output, identify what is wrong, reason about the cause of specific artifacts, and address artifacts at their causes rather than only at their symptoms.

4. **Fluency with real-time performance.** Real-time graphics requires attention to performance constraints, graphics-pipeline bottlenecks, optimization techniques, and GPU performance characteristics.

5. **Integration of mathematical, algorithmic, systems, and artistic concerns.** Graphics work combines mathematics, algorithms, systems engineering, artistic judgment, and design concerns. Competent practice recognizes when a problem requires several of these at once.

6. **Diversity of graphics application domains.** Game graphics, film graphics, scientific visualization, virtual reality, and other domains have different requirements. Graphics practice has to adapt to the application context.

7. **Fluency with contemporary AI integration.** Generative visual AI, neural rendering, and other intersections between graphics and machine learning are reshaping parts of graphics practice. Productive integration requires understanding where these tools fit and where they do not.

8. **Historical and conceptual perspective.** Graphics has a long tradition of techniques tried, successes achieved, failures repeated, and ideas rediscovered. Historical perspective helps distinguish genuinely new work from recurring patterns.

#### How the field sees the world

Computer graphics and games embeds a worldview that has emerged over decades of practice.

The first commitment is *to physical correctness as foundation for visual quality*. The graphics worldview holds that visual quality benefits from physical correctness — that rendering algorithms based on physics produce better visual results than ad-hoc approaches that approximate visual appearance without physical grounding. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer ad-hoc approaches when they are simpler. The graphics practitioner takes a different view: the investment in physical correctness pays visual dividends, and the physically-based rendering tradition has demonstrated this. The disposition shapes how rendering is approached and produces engineering different from less physically-grounded alternatives.

The second commitment is *to mathematical rigor as integral to graphics*. The graphics worldview takes seriously that graphics requires mathematics, and that the mathematical content is integral to the discipline rather than auxiliary. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer to abstract over mathematical content. The graphics practitioner engages with the mathematics — linear algebra, calculus, probability theory, various mathematical content that graphics depends on. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat mathematical fluency as required rather than as nice-to-have.

The third commitment is *to taking real-time performance as real engineering concern*. The graphics worldview takes seriously that real-time performance is engineering concern, that parts of graphics practice operate under performance constraints, and that engineering for performance is integral to graphics work rather than auxiliary. This commitment shapes how problems are approached: with attention to performance from the start, with methodology for measuring and addressing performance concerns, with appreciation for what real-time performance actually requires.

The fourth commitment is *to integration of art and engineering*.
