| Project Zero adversarial perspective writings | Heterodox offensive perspective | Current, heterodox |
| Pre-2015 security books | Often dated | Skip (with foundational exceptions) |
| Marketing-driven security product books | Mixed quality | Skip (in favor of canonical sources) |
| "Security for managers" books as technical reference | Insufficient technical depth | Skip (as primary technical references) |
| Old penetration testing tool books | Often outdated | Skip (in favor of current sources) |
| Cryptography books abstracting over mathematics | Incomplete coverage | Skip (in favor of mathematically grounded treatments) |
| Most security certification prep books as real learning | Optimize for certification | Skip (as primary useful references) |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Security engineering is one of the more active application domains in this map. Several developments now reshape the discipline:

1. **AI is changing both attack and defense.** AI capabilities affect phishing, vulnerability discovery, threat detection, automated patching, and the analysis of security-relevant code. AI systems also create new attack surfaces, including prompt injection, jailbreaks, and training-data poisoning. The cat-and-mouse dynamic is still developing.
2. **Zero-trust architectures are replacing perimeter assumptions.** Distributed infrastructure has made perimeter defense insufficient in many contexts, so security practice increasingly assumes that internal networks are not automatically trustworthy.
3. **Supply-chain security has moved to the center.** Attacks such as SolarWinds made dependency signing, software bills of material, reproducible builds, and provenance tracking central rather than peripheral concerns.
4. **Passwordless authentication is becoming practical.** WebAuthn and passkeys have made password alternatives deployable at consumer and enterprise scale.
5. **Post-quantum cryptography is entering deployment planning.** NIST standardization and migration work have made quantum-resistant cryptography a present engineering concern, even though the hardware timeline remains uncertain.
6. **Regulation is shaping practice.** GDPR, data-protection laws, AI-specific rules, and sectoral compliance regimes increasingly define what organizations must do, not only what security engineers recommend.
7. **Cloud security has become its own body of practice.** Cloud identity, configuration, workload isolation, secret management, and provider-specific control planes now require specialized fluency.
8. **The field is reckoning with the gap between known best practice and actual deployment.** Security failures often come from organizational limits, misaligned incentives, and operational shortcuts rather than from missing technical knowledge.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of security engineering is robust. The core concerns — adversarial design, cryptographic correctness, full-stack defense, authentication and authorization, evolving threats — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Security is unusual because it is adversarial. The practitioner is not merely optimizing a system against load or failure; they are working against intelligent opponents who adapt. That history is humbling: systems once regarded as secure have been broken, defenses once regarded as adequate have failed, and overconfidence has repeatedly proved costly. Good security practice therefore combines technical depth with humility, defense in depth, attention to human factors, organizational incentives, and policy constraints.

### 6.4 — Human-Computer Interaction

#### What it is, properly

Human-computer interaction is the discipline concerned with how people interact with computational systems — how interfaces should be designed, how interactions should be structured, how systems should respond to users, how the range of human capabilities and limitations should shape the design of computational tools. The subject treats the relationship between people and computers as intellectual and engineering work, with characteristic empirical content (research methods drawn from psychology and design research, user studies, usability evaluation methodologies), characteristic design content (design principles and patterns, work on visual and interaction design, accumulated wisdom about what works and what does not), characteristic theoretical content (cognitive science foundations, work on perception and action, work on forms of cognitive load and attention), and application domains (interface design for applications, accessibility work, design for diverse populations, the contemporary work on AI-mediated interaction). The discipline has historical depth, with development from the 1960s onward through the foundational work on graphical user interfaces, subsequent development through the maturation of usability engineering and user experience design, and contemporary development driven by mobile and ubiquitous computing, by accessibility concerns, and by the recent emergence of AI-mediated interaction as active subdiscipline.

The conventional account treats HCI as essentially the practice of "making things look nice" — choosing colors, arranging buttons, ensuring that interfaces are visually pleasant. This account misrepresents what HCI is. The conceptual core of HCI is the recognition that *interactions between people and computers involve complexity that visual surface concerns do not capture*, that *design depends on understanding of human cognition, perception, and behavior*, that *empirical work is integral to HCI rather than auxiliary*, and that *deployment of computational systems in contexts produces responsibilities for design that affects human lives*. These recognitions distinguish HCI engineering and design from the surface aesthetics that the conventional account emphasizes.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Interaction design* is the subdiscipline concerned with how users interact with systems — work on the structures of interaction, on various interaction patterns (direct manipulation, command languages, and others), on the work on what interactions are appropriate for what tasks. *Visual and graphic design for interfaces* covers the visual dimension of interface work — typography, layout, visual hierarchy, the work on visual design principles. *Information architecture* covers the work on organizing information in ways that support use — taxonomy and ontology work for content, navigation design, the work on findability. *Usability engineering* covers the empirical and methodological work on evaluating whether designs actually work — usability testing, heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough, other evaluation methods. *User experience research* covers the work on understanding users — qualitative methods (interviews, ethnography, and others), quantitative methods (surveys, behavioral analytics, and others), work on translating user understanding into design implications. *Accessibility* is the subdiscipline concerned with design for populations including people with disabilities — work on visual accessibility (color, contrast, various concerns), work on motor accessibility, work on cognitive accessibility, work on accessible technology more broadly. *Universal design and inclusive design* extend accessibility to design for broader range of human variation. *Internationalization and localization* cover design for diverse cultural and linguistic contexts. *Mobile and ubiquitous computing interaction* cover interaction concerns specific to mobile and ubiquitous contexts. *Voice and conversational interaction* cover interaction concerns specific to voice-based and conversational interfaces. *AI-mediated interaction* is the contemporary subdiscipline concerned with interaction with AI-augmented systems and with AI-mediated experiences. *Information visualization* overlaps with graphics (Section 6.2) but with specific concerns for visual communication of information. *Cognitive science foundations* covers the scientific foundations from cognitive science that HCI depends on.

The intellectual content of HCI is distinctive because it combines empirical research, design judgment, and engineering. Some of its methods come from psychology and the behavioral sciences; some come from graphic design, industrial design, and design research; some come from software engineering and interactive-systems work. Accessibility is not an add-on in this account but part of competent design from the start. The deeper commitment is humanistic: HCI treats human needs, limits, and contexts as part of the engineering problem rather than as external decoration.

The reason this account matters is that HCI has become central to parts of contemporary computing. Portions of software work involve HCI concerns; parts of users' experiences with computational systems are shaped by HCI decisions; parts of successful and unsuccessful technology adoption depend on HCI work. The gap between informed HCI practice and uninformed practice is large, and the cost of the gap falls on users. The discipline of HCI as design and research practice differs from HCI as collection of UI conventions, and the difference matters for whether systems actually serve users effectively.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize human-computer interaction as a discipline.

**The problem of designing for human cognitive capabilities and limitations.** Humans have cognitive capabilities (pattern recognition, language processing, spatial reasoning, and others) and cognitive limitations (limited working memory, limited attention, vulnerability to certain cognitive biases, and others). design depends on understanding of these capabilities and limitations, with design adapting to human cognition rather than expecting humans to adapt to systems. The discipline includes work on this problem: work on cognitive load (Sweller's work and the subsequent literature), work on attention and forms of attention failure, work on memory and the implications for interface design, work on perception and the implications for visual design. The intellectual content includes both cognitive science foundations and design implications. Norman's *The Design of Everyday Things* articulates parts of this orientation, and subsequent work has developed it.

**The problem of designing for diversity of users.** Users have variation — visual abilities, motor abilities, cognitive abilities, linguistic and cultural backgrounds, technical fluency, other dimensions of variation. The difficulty arises in designing systems that serve variety of users effectively rather than only the subset closest to designers. The discipline includes work on this problem: the accessibility tradition with accumulated wisdom and standards (WCAG and others), the universal design tradition extending accessibility to broader variation, the internationalization and localization work, the work on design for low-resource and emerging-market contexts. The intellectual content includes both methodological content (how to study and design for diversity) and accumulated wisdom about what works.

**The problem of evaluating whether designs actually work.** design intuitions are unreliable — designers routinely produce designs that they believe work well but that users actually struggle with. The difficulty has implications for design practice: empirical evaluation is integral to HCI rather than auxiliary, with methodology required for reliable evaluation. The discipline includes work on this problem: the usability testing tradition, the heuristic evaluation methodology (Nielsen's heuristics and the subsequent work), the cognitive walkthrough methodology, the work on more rigorous evaluation methods (controlled experiments, quantitative methods, and others). The intellectual content includes both methodological content and accumulated wisdom about what evaluation reveals about designs.

**The problem of interaction across contexts and modalities.** computational interaction happens across diverse contexts (desktop, mobile, ubiquitous, voice, augmented reality, and others) and diverse modalities (visual, auditory, haptic, and others). The difficulty arises in designing for range of contexts and modalities, with appreciation for what works in what context. The discipline includes work on each context and modality and on general principles that apply across them. The intellectual content includes both context-specific knowledge and broader principles about interaction design across contexts.

**The problem of integration of design with engineering and broader concerns.** HCI does not happen in isolation — design must integrate with engineering constraints, business concerns, regulatory requirements, other concerns. The difficulty arises in producing design work that integrates productively with broader development context. The discipline includes work on this integration: work on agile and iterative design processes, work on design systems that scale design work, work on collaboration between designers and engineers, the work on design's role in product development.

#### Where it sits in the map

Human-computer interaction depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational trunk: programming as conceptual practice (Section 2.1) is foundational for HCI engineering work. Various other trunk subjects provide context.

From the systems axis: the engagement is through implementation of interactive systems. Various systems-axis subjects (operating systems, networking, databases, and others) all affect HCI work in specific ways.

From the intelligence axis: the engagement is increasingly through AI-mediated interaction. Foundation models (Section 5.3) are increasingly central to contemporary HCI work. The work on conversational interfaces, on AI-augmented applications, on various AI-HCI intersections is rapidly developing.

From other application domains: web and application development (Section 6.1) involves HCI whenever applications are designed for users; graphics (Section 6.2) connects through visual interface work and information visualization; robotics (Section 6.5) connects through human-robot interaction.

From outside computer science: psychology provides cognitive foundations and research methods; design disciplines provide methodological and conceptual material; anthropology and sociology inform qualitative research and social context; communication studies matters for mediated interaction; disability studies matters for accessibility; philosophy enters through agency, autonomy, and design ethics; art and aesthetics shape visual and interaction design.

The position within this map is characteristic of application development with difference: engagement with the system axis (implementation work), light engagement with the computation axis (some interaction theory), moderate engagement with the data axis (data presentation), and increasingly engagement with the intelligence axis (AI-mediated interaction). The discipline is unusual in its integration with non-CS disciplines, particularly cognitive science and design fields.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized human-computer interaction acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat HCI as collection of UI conventions.

1. **Empathy for users.** HCI trains attention toward diverse users and perspectives different from the designer's own. User research and direct engagement make clear that designers are not representative users.

2. **Fluency with empirical evaluation methods.** Usability testing, heuristic evaluation, and related methods differ in what they reveal and what claims they can support. HCI competence requires using these methods with methodological care.

3. **Accessibility.** Accessibility belongs inside design from the start, not as late remediation. Design choices have concrete consequences for users with disabilities.

4. **Fluency with design principles.** Visual design principles, interaction design patterns, information architecture, and related material give practitioners a vocabulary for design decisions and design critique.

5. **Integration of design and engineering.** HCI work must negotiate design ideals and engineering constraints. The valuable capacity is cross-functional competence, not design detached from implementation.

6. **Cognitive science foundations.** Cognitive psychology, perception research, human factors, and related fields give HCI a scientific grounding that design intuition alone cannot provide.

7. **Humility about design intuitions.** Empirical evaluation routinely contradicts designer expectations. HCI trains practitioners to treat intuition as a hypothesis, not as sufficient evidence.

8. **Fluency with AI-mediated interaction.** AI-augmented experiences introduce new design challenges: uncertainty, opacity, user calibration, and changing interaction patterns. Contemporary HCI increasingly requires this fluency.

9. **Broader implications of design.** Design affects attention, autonomy, access, and social behavior. HCI practitioners need to see beyond immediate usability metrics.

#### How the field sees the world

Human-computer interaction embeds a worldview that is distinctive in its humanism among CS subjects.

The first commitment is *to taking users seriously*. The HCI worldview holds that design must take users seriously as intelligent agents with purposes, constraints, and dignity, rather than as obstacles to engineering or as objects to be manipulated for business metrics. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that treat user concerns as secondary to technical concerns. The HCI practitioner takes a different view: systems exist to serve users, and design must center user concerns as integral rather than auxiliary. The disposition has consequences for how design is approached and produces work different from alternatives.

The second commitment is *to empirical evaluation of designs*. The HCI worldview holds that design intuitions are unreliable and that empirical evaluation is integral to design rather than auxiliary. This commitment shapes practice: with usability testing as routine practice, with appreciation for what evaluation reveals, with humility about design judgment that has not been validated empirically. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment treat empirical evaluation as integral engineering practice rather than as nice-to-have addition.

The third commitment is *to design for diversity*. The HCI worldview takes seriously that users are diverse — in abilities, cultural backgrounds, technical fluency, various dimensions of variation — and that design must accommodate this diversity. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that design for average users or for users similar to designers. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment design with attention to diversity, treat accessibility as integral, and recognize that designs serving narrow user contexts fail broader populations.

The fourth commitment is *to integration with cognitive science*. The HCI worldview takes seriously that scientific understanding of human cognition, perception, and behavior provides foundation for design, and that design without this scientific grounding is less reliable than design with it. This commitment runs against design dispositions that treat design as exclusively artistic concern. Practitioners who have absorbed it engage with cognitive science literature and integrate scientific understanding into design practice.

The fifth commitment is *to humility about design judgment*. The HCI worldview takes seriously that design intuitions are unreliable, that documented evaluation routinely contradicts designer expectations, and that humility about design judgment is integral to competent practice. This commitment runs against designer dispositions that treat design judgment as professional expertise sufficient on its own. Practitioners who have absorbed it treat design claims with care, validate designs through evaluation, and recognize that designer experience is necessary but insufficient.

The sixth commitment is *to cross-disciplinary engagement*. The HCI worldview takes cross-disciplinary engagement seriously — with psychology, with design fields, with sociology and anthropology, with communication studies, other disciplines. This commitment runs against CS dispositions that prefer to remain within computer science. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with adjacent disciplines as integral to HCI practice, and recognize that HCI is interdisciplinary in ways that other CS subjects are not.

The seventh commitment is *to integration of design with engineering and broader concerns*. The HCI worldview holds that design must integrate productively with engineering and broader product development concerns. This commitment runs against design dispositions that treat design as concern separate from engineering. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with engineering teams, contribute productively to cross-functional development, and recognize that design effectiveness depends on integration.

The eighth commitment is *to responsibility for broader implications*. The HCI worldview takes seriously that design has implications beyond immediate user experience — implications for users' attention, implications for broader social and cultural patterns, other implications. This commitment runs against design dispositions that focus narrowly on immediate metrics. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with broader implications, design with awareness of consequences beyond immediate use, and contribute to design ethics rather than only to design effectiveness in narrow senses.

These eight commitments — to taking users seriously, to empirical evaluation, to designing for diversity, to integration with cognitive science, to humility about design judgment, to cross-disciplinary engagement, to integration of design with engineering, and to responsibility for broader implications — distinguish the HCI worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in HCI as intellectual and design practice rather than as application of UI conventions.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning human-computer interaction are characteristic and distinctive — the integration of design and engineering, the empirical character, and the cross-disciplinary nature produce many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the design-as-decoration misconception*. Parts of public discussion of UI design treat the work as essentially aesthetic — choosing colors, choosing fonts, ensuring visual prettiness. This account misrepresents design work. The intellectual content of HCI extends beyond visual aesthetics to concerns about cognition, behavior, accessibility, and others. The countermeasure is to engage with serious HCI literature: to study cognitive psychology foundations, to engage with usability research, to recognize that visual design is part of HCI but not the whole.

A second pitfall is *the designer-as-user misconception*. Designers are unrepresentative of users — more technically fluent, more familiar with design conventions, differently positioned cognitively in ways. Designers who design for themselves produce work that fails broader user populations. The countermeasure is engagement with users different from designers: user research, usability testing with actual users, humility about designer perspective.

A third pitfall is *the methodology-skipping problem*. HCI depends on empirical methodology, and learners can come to skip methodology — designing without user research, evaluating without methodological rigor, drawing conclusions from inadequate evidence. The result is design work that lacks empirical grounding and that fails users. The countermeasure is engagement with methodology: study of research methods, practice with evaluation methods, appreciation for what methodology reveals that intuition cannot.

A fourth pitfall is *the accessibility-as-addon misconception*. accessibility is integral to HCI rather than addon, but learners can come to treat accessibility as extra concern to be addressed after main design work is complete. The result is designs that are difficult to make accessible, accessibility added late with gaps, users with disabilities failed by the design. The countermeasure is to integrate accessibility from start of design work: study of WCAG and broader accessibility literature, practice with accessibility evaluation tools, engagement with users with disabilities.

A fifth pitfall is *the trends-driven design problem*. design has trends and fashions that influence practice, and learners can come to follow trends without understanding of why specific approaches work or fail. The result is designs that follow trends but that fail users for fundamental reasons. The countermeasure is to engage with timeless design principles alongside contemporary trends: to study Norman, Tognazzini, Nielsen, the classic literature, alongside contemporary developments.

A sixth pitfall is *the engineer-design-divide*. development can suffer from divide between designers and engineers, with design and engineering proceeding without integration. Learners can come to treat this divide as natural and produce work that fits this dysfunctional pattern. The countermeasure is to engage with integration of design and engineering: appreciation for engineering constraints in design, appreciation for design concerns in engineering, work on collaboration patterns that produce integration.

A seventh pitfall is *the metrics-myopia problem*. design can be evaluated by metrics (conversion rates, engagement metrics, and others), and learners can come to optimize for metrics without appreciation for what they actually measure. The result is designs that perform well on metrics but that fail users in ways the metrics do not capture. The countermeasure is to engage with metric limitations: appreciation for what specific metrics measure and what they miss, use of qualitative methods alongside metrics, humility about metric-driven optimization.

An eighth pitfall is *the dark-pattern adoption problem*. design can produce dark patterns — designs that exploit cognitive biases or user limitations to business benefit at user cost. Learners can come to adopt these patterns without recognition of their ethical implications. The countermeasure is engagement with design ethics: Brignull's documentation of dark patterns, various ethical design literature, appreciation that design has ethical dimensions and that designers have responsibility for design choices.

A ninth pitfall is *the narrow-context specialization without breadth*. HCI has diversity of application contexts (mobile, desktop, voice, AR/VR, and others), and learners can come to specialize narrowly in one context while neglecting conceptual content other contexts contribute. The countermeasure is to engage with breadth of HCI contexts, recognizing that transferable lessons emerge from cross-context engagement.

A tenth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied to HCI. Modern AI tools can produce design code, wireframes, design suggestions. The cost in HCI is because competent practice depends on empirical understanding of users that develops through direct engagement, on humility about design intuitions that develops through documented failures, on design judgment that develops through practice. The countermeasure is to use AI tools as supplements to direct engagement with HCI work rather than as replacements for it.

An eleventh pitfall is *the resistance to methodological rigor problem*. design fields have tradition that sometimes resists empirical rigor as foreign to design thinking. Learners can come to adopt this resistance, with result that design work suffers from empirical inadequacy. The countermeasure is appreciation for empirical methods as integral to design rather than as intrusion: recognition that design without empirical grounding is less reliable than design with it, appreciation that methodological rigor and design creativity are compatible.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on human-computer interaction has diverse character. The discipline draws from computer science, psychology, design fields, social sciences, with corresponding diversity of literature. canonical texts have remained valuable across decades, while contemporary literature addresses rapidly evolving application contexts.

##### Canonical entry points

The canonical introduction to HCI as discipline is Norman's *The Design of Everyday Things* (revised and expanded edition, 2013). The book is unusually accessible and deep, articulating fundamental design principles with engaging examples drawn from range of designed objects. The book has canonical status across design and HCI fields, and reading of it is essentially required for HCI engagement. Norman's subsequent books extend his work.

For more academic HCI introduction, Rogers, Sharp, and Preece's *Interaction Design: Beyond Human-Computer Interaction* (sixth edition, 2023) is the standard entry. The book covers breadth of HCI with appropriate depth for foundational study. Dix, Finlay, Abowd, and Beale's *Human-Computer Interaction* (third edition, 2003, free) covers similar material with somewhat older perspective.

For usability engineering specifically, Nielsen's *Usability Engineering* (1993) is foundational despite its age, articulating usability methodology that remains valuable. Krug's *Don't Make Me Think* (third edition, 2014) provides accessible introduction to usability concepts. Tognazzini's various writings on design principles (Tog Magazine and others) are valuable.

For visual and interaction design specifically, Shneiderman, Plaisant, Cohen, Jacobs, Elmqvist, and Diakopoulos's *Designing the User Interface* (sixth edition, 2017) is. Cooper, Reimann, Cronin, and Noessel's *About Face: The Essentials of Interaction Design* (fourth edition, 2014) covers interaction design principles. Tidwell, Brewer, and Valencia's *Designing Interfaces* (third edition, 2020) covers interaction patterns with breadth.

For information architecture, Rosenfeld, Morville, and Arango's *Information Architecture for the Web and Beyond* (fourth edition, 2015) is. Wodtke and Govella's *Information Architecture: Blueprints for the Web* covers similar material more accessibly.

For accessibility specifically, the WCAG documentation (free) is authoritative reference. Horton and Quesenbery's *A Web for Everyone* and various accessibility books provide introduction. The Inclusive Components by Pickering covers component-level accessibility.

For visual design foundations, Lupton's *Thinking with Type* covers typography. Tufte's *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information* (mentioned in Section 6.2) is for information visualization. Bringhurst's *The Elements of Typographic Style* is typography reference.

For cognitive psychology foundations relevant to HCI, Anderson's *Cognitive Psychology and Its Implications* covers foundations. Card, Moran, and Newell's *The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction* is foundational despite its age.

For mobile design specifically, Hoober and Berkman's *Designing Mobile Interfaces* and various Apple Human Interface Guidelines and Google Material Design documentation provide reference.

The reading-order recommendation: Norman as foundational orienting text, Rogers, Sharp, and Preece for academic breadth, Krug for accessible usability introduction, the WCAG material for accessibility, with various specialty references for specific subareas of interest. The Apple Human Interface Guidelines and Google Material Design documentation provide platform-specific reference.

Various courses from major universities (Stanford CS147, Carnegie Mellon's HCI program, and others) provide pedagogical material. The Interaction Design Foundation and various online education platforms provide structured curriculum.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in HCI branches according to specialization.

*Usability research methods at depth.* For learners pursuing usability research seriously, Rubin and Chisnell's *Handbook of Usability Testing* covers usability testing methodology. Tullis and Albert's *Measuring the User Experience* covers UX measurement. Sauro and Lewis's *Quantifying the User Experience* covers quantitative UX methods. Kuniavsky's *Observing the User Experience* covers qualitative methods.

*User research methodology at depth.* For learners pursuing UX research, the qualitative research methodology literature is relevant. Goodman, Kuniavsky, and Moed's *Observing the User Experience* (second edition, 2012) covers UX research methods. Beyer and Holtzblatt's *Contextual Design* covers contextual inquiry. Various ethnography-in-design literature extends the picture.

*Interaction design at depth.* Beyond Cooper et al. and Shneiderman et al., Saffer's *Designing for Interaction* and Buxton's *Sketching User Experiences* cover interaction design with different emphases. Various interaction design pattern libraries (Material Design, Apple HIG, and others) provide pattern reference.

*Information visualization at depth.* The Tufte books, Few's *Show Me the Numbers* and *Now You See It*, Cairo's *The Functional Art*, other information visualization literature constitute deep coverage. intersection with the visualization material in Section 6.2.

*Accessibility at depth.* Beyond WCAG, the Inclusive Design Patterns by Pickering covers component-level patterns. The *Accessibility for Everyone* by Kalbag covers accessibility foundations. The WAI-ARIA documentation covers accessible rich application interfaces.

*Mobile and ubiquitous computing at depth.* Hoober and Berkman cover mobile design fundamentals. The Apple HIG and Google Material Design documentation cover platform-specific concerns. The Weiser papers and the subsequent ubiquitous computing literature cover broader ubiquitous computing concerns.

*AR/VR interaction at depth.* The AR/VR literature overlaps with the graphics material in Section 6.2 but with HCI-specific concerns. The *Immersive Design* by Geszten and various AR/VR design books cover AR/VR-specific HCI. The Oculus, Apple Vision Pro, and other platform documentation provide reference.

*Voice and conversational interaction at depth.* The *Designing Voice User Interfaces* by Pearl, the *Conversational Design* by Hoober, various conversational AI design resources cover voice and conversational interaction. The Amazon Alexa, Google Assistant, and OpenAI/Anthropic API documentation provide platform reference.

*AI-mediated interaction at depth.* The recent literature on AI-mediated interaction is rapidly developing. The Microsoft Human-AI Interaction Guidelines, various papers on designing for AI uncertainty, the work on AI explanation, various ChatGPT and similar interface design discussions constitute the literature. This material integrates with the foundation models material in Section 5.3.

*Information architecture at depth.* Beyond Rosenfeld, Morville, and Arango, Wurman's work, various IA books, the information architecture community publications constitute deeper material.

*Design systems at depth.* The design systems literature has emerged over the past decade. Frost's *Atomic Design* (free) is foundational. Various design system documentation (Material Design system documentation, IBM Carbon, Salesforce Lightning, and others) provides concrete examples. Curtis's *Modular Web Design* covers component-based design.

*Evaluation methods at depth.* Beyond the standard texts, Nielsen's heuristic evaluation work, the cognitive walkthrough literature, other evaluation methodology constitutes deeper material.

*Design ethics at depth.* The design ethics literature is broad. Brignull's dark patterns work (deceptive.design, free) is central. Monteiro's *Ruined by Design* covers design ethics provocatively. Various ethical design literature extends the picture.

*Cognitive psychology for HCI at depth.* Beyond Anderson, the cognitive psychology literature relevant to HCI is extensive. Card, Moran, and Newell remain valuable despite age. The GOMS family of analyses covers cognitive modeling for HCI. Sweller's cognitive load theory work and subsequent literature is useful.

*Cross-cultural design at depth.* The international and cross-cultural design literature includes Marcus and Gould's foundational work on Hofstede's cultural dimensions in design, various i18n/l10n technical literature, various papers on designing for diverse cultural contexts.

*HCI research at depth.* For learners pursuing HCI research, the CHI conference proceedings (largely free) constitute research literature. CSCW, UIST, and other ACM HCI venues cover subareas. Various HCI journals (TOCHI, IJHCS, HCI, Interacting with Computers) provide peer-reviewed venues.

##### Reference works

Norman's *The Design of Everyday Things* serves as reference work that practitioners consult throughout careers.

The Apple Human Interface Guidelines and Google Material Design documentation constitute authoritative reference for the dominant mobile and cross-platform design systems.

The WCAG documentation constitutes authoritative reference for accessibility standards.

The WAI-ARIA documentation constitutes authoritative reference for accessible rich applications.

Rogers, Sharp, and Preece's *Interaction Design* serves as reference for breadth of HCI.

Various platform Human Interface Guidelines (Microsoft Fluent, Apple HIG, Google Material Design) constitute platform-specific reference.

The Nielsen Norman Group articles (nngroup.com, much free) constitute practitioner reference for usability and UX topics.

The UX research method documentation (usability testing, card sorting, and others) constitutes methodological reference.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within HCI and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The critical perspective on mainstream UX.* critical writing questions whether mainstream UX practice serves users or serves business interests at user cost. Various papers and writings on dark patterns, on deceptive design, on attention economy critiques constitute heterodox literature with intellectual content.

*The slow-design and humane-design perspective.* work argues that mainstream contemporary design optimizes for engagement metrics in ways that harm users, and that alternative design philosophies (slow design, humane technology, and others) would better serve users. The Center for Humane Technology and various humane design writings constitute heterodox literature.

*The anti-design-thinking perspective.* The design thinking methodology has been fashionable in business contexts, and critical writing questions whether design thinking produces value or serves as corporate ritual. Various critical writings on design thinking constitute heterodox literature.

*The accessibility-first perspective.* Various practitioners argue that accessibility should be design starting point rather than concern integrated with broader design, with argument that accessibility-first produces better designs for broader populations. The inclusive design literature articulates this position.

*The textual interface perspective.* work argues that mainstream graphical user interface paradigm has limitations and that textual and command-line interfaces better serve expert users. The Unix command-line tradition and various critiques of GUI dominance constitute heterodox literature.

*The design-as-political-act perspective.* work positions design as fundamentally political, with designers having responsibility for broader implications of design choices. Costanza-Chock's *Design Justice* and subsequent work in this tradition constitute heterodox literature with intellectual content.

*The radical disability studies perspective.* work questions whether mainstream accessibility approaches serve people with disabilities or serve others' comfort with accessibility while failing disabled users. Various disability studies writings constitute heterodox literature.

*The AI-skeptical perspective on AI-mediated interaction.* work questions whether AI integration into interaction serves users or serves AI deployment incentives. Various critical writings on AI assistants and chatbots constitute heterodox literature.

##### What to skip and why

Books on UI design published before approximately 2010 should be approached with caution as primary references for contemporary practice, with exceptions of foundational books (Norman, Card-Moran-Newell, and others that retain real value despite age).

Most marketing-driven books on specific UX methodologies should be approached as practical references rather than as foundational learning. The genre exists and is uneven.

Books on design that present specific aesthetic styles as universally appropriate should be supplemented with perspective on style trade-offs. styles serve purposes but no style is universally appropriate.

Books on UX that abstract over empirical methodology should be approached with caution. UX requires empirical engagement.

Most certification preparation books in UX should be approached as certification preparation rather than as real learning.

Most online UX tutorials are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the Nielsen Norman Group articles, the Smashing Magazine UX content, the well-maintained materials from design organizations — are valuable. The typical tutorial covers introductory mechanics without depth.

Books on AI-mediated interaction that predate foundation models are dated for contemporary practice.

Books on mobile design from early mobile era (pre-2015) are dated.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary HCI research include conferences. CHI is the primary HCI conference. CSCW covers computer-supported cooperative work. UIST covers user interface software and technology. Various other ACM HCI venues cover subareas. Their proceedings are portions free.

Various HCI journals (TOCHI, IJHCS, HCI, Interacting with Computers) provide peer-reviewed venues.

Several blogs and online resources provide ongoing engagement. The Nielsen Norman Group articles (nngroup.com) provide ongoing UX content. The UX Collective publications provide ongoing content. Various practitioner blogs from prominent designers provide ongoing perspective.

Various conferences (UX conferences, design conferences, and others) have talks freely available.

Various practitioner-focused podcasts (UX Podcast, Design Better, and others) provide audio content.

Various design newsletters provide curated coverage.

Various UX research community resources (UXR Conf, various UX research community publications) provide real content.

Various platform documentation updates (Material Design updates, Apple HIG updates) provide ongoing reference.

Various AI-HCI integration resources (Microsoft Human-AI Interaction Guidelines, OpenAI cookbook, various AI design resources) provide contemporary content.
