| UX Collective publications | Contemporary UX writing | Current, ongoing |
| UX Podcast / Design Better podcasts | Audio practitioner content | Current, sampling |
| Pre-2010 UI design books | Often dated | Skip (with foundational exceptions) |
| Marketing-driven UX methodology books | Mixed quality | Skip (in favor of canonical sources) |
| UX certification prep books as real learning | Optimize for certification | Skip (as primary useful references) |
| Pre-foundation-model AI-HCI books | dated | Skip (in favor of current sources) |
| Pre-2015 mobile design books | dated | Skip (in favor of current sources) |
| Aesthetic-style-only design books | Limited utility as foundation | Skip (in favor of serious treatments) |

#### A note on contemporary relevance

Human-computer interaction is one of the more active application domains in this map. Current practice is being pulled in several directions at once:

1. **AI-mediated interaction is changing interface design.** Conversational interfaces, AI assistants, and AI-augmented applications have introduced new questions about uncertainty, explanation, control, trust, and recoverability. The design conventions are still unstable.
2. **Accessibility has moved toward mainstream practice.** Accessibility is increasingly expected in ordinary product work, and regulatory pressure such as the European Accessibility Act raises the cost of treating it as a late-stage specialty.
3. **Design systems have become infrastructure.** Mature organizations now use design systems to coordinate design, engineering, accessibility, brand, and product consistency across large surfaces.
4. **Mobile remains a primary context of use.** Mobile-first design has become ordinary practice, but the constraint of small screens, intermittent attention, and touch interaction still shapes interface decisions.
5. **AR, VR, and spatial computing have reopened interaction questions.** Apple Vision Pro, Meta Quest, and related platforms make spatial interfaces more visible, though their long-term role remains uncertain.
6. **Dark patterns and attention extraction have become central ethical concerns.** Work on humane design, slow design, and deceptive-pattern documentation responds to interfaces that optimize engagement at user cost.
7. **Inclusive and equity-oriented design has broadened the field's obligations.** Design justice and inclusive-design movements push HCI beyond average-user assumptions.
8. **Design ethics is no longer optional background.** Interface choices can shape autonomy, privacy, labor, accessibility, and social participation.
9. **UX research methods are under pressure.** Sample diversity, overreliance on controlled experiments, and the difficulty of evaluating AI-mediated systems have all forced methodological reflection.

Despite these developments, the foundational character of HCI is robust. The core concerns — designing for human cognition, designing for diversity, evaluating designs empirically, interaction across contexts, integration with broader concerns — remain as articulated decades ago. The contemporary developments add to this foundation rather than replacing it.

Among the application chapters, HCI is especially dependent on non-CS disciplines: cognitive psychology, design, social science, accessibility work, and communication studies all matter. Its difficulty comes from this integration. Good HCI work requires empirical judgment, design judgment, engineering judgment, and a willingness to take responsibility for how design choices affect users and society.

### 6.5 — Robotics

#### What it is, properly

Robotics is the discipline concerned with computational systems that perceive their environments and take physical actions in those environments — integration of perception (sensing the world), computation (processing sensed information and deciding what to do), and actuation (acting physically on the world) in ways that produce autonomous or semi-autonomous behavior. The subject treats embodied computation as intellectual and engineering work, with characteristic mathematical content (work on kinematics and dynamics, control theory, probabilistic robotics, estimation theory), characteristic engineering content (systems engineering across hardware and software, work on real-time systems, work on the integration of subsystems), characteristic application domains (industrial robotics, mobile robotics, manipulation, autonomous vehicles, surgical robotics, and others), and intersection with machine learning (work on learning-based robotics, deep reinforcement learning for robotics, recent foundation-model-augmented robotics). The discipline has historical depth, with development from the 1960s onward through industrial robotics, subsequent development through mobile robotics and autonomous systems, and contemporary development driven by machine learning advances and by maturation of sensors and computational hardware.

The conventional account treats robotics as either industrial automation (the robot arms doing repetitive tasks in factories) or future-AI fantasy (the humanoid robots of science fiction). Both accounts misrepresent contemporary robotics. The conceptual core is the recognition that *embodied computation faces challenges that disembodied computation does not*, that *physical interaction with uncertain environments produces difficulties throughout the engineering*, that *integration across subsystems is integral rather than auxiliary*, and that *robotics combines mathematical, engineering, and increasingly machine learning concerns in distinctive ways*. These recognitions distinguish robotics engineering from the surface accounts that introductory materials sometimes provide.

A more honest account distinguishes several distinct concerns within the broader subject. *Kinematics and dynamics* is the subdiscipline concerned with the mathematics of how robots move — work on forward and inverse kinematics, work on Jacobians and singularities, work on dynamics (how forces and torques produce motion), work on various coordinate frames and transformations that robotic computation depends on. *Control* is the subdiscipline concerned with making robots execute desired motions — classical control (PID and others), modern control (state-space methods, optimal control, and others), robust control for handling uncertainty, adaptive control, the work on contemporary learning-based control. *Perception* is the subdiscipline concerned with making sense of sensor data — computer vision for visual perception, work on lidar and other 3D sensing, sensor fusion across multiple sensor modalities, work on object recognition, scene understanding, and other perception tasks. *State estimation and SLAM* covers the work on estimating robot state and environment state from sensor data — Kalman filtering and various extensions, particle filters, simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM), visual-inertial odometry, other estimation techniques. *Planning* is the subdiscipline concerned with deciding what actions to take — motion planning (finding paths through configuration spaces), task planning (deciding what high-level actions to perform), trajectory optimization, work on planning under uncertainty (POMDPs and various approaches), work on multi-robot planning. *Manipulation* covers the work on robots manipulating objects — work on grasping, work on contact-rich manipulation, work on compliant manipulation, other manipulation concerns. *Mobile robotics* covers concerns specific to robots that move through environments — wheeled robots, legged robots, flying robots (drones, and others), underwater robots. *Human-robot interaction* covers work on robots interacting with humans — collaborative robots in industrial contexts, assistive robotics, social robotics, concerns about human-robot communication. *Robot learning* covers the work on robots that learn from experience — learning from demonstration, reinforcement learning for robotics, sim-to-real transfer, the recent work on foundation-model-augmented robotics. *Multi-robot systems* covers concerns of systems with multiple coordinating robots. *Robot software architectures* covers systems engineering of robotic software — ROS (Robot Operating System), real-time concerns, work on system-level architectures for robotics. *Specific application domains* (industrial robotics, autonomous vehicles, surgical robotics, agricultural robotics, and others) each have specific concerns alongside the broader principles.

The intellectual content of robotics is distinctive because it joins mathematical modeling, software, hardware, and physical deployment. Linear algebra, probability, optimization, and control theory are central. Robotics also integrates computer science with mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, and control engineering. Robots are cyber-physical systems, so simulation is useful but never decisive: deployment routinely exposes issues that simulation misses. The field's history of enthusiasm and disappointment also matters, especially when evaluating new claims about general-purpose robots and foundation models for robotics.

The reason this account matters is that robotics has become central to parts of contemporary computing while remaining technically challenging. The deployment of robotic systems in consequential contexts (industrial automation, autonomous vehicles, and others) produces demand for competent robotics engineering. The gap between robotics enthusiasm in popular discussion and technical realities of robotics engineering produces confusion that competent practitioners must navigate. The discipline of robotics as serious engineering practice differs from robotics fantasies, and competent work requires preparation that popular accounts often understate.

#### The deep problems the field tries to answer

Five problems organize robotics as a discipline.

**The problem of operating with uncertainty about the world.** Robots operate in environments that they perceive imperfectly — sensors are noisy, environments contain unmodeled features, actions have unintended consequences, dynamics are uncertain. The difficulty has implications throughout robotics. The discipline includes work on this problem: the probabilistic robotics tradition (Thrun, Burgard, and Fox's *Probabilistic Robotics* and the subsequent literature), work on Bayesian estimation, work on planning under uncertainty (POMDPs and various approximations), work on robust control, work on sensor fusion. The intellectual content includes mathematical content (probability theory, estimation theory, decision theory under uncertainty) and methodological content about how to build systems that work despite uncertainty. The contemporary work on this problem continues actively, with recent advances in learning-based approaches that handle uncertainty differently than traditional probabilistic approaches.

**The problem of real-time computation.** Robots must operate in real time — decisions must be made on timescales matching the physical dynamics of the environment, with implications throughout the systems engineering. The difficulty arises because perception, planning, and control all have computational requirements, and real-time constraints shape what computations are feasible. The discipline includes work on this problem: the real-time systems engineering (overlapping with Section 4.8), work on computational structures appropriate to robotics, work on hardware acceleration for robotics, work on trade-offs between accuracy and computational cost. The intellectual content includes systems engineering content and appreciation for what real-time constraints actually require.

**The problem of integration across subsystems.** robots have subsystems — sensors, perception, state estimation, planning, control, actuation — and integration across these subsystems is integral to robot operation. The difficulty arises because subsystems have dependencies on each other, errors compound across subsystems, and integration problems can be difficult to diagnose. The discipline includes work on this problem: work on robot software architectures (ROS and various alternatives), work on system-level analysis and testing, work on the integration testing methodology that robotics requires. The intellectual content includes systems engineering and methodological content.

**The problem of physical interaction with environments.** Robots interact physically with environments — environments push back, contacts have implications, physical dynamics are. The difficulty arises because physical interaction is more complex than pure computation, with implications for what tasks robots can and cannot reliably perform. The discipline includes work on this problem: work on contact-rich manipulation, work on compliant control, work on impedance control, work on physical interaction safety. The contemporary work on dexterous manipulation continues actively, with recent advances through learning-based approaches that handle physical interaction differently than traditional analytical approaches.

**The problem of transfer from simulation to reality.** parts of robotics development happen in simulation — training of learned policies, development of planning algorithms, testing of system integration. But deployment to real robots routinely reveals issues that simulation did not capture: sensor characteristics that simulation modeled inadequately, physical dynamics that simulation approximated, environmental variation that simulation did not include. The difficulty has implications for methodology. The discipline includes work on this problem: domain randomization (training in varied simulated environments to produce policies robust to reality), system identification (calibrating simulation to match reality), work on sim-to-real transfer specifically, work on real-world testing methodology. The intellectual content includes methodological content about how simulation and reality should be integrated in development.

#### Where it sits in the map

Robotics depends on preparation across multiple regions of the map. From the foundational trunk: algorithms and data structures (Section 2.7) provides foundation, particularly work on graph algorithms used in planning. Computer architecture (Section 2.4) is relevant for real-time computation concerns. Operating systems (Section 2.5) is relevant through real-time operating systems. Programming as conceptual practice (Section 2.1) is foundational.

From the mathematical substrate (Chapter 7): mathematical preparation is essential. Linear algebra is foundational and load-bearing throughout — parts of robotics involve linear algebra applied to geometric and dynamical problems. Calculus and differential equations are foundational for dynamics and control. Probability theory is foundational for probabilistic robotics. Optimization theory is foundational for planning and control. work on Lie groups and Lie algebras is increasingly for modern robotics through geometric formulations.

From the computation axis: programming language theory (Section 3.1) and various subjects are relevant for robotic software systems.

From the systems axis: the engagement is strong. Embedded and real-time systems (Section 4.8) is relevant — robotics is embedded systems engineering with real-time concerns. High-performance computing (Section 4.9) is relevant through computational requirements of perception and planning. Modern compilers and language runtimes (Section 4.7) is relevant through robot software systems.

From the intelligence axis: the engagement is active and growing. Machine learning foundations (Section 5.1) is relevant. Deep learning (Section 5.2) is increasingly relevant through deep learning approaches in robotics. Reinforcement learning (Section 5.4) is relevant: parts of contemporary robot learning use reinforcement learning. Foundation models (Section 5.3) are increasingly relevant through recent foundation-model-augmented robotics. AI safety (Section 5.7) is relevant through robot safety concerns.

From the data axis: the engagement is through robot sensor data and training data for learned components.

From other application domains: the relationship to graphics (Section 6.2) is through robotics simulation. The relationship to security engineering (Section 6.3) is increasingly through concerns about robotic system security. The relationship to HCI (Section 6.4) is through human-robot interaction.

From outside computer science: the relationships are unusually broad. Mechanical engineering provides foundation for robot mechanical design. Electrical engineering provides foundation for robot electrical systems. Control engineering provides foundation for control work — parts of robotics control share foundations with broader control engineering. Mechatronics provides integrative foundation. Mathematics broadly is central, with robotics using mathematics across mathematical subdisciplines. Physics provides foundation. Cognitive science and neuroscience matter through work on biologically-inspired robotics and work on cognitive architectures for robotics.

The position within this map is characteristic of application development: engagement with the system axis (embedded systems engineering), engagement with the computation axis (mathematical and algorithmic content), moderate engagement with the data axis (sensor data engineering), and increasingly engagement with the intelligence axis (learning-based robotics, foundation models for robotics). The discipline is configurational and integrative, with integration across CS axes and integration with non-CS engineering disciplines.

#### What you become after studying it

A reader who has internalized robotics acquires several capacities that distinguish them from practitioners who treat robotics as collection of techniques.

1. **Fluency with robotics mathematics.** Robotics depends on linear algebra, probability theory, control theory, and optimization. This mathematical fluency transfers across specific robotics contexts and remains useful as techniques change.

2. **Physical reality.** Robotics is about machines acting in the physical world. Physical constraints, contact, uncertainty, hardware limits, and sim-to-real gaps are part of the subject, not implementation annoyances.

3. **Integration of perception, planning, and control.** Complete robotic systems require perception, planning, and control to work together. Treating them as separable topics leaves the central engineering problem untouched.

4. **Fluency with probabilistic reasoning.** Bayesian estimation, probabilistic planning, and reasoning under uncertainty are central because robots rarely receive clean, complete information.

5. **Fluency with real-time systems engineering.** Robotic systems have timing constraints. Practitioners need to identify bottlenecks, reason about latency, and apply optimization techniques appropriate to embodied systems.

6. **Diversity of robotics application domains.** Industrial robotics, autonomous vehicles, manipulation, mobile robotics, and other domains have different requirements. Robotics practice must adapt to the application context.

7. **Fluency with contemporary learning-based robotics.** Learning-based approaches are increasingly important, but they have strengths and limits. Robotics practitioners need to know what these methods do well and where they fail.

8. **Historical and conceptual perspective.** Robotics has a long record of approaches tried, successes achieved, and failures repeated. Historical perspective helps distinguish genuinely new developments from recurring optimism.

9. **Broader implications.** Robotic deployment affects workers, safety, public spaces, and social institutions. Robotics cannot be evaluated only by technical performance.

#### How the field sees the world

Robotics embeds a worldview that has emerged over decades of practice with physical systems.

The first commitment is *to physical reality as important*. The robotics worldview holds that engineering for physical systems differs from engineering for purely computational systems, with physical reality producing constraints that pure computation does not face. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that abstract over physical concerns. The roboticist takes a different view: physical concerns are integral, with implications throughout engineering.

The second commitment is *to humility about systems*. The robotics worldview takes seriously documented record of robotic systems failing in unanticipated ways, of promised capabilities not materializing, of gap between demonstration and reliable operation. This commitment shapes practice: with humility about capability claims, with respect for difficulty of producing reliable robotic systems, with appreciation that demonstration in controlled conditions is insufficient for reliable deployment.

The third commitment is *to integration as integral engineering*. The robotics worldview holds that integration across subsystems is integral engineering work rather than auxiliary. This commitment shapes how problems are approached: with attention to subsystem interactions, with appreciation that errors compound across subsystems, with recognition that integration testing is essential.

The fourth commitment is *to probabilistic thinking under uncertainty*. The robotics worldview takes seriously uncertainty about sensor measurements, environment models, action outcomes, with implications for how engineering must proceed. This commitment shapes practice: with probabilistic reasoning throughout, with appreciation for what probability provides for robotics, with humility about deterministic models.

The fifth commitment is *to empirical engineering*. The robotics worldview takes seriously that parts of effective practice depend on empirical observations about what works in reality, that theoretical reasoning often underdetermines what deployment requires, and that methodology depends on empirical observation including real-world testing. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer pure simulation. Practitioners who have absorbed it treat real-world testing as integral.

The sixth commitment is *to real-time engineering*. The robotics worldview takes seriously real-time constraints as important rather than as implementation detail. This commitment shapes how problems are approached: with attention to computational requirements, with appreciation for what real-time constraints actually require, with methodology for measuring and addressing real-time concerns.

The seventh commitment is *to cross-disciplinary engagement*. The robotics worldview takes cross-disciplinary engagement seriously — with mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, control engineering, mathematics, physics. This commitment runs against engineering dispositions that prefer to remain within computer science narrowly understood. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with adjacent engineering disciplines as integral to robotics practice.

The eighth commitment is *to appreciation for cycles in robotics history*. The robotics worldview takes seriously cycles of enthusiasm and disappointment that have characterized robotics development, with implications for how contemporary claims should be evaluated. Practitioners who have absorbed this commitment engage with contemporary developments rather than reactively, recognizing that cycles are likely to continue.

The ninth commitment is *to responsibility for deployment*. The robotics worldview takes seriously implications of robotic deployment for people who interact with robots — workers in industrial contexts, people in spaces where autonomous vehicles operate, other people affected by deployment. This commitment shapes how engineering proceeds, with attention to safety concerns, ergonomic concerns, other concerns that deployment introduces.

These nine commitments — to physical reality, to humility about systems, to integration as integral engineering, to probabilistic thinking, to empirical engineering, to real-time engineering, to cross-disciplinary engagement, to appreciation for cycles in robotics history, and to responsibility for deployment — distinguish the robotics worldview from adjacent dispositions. Practitioners who have absorbed them work in robotics as professional engineering practice rather than as application of demos.

#### Common pitfalls in learning it

The pitfalls of learning robotics are characteristic and distinctive — the integration character, the mathematical content, and the gap between simulation and reality produce many opportunities for misdirected learning.

The most common pitfall is *the simulation-only practice*. robotics development happens in simulation, and learners can come to develop simulation competence while remaining unprepared for real-world deployment. The result is work that performs well in simulation but fails in deployment. The countermeasure is engagement with real robots: work with real hardware supplementing simulation work, appreciation for sim-to-real gaps, methodology that integrates simulation and real-world testing.

A second pitfall is *the math-avoidance trap*. Robotics has mathematical content — linear algebra, probability theory, control theory, optimization — and learners can come to avoid the mathematics, relying on libraries to handle the mathematical content. The result is practitioners who can use robotic libraries for standard tasks but cannot reason about what they are doing, cannot adapt techniques to non-standard problems, cannot diagnose issues whose cause is mathematical. The countermeasure is to engage with the mathematics seriously: to study the probabilistic robotics, the control theory, the linear algebra in robotics contexts, recognizing that robotics competence requires mathematical preparation.

A third pitfall is *the perception-only or planning-only or control-only specialization without integration*. robotics requires integration across subsystems, and learners can come to specialize narrowly in one subsystem without appreciation for how it fits into complete systems. The result is work that performs well in isolation but fails to integrate. The countermeasure is work on complete robotic systems: integration of perception, planning, and control even when primary specialization is narrow, appreciation for subsystem interactions.

A fourth pitfall is *the demo-driven understanding without engineering depth*. Robotics has visible demonstrations — videos of impressive demonstrations widely circulate — and learners can come to overestimate robotic capabilities based on demonstrations without engineering context. The gap between cherry-picked demonstrations and reliable deployment is large. The countermeasure is engineering engagement: appreciation for what reliable deployment actually requires, humility about what demonstrations show, engagement with documented record of systems that demonstrated well but deployed poorly.

A fifth pitfall is *the framework-as-discipline misconception applied to ROS*. Robot Operating System (ROS) is software framework for robotics, and learners can come to treat learning ROS as the substance of learning robotics. The result is practitioners who can use ROS for standard tasks but cannot reason about underlying concepts. The countermeasure is to engage with concepts that ROS implements: to study messaging and computation graph concepts, to engage with alternative architectures, to recognize that ROS knowledge is valuable but insufficient as foundation.

A sixth pitfall is *the under-appreciation of physical intuition*. robotics requires physical intuition — appreciation for mechanical behavior, appreciation for sensor characteristics, appreciation for real-world dynamics. Learners can come to develop computational competence without physical intuition. The countermeasure is hands-on work with physical systems: building of robots, debugging of real hardware, development of physical intuition through direct engagement.

A seventh pitfall is *the safety-as-afterthought disposition*. robots can harm people — industrial robots have track record of industrial accidents, autonomous vehicles have documented incidents, collaborative robots have safety concerns. Learners can come to treat safety as concern to be addressed after primary engineering, with result that safety has gaps. The countermeasure is to integrate safety from start of robotics work: study of robotic safety standards, appreciation for safety implications of robot deployment, methodology that treats safety as integral.

An eighth pitfall is *the optimism-driven practice*. Robotics has history of optimistic claims that fail to materialize, and learners can come to absorb optimism without historical context. The result is work that overpromises and underdelivers. The countermeasure is historical engagement: study of robotics history including documented failures, appreciation for cycles of enthusiasm and disappointment, humility about contemporary capability claims.

A ninth pitfall is *the LLM-shortcut problem* as applied to robotics. Modern AI tools can produce robotics code, can answer robotics questions. The cost is in robotics because competent practice depends on physical intuition that develops through hands-on engagement, on integration competence that develops through complete-system work, on appreciation for reality versus simulation that develops through real-world testing. The countermeasure is to use AI tools as supplements to direct engagement rather than as replacements.

A tenth pitfall is *the AI-everything trap*. recent enthusiasm for learning-based robotics can produce expectation that machine learning will solve all robotics problems, with result that classical approaches are neglected when they would better serve specific applications. The classical robotics approaches (planning algorithms, classical control, probabilistic estimation, and others) work well for application categories. The countermeasure is appreciation for appropriate use of different approaches: classical methods for problems where they work well, learning methods where they provide unique value, integration of approaches when integration benefits.

An eleventh pitfall is *the deployment-naive practice*. robotics research can proceed without engagement with deployment realities — cost concerns, maintenance concerns, regulatory concerns, various deployment realities. Learners can come to develop research competence without deployment understanding. The countermeasure is engagement with deployment: attention to deployment concerns from early in learning, appreciation for what deployment actually requires beyond technical performance.

#### The literature of the subject

The literature on robotics has a diverse character. The discipline has canonical texts that remain valuable, contemporary literature addressing recent developments particularly in learning-based robotics, and diversity across robotics subspecialties. Engagement requires fluency with this diverse literature.

##### Canonical entry points

The canonical introduction to robotics as integrated discipline is Siciliano, Sciavicco, Villani, and Oriolo's *Robotics: Modelling, Planning and Control* (2009). The book covers breadth of foundational robotics with mathematical depth, providing preparation for serious robotics study. The book has canonical status across robotics programs.

For probabilistic robotics specifically, Thrun, Burgard, and Fox's *Probabilistic Robotics* (2005) is the standard text. The book covers probabilistic foundations of robotics with depth, particularly work on state estimation and localization. The book remains foundational despite age, with subsequent literature building on its foundations.

For control specifically, Spong, Hutchinson, and Vidyasagar's *Robot Modeling and Control* (second edition, 2020) covers robot control with depth. Slotine and Li's *Applied Nonlinear Control* covers broader nonlinear control that robotics depends on.

For modern robotics with contemporary perspective, Lynch and Park's *Modern Robotics: Mechanics, Planning, and Control* (2017, freely available online) provides accessible introduction with mathematical care. The book is unusually well-pedagogically structured and is freely available with accompanying software, making it appropriate primary text for contemporary robotics study.

For motion planning specifically, LaValle's *Planning Algorithms* (2006, freely available) is the standard reference. The book covers planning at depth with mathematical care.

For computer vision in robotics specifically, Hartley and Zisserman's *Multiple View Geometry in Computer Vision* (second edition, 2003) covers geometric computer vision foundational for visual perception in robotics. Szeliski's *Computer Vision: Algorithms and Applications* (mentioned in Section 5.2) provides broader computer vision coverage.

For learners interested in robotic manipulation specifically, Mason's *Mechanics of Robotic Manipulation* covers manipulation foundations. Murray, Li, and Sastry's *A Mathematical Introduction to Robotic Manipulation* (1994, freely available) covers mathematical foundations of manipulation.

The reading-order recommendation depends on focus area. For comprehensive foundation: Lynch and Park as foundational text given accessibility and freely available status, supplemented by Siciliano et al. and Thrun et al. for depth in specific areas. For learners pursuing specific subareas: specialty-specific resources alongside foundational material.

Various robotics courses at major universities (MIT 6.832, Stanford CS223A, CMU 16-XXX, Berkeley CS287, and others) provide pedagogical material with freely available content. The Coursera and edX robotics specializations provide structured curriculum.

##### The progression

After the canonical introduction, the progression in robotics branches according to specialization.

*Kinematics and dynamics at depth.* For learners pursuing kinematics and dynamics seriously, Featherstone's *Rigid Body Dynamics Algorithms* covers efficient algorithms. The papers on Lie group formulations of robot kinematics extend mathematical depth.

*Control at depth.* Beyond Spong et al. and Slotine-Li, the broader control literature is relevant. Khalil's *Nonlinear Systems* covers nonlinear control. Various optimal control texts cover optimal control. Various MPC (Model Predictive Control) literature covers MPC for robotics. The papers on contemporary reinforcement-learning-based control extend the picture.

*Probabilistic robotics at depth.* Beyond Thrun et al., the subsequent literature on Bayesian filtering, on particle filters, on graph-based SLAM (Grisetti et al. and others), on visual-inertial estimation extends the picture. Stachniss's online courses provide educational complement.

*SLAM at depth.* Cadena et al.'s "Past, Present, and Future of SLAM" survey covers SLAM evolution. The papers on graph-based SLAM (g2o, GTSAM, and others), on visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM and the subsequent work), on LiDAR SLAM constitute deeper material.

*Motion planning at depth.* Beyond LaValle, the papers on RRT and PRM variants, on trajectory optimization (CHOMP, TrajOpt, and others), on planning under uncertainty constitute deeper material.

*Manipulation at depth.* Beyond Mason and Murray-Li-Sastry, the recent literature on dexterous manipulation, on learning-based manipulation, on bimanual manipulation, on various manipulation challenges constitutes active research.

*Mobile robotics at depth.* Siegwart, Nourbakhsh, and Scaramuzza's *Introduction to Autonomous Mobile Robots* (second edition, 2011) covers mobile robotics foundations. The papers on wheeled robots, on legged robots (the Boston Dynamics work, the ANYmal work, the subsequent work), on flying robots (drones, and others) extend the picture.

*Autonomous vehicles at depth.* The autonomous vehicles literature is extensive. The Waymo, Cruise, and various other technical publications, the DARPA Urban Challenge papers, various academic papers on autonomous driving constitute literature. Work on perception for autonomous vehicles, on behavior prediction, on planning for autonomous driving extends the picture.

*Robot learning at depth.* The recent literature on robot learning is rapidly developing. Levine et al.'s "End-to-End Training of Deep Visuomotor Policies" and the subsequent work cover deep learning for robotics. The papers on sim-to-real transfer, on domain randomization, on imitation learning for robotics, on reinforcement learning for robotics, on the recent work on foundation-model-augmented robotics constitute the contemporary research literature.

*Foundation models for robotics at depth.* The recent literature on foundation models for robotics is rapidly developing. The RT-1, RT-2 papers from Google DeepMind, the PaLM-E paper, various contemporary papers on vision-language-action models, various papers on code-generating models for robotics constitute the recent literature.

*Human-robot interaction at depth.* The HRI literature has breadth. Goodrich and Schultz's "Human-Robot Interaction: A Survey" provides introduction. The papers on collaborative robots (cobots), on assistive robotics, on social robotics extend the picture.

*Multi-robot systems at depth.* The multi-robot literature includes papers on multi-robot coordination, on multi-robot SLAM, on swarm robotics. The intersection with multi-agent systems and with multi-agent reinforcement learning is active.

*Robot software architectures at depth.* Beyond the ROS documentation, papers on robot software architectures, on real-time robotics, on systems engineering for robotics constitute literature. ROS 2 documentation extends ROS.

*Specific application domains at depth.* Each application domain has sub-literature. Industrial robotics has literature on industrial robot programming, collaborative robotics, flexible automation. Surgical robotics has literature on surgical robot design and clinical applications. Agricultural robotics, construction robotics, other domains each have their own sub-literatures.

*Robot ethics at depth.* The robot ethics literature has development. Lin, Abney, and Bekey's *Robot Ethics* and the subsequent work cover ethical questions. Various papers on autonomous weapon systems, on labor displacement implications, on broader ethical concerns constitute literature.

##### Reference works

Lynch and Park's *Modern Robotics* (free) serves as foundational reference with mathematical clarity.

Siciliano et al.'s *Robotics* serves as reference work for breadth.

Thrun, Burgard, Fox's *Probabilistic Robotics* serves as reference for probabilistic foundations.

LaValle's *Planning Algorithms* (free) serves as reference for planning.

The ROS documentation (free) constitutes reference for the dominant robotic software framework.

The OpenAI Gym (now Gymnasium) and Isaac Gym documentation constitute reference for RL-for-robotics environments.

Various IEEE robotics journals (IEEE Transactions on Robotics, IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, the others) constitute peer-reviewed reference.

The IROS, ICRA, RSS, CoRL conferences constitute primary research venues with proceedings.

##### Heterodox correctives

Several positions are heterodox correctives within robotics and worth engagement for the perspective they provide.

*The skeptical perspective on autonomous vehicle timelines.* work questions whether autonomous vehicle deployment will materialize on timelines that have been repeatedly promised. Various papers and writings on autonomous driving difficulties, on documented failures, on gap between demonstration and reliable deployment constitute heterodox literature.

*The classical robotics perspective on learning-based enthusiasm.* practitioners argue that recent enthusiasm for learning-based robotics has overshadowed classical robotics methods that work better for application categories. The argument has proponents and intellectual content. Engagement with this perspective gives perspective on what appropriate use of different approaches looks like.

*The embodiment-centric perspective.* work in robotics emphasizes that intelligence is fundamentally embodied — that cognitive capabilities arise from physical interaction with environments rather than from disembodied computation. Brooks's "Intelligence Without Reason" (mentioned in Section 5.5) articulates this perspective, and subsequent work has developed it. Engagement gives perspective on relationship between embodiment and intelligence.

*The "robotics has been overpromised for decades" perspective.* The documented record of robotic capability claims that failed to materialize has produced skeptical literature. The historical reviews and critical writings constitute heterodox literature. The implications for how contemporary claims should be evaluated are important.

*The labor and political economy perspective.* work questions implications of robotic deployment for workers and broader political economy. The papers on labor displacement, on concentration of robotic ownership, on broader political implications constitute heterodox literature with intellectual content.

*The "sim-to-real has fundamental limitations" perspective.* Various practitioners argue that sim-to-real transfer has fundamental limitations that methodology cannot fully address. The argument has implications for what development methodology should look like.

*The disability studies perspective on assistive robotics.* work questions assumptions of assistive robotics — questioning whether robotic solutions serve disabled people or serve others' assumptions about what disabled people need. Engagement with disability studies literature gives perspective on design assumptions.

##### What to skip and why

Books on robotics published before approximately 2010 should be approached as historical references rather than as guides to contemporary practice, with exceptions of foundational books (Murray-Li-Sastry, Mason, and others that retain value).

Marketing-driven books on future robotics that conflate speculation with engineering should be approached with caution. documented record of unfulfilled robotic promises makes speculative writing about robotics future unreliable.

Books on specific old robotic platforms or old robotic frameworks are obsolete for contemporary practice.

Books on robotics that abstract over mathematics should be approached with caution. robotics has mathematical content that serious practice requires.

Most online robotics tutorials are uneven in quality. The exceptions — the well-maintained Lynch-Park course materials, the ROS tutorials, the talks from robotics conferences — are valuable.

Books that present autonomous vehicles as solved problem should be approached with caution given documented difficulties.

Books on AI/robotics that anthropomorphize robots without grounding should be approached with caution.

Pre-foundation-model books on robotic learning are dated for contemporary practice.

##### Contemporary sources

The major venues for contemporary robotics research include conferences. ICRA (International Conference on Robotics and Automation) is. IROS (International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems) is. RSS (Robotics: Science and Systems) is. CoRL (Conference on Robot Learning) covers learning-based robotics. The proceedings of these conferences constitute contemporary research literature.

Various IEEE robotics journals (IEEE Transactions on Robotics, IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, the others), the Science Robotics journal, the International Journal of Robotics Research provide peer-reviewed venues.

The arXiv repository covers robotics research. The cs.RO category covers robotics specifically.

Several blogs and online resources provide ongoing engagement. The Boston Dynamics, Toyota Research Institute, Google DeepMind, OpenAI robotics, various corporate robotics blogs provide contemporary content. The robotics-focused YouTube channels (Two Minute Papers when covering robotics, and others) provide video content.

Various practitioner-focused podcasts provide audio content.

The robotics newsletters (Import AI when covering robotics, the robotics-focused newsletters) provide curated coverage.

The conference talk recordings (ICRA, IROS, RSS, CoRL talks freely available) provide video content.

The open source robotics projects (PX4 for drones, ROS, and others) provide source-level reference.
